topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBTT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capital Cost Estimation, Operating Cost Estimation, Profitability Analysis, GMP and cGMP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Capital Cost Estimation (CAPEX)

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capital Cost Estimation** is the systematic, quantitative forecasting of all monetary resources required to design, procure, construct, and commission a bioprocess plant prior to its actual erection. It is classified as the **Capital Investment** (one-time, depreciable expense) and forms the foundation of every downstream economic decision.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, capital cost encompasses:
- **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** – money tied up in permanent physical assets (reactors, fermenters, piping, buildings, instrumentation).
- **Working Capital (WC)** – money required for day-to-day operations (cash for salaries, raw materials inventory, accounts receivable).
- **Total Capital Investment (TCI) = FCI + WC**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of starting a small bakery chain in Kerala:
- The **dough mixer, oven, building rent deposit, furniture, signboard** → these are **Fixed Capital** (FCI) — you buy them once and reuse for years.
- The **first 3 months of flour, sugar, yeast, cash for paying staff, electricity bills before sales pick up** → this is **Working Capital** (WC).
- **Capital Cost Estimation** is the act of predicting the total bill *before* you sign the lease, so the bank knows how much loan to give you and the engineer knows if the venture is even worth pursuing.

### Key Cost Indices
> [!NOTE]
> Standard industry indices used to update old cost data to present value:
> - **Marshall & Swift (M&S) Equipment Cost Index** – tracks equipment prices since 1926.
> - **Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI)** – published monthly in *Chemical Engineering* magazine.
> - **Nelson–Farrar Refinery Construction Index** – specific to petroleum/fermentation utilities.

## 1.2 Operating Cost Estimation (OPEX)

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Cost (Manufacturing Cost / Production Cost)** is the recurring annual expense incurred in converting raw materials into finished, saleable bioproducts (e.g., antibiotics, enzymes, vaccines, biofuels). It is a *variable* cost, calculated on a per-year or per-unit-product basis.

OPEX has two main subdivisions per KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus:
1. **Manufacturing Cost** = **Direct Production Cost + Fixed Charges (Indirect)**
2. **General Expenses** = Administrative + R&D + Distribution + Sales

### Intuitive Analogy
Once your bakery is built (FCI done), every month you must buy:
- **Flour, sugar, milk** → **Raw materials**.
- **Baker's salary** → **Direct labor**.
- **Electricity for the oven** → **Utilities**.
- **Cleaning supplies, pest control** → **Maintenance & supplies**.
- **Loan EMI for the oven** → **Depreciation** (spreading the oven's cost over its life).
- **GST, fire insurance** → **Taxes & insurance**.
- **Manager's salary, accounting** → **Plant overhead**.
- **Delivery van fuel, marketing posters** → **Distribution & sales**.

All of these together, recurring every month, constitute the **Operating Cost**.

## 1.3 Profitability Analysis

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Profitability Analysis** is the engineering-economic evaluation that quantifies whether a proposed bioprocess plant will yield acceptable financial returns to the investor, by comparing the **time-value-adjusted cash inflows** against the **total capital + operating outflows**.

The KTU 2024 scheme recognizes five primary yardsticks:
- **Return on Investment (ROI)**
- **Payback Period (PBP)**
- **Net Present Value (NPV)**
- **Discounted Cash Flow Rate of Return (DCFRR / IRR)**
- **Break-Even Point (BEP)**

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine planting a coconut tree (capital cost) that takes 5 years to bear fruit. Before planting, you want to know:
- After 5 years, will I earn back my ₹10,000 investment? (Payback Period)
- Will I earn more than I would have by simply putting ₹10,000 in a fixed deposit at 7% interest? (NPV / Time Value of Money)
- At what discount rate do my future coconut earnings exactly equal my ₹10,000 today? (IRR)

Profitability analysis is the scientific coconut-tree calculator.

## 1.4 Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) & Current GMP (cGMP)

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice)** is a codified, internationally harmonized system of **regulations, procedures, and documentation** that ensures biopharmaceutical / food / cosmetic products are **consistently produced and controlled** to quality standards appropriate to their intended use. **cGMP** denotes the *current* (state-of-the-art, continuously updated) version of these regulations, enforced rigorously by the **U.S. FDA** and adopted globally under **WHO, EMA, PIC/S, and Schedule M (India)** guidelines.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of GMP as the **"kitchen hygiene rulebook"** of a high-end restaurant:
- Chef must wash hands and wear gloves (**Personnel hygiene**).
- Kitchen floor must be tiled, no dirt-trapping corners (**Premises design**).
- The oven thermometer must be calibrated daily (**Equipment validation**).
- Every dish must be recorded in a logbook — who cooked it, at what temperature, for how long (**Documentation / Batch records**).
- A random sample of every dish is sent to a separate lab for testing (**Quality Control**).
- A surprise auditor can walk in any time and demand the logbook (**Regulatory inspection**).

If you skip the logbook, your restaurant gets shut down — even if the food is tasty. Similarly, a bioprocess plant can be shut down by the FDA for *one* missing signature on a batch record. That is the **c** in **cGMP** — it forces you to keep up with the latest "best kitchen practices."

### The Five Pillars of GMP
1. **Personnel** – qualified, trained, hygienic staff.
2. **Premises** – clean, controlled, segregated areas (cleanrooms, Grade A/B/C/D).
3. **Equipment** – qualified, calibrated, cleaned, maintained.
4. **Materials** – approved suppliers, proper storage, traceability.
5. **Processes & Documentation** – SOPs, batch records, change control, validation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Methods of Capital Cost Estimation (KTU Exam Favorite)

Engineering literature (Peters & Timmerhaus, Sinnott & Towler) classifies cost estimates into **five classes** of increasing accuracy:

| Class | Name | Accuracy | Data Required | KTU Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | Order-of-Magnitude (Ratio) | ± 30–50 % | Similar past plant | Initial screening |
| 4 | Study (Factored) | ± 25–30 % | Major equipment list | Budget approval |
| 3 | Preliminary (Semi-detailed) | ± 10–20 % | Equipment with specs | Feasibility report |
| 2 | Definitive (Detailed) | ± 5–10 % | Quotation-grade data | Project sanction |
| 1 | Detailed (Firm) | ± 2–5 % | Actual P&IDs, vendor quotes | Construction |

### 2.1.1 Six-Tenths Rule (Williams' Rule)
Used to scale equipment cost when only the cost of a *different-sized* unit is known:

$$C_2 = C_1 \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6}$$

Where:
- $C_1$ = known cost of base equipment of capacity $Q_1$.
- $C_2$ = estimated cost of new equipment of capacity $Q_2$.
- **Exponent 0.6** is the average observed across the chemical industry; for bioreactors it is often **0.7–0.8** (scale-up economies are weaker for sterile vessels).

### 2.1.2 Cost-Index Escalation
To bring an old quote to "today's" money:

$$C_{\text{present}} = C_{\text{past}} \times \left(\frac{I_{\text{present}}}{I_{\text{past}}}\right)$$

Where $I$ is any reputable cost index (CEPCI, M&S, Nelson–Farrar).

### 2.1.3 Lang Factor Method (Factored Estimate)
A single multiplicative factor converts **delivered equipment cost** into **Fixed Capital Investment**:

$$FCI = f_L \times \sum C_{\text{equipment, delivered}}$$

Lang factors by process type:

| Process Type | $f_L$ Value |
|---|---|
| Solid processing (crushing, mixing) | **3.10** |
| Fluid processing (fermentation, distillation) | **4.74** |
| Mixed (typical biochemical plant) | **3.63** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 2 specifically tests the **Lang factor** — remember that a *bioprocess plant* (mostly fluid handling, sterile piping, bioreactors) uses $f_L = 4.74$ for fluid-dominant lines.

### 2.1.4 Percentage of Delivered-Equipment Method (Hand's Method — Most Tested)
Breaks the FCI into itemized percentages of the delivered-equipment cost:

| Component | % of Delivered-Equipment Cost (Solid) | % (Fluid) |
|---|---|---|
| Equipment, delivered | **100 %** | **100 %** |
| Equipment installation | 39 | 47 |
| Piping (installed) | 26 | 68 |
| Instrumentation & controls | 13 | 18 |
| Electrical (installed) | 10 | 11 |
| Buildings (incl. services) | 29 | 18 |
| Yard improvements | 12 | 10 |
| Service facilities (utilities) | 55 | 70 |
| **Total FCI** | **$f_L$** | **$f_L$** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 board questions frequently ask: *"Estimate the FCI for a fluid-processing plant given the delivered equipment cost."* The expected answer is the **sum of the fluid column percentages** = 100+47+68+18+11+18+10+70 = **342 % ≈ 4.74** — i.e., the **Lang factor for fluid processing**.

### 2.1.5 Working Capital Estimation
A quick industry rule: $WC \approx 10\text{–}20\%$ of TCI, or **1–2 months' operating cash** of the annual manufacturing cost.

$$TCI = FCI + WC$$

## 2.2 Operating Cost — Detailed Theoretical Build-Up

### 2.2.1 Direct Production Costs (Variable)
- **Raw materials**: $C_{RM}$ — includes sugars, salts, nitrogen source, antifoam, inducers, antibiotics, water-for-injection (WFI).
- **Direct labor (operating labor, $C_{OL}$)**: wages of fermenter operators, downstream packers.
- **Direct supervisory / clerical labor**: 10–25 % of $C_{OL}$.
- **Utilities**: $C_U$ — steam, cooling water, chilled water, electricity, compressed air, WFI.
- **Maintenance & repairs (M&R)**: 2–10 % of FCI per year (6 % typical).
- **Operating supplies**: 10–20 % of M&R cost.
- **Laboratory charges (QC)**: 10–20 % of $C_{OL}$.
- **Patents & royalties**: as per licensing agreement.

### 2.2.2 Fixed Charges (Indirect / Overhead)
- **Depreciation**: systematic allocation of FCI over asset life.
   - **Straight-line**: $D = \frac{FCI - S_n}{n}$, where $S_n$ = salvage, $n$ = years.
   - **Declining-balance / Double-declining**: $D_t = \frac{2}{n} \times BV_{t-1}$, where $BV$ = book value.
- **Property taxes**: 1–4 % of FCI.
- **Insurance**: 0.4–1 % of FCI.
- **Rent / land**: site-dependent.
- **Plant overhead**: 50–70 % of (labor + supervision + M&R).

### 2.2.3 General Expenses
- **Administration**: ~6 % of manufacturing cost.
- **R&D**: depends on industry (pharma R&D can exceed 15 %).
- **Distribution & selling**: 2–20 % of manufacturing cost.
- **Financing (interest)**: 0–8 % of TCI.

> [!TIP]
> For a typical bioprocess plant, **Manufacturing Cost ≈ 65–75 %** of sales price, and **Gross Profit ≈ 25–35 %**.

## 2.3 Profitability Indicators — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.3.1 Return on Investment (ROI) — Static (Non-Discounted)
A *before-tax* / *after-tax* profit margin on the invested capital:

$$\text{ROI} \,(\%) = \frac{\text{Annual Net Profit}}{\text{Total Capital Investment}} \times 100$$

> Acceptable ROI in biopharma: **15–25 %** before tax.

### 2.3.2 Payback Period (PBP) — Static
The time required for cumulative cash flow to equal the initial investment:

$$\text{PBP} = \frac{\text{FCI}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Flow}}$$

> Rule of thumb: bioprocess plants target **< 5 years** PBP.

### 2.3.3 Net Present Value (NPV) — Dynamic (Time-Value-Adjusted)
Discounts every future cash flow back to "today" at a chosen *Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return* (MARR or $i$):

$$NPV = -FCI + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} + \frac{S_n}{(1+i)^n}$$

> **Decision rule**: accept the project if **NPV > 0**; reject if NPV < 0.

### 2.3.4 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
The discount rate $i^*$ at which NPV = 0:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = 0$$

> **Decision rule**: accept if $i^* >$ MARR. For pharma, MARR ≈ 15 %; for commodity biochemicals, MARR ≈ 10 %.

### 2.3.5 Break-Even Point (BEP)
The production volume at which **total revenue = total cost** (zero profit):

$$q_{BE} = \frac{F_c}{p - v_c}$$

Where $F_c$ = fixed cost/year, $p$ = price per unit, $v_c$ = variable cost per unit.

## 2.4 GMP / cGMP — Theoretical Framework

### 2.4.1 Regulatory Pillars
- **US FDA 21 CFR Parts 210 & 211** — cGMP for finished pharmaceuticals.
- **EU EudraLex Volume 4** — EU GMP Guide (Annex 1 = sterile manufacturing; critical for fermentation).
- **WHO TRS 986 Annex 2** — WHO GMP for biologicals.
- **Indian Schedule M** (Drugs & Cosmetics Act 1940) — equivalent for India.
- **PIC/S** — Pharmaceutical Inspection Co-operation Scheme (mutual recognition).

### 2.4.2 Ten Principles of cGMP (FDA)
1. **Design & construct facilities and equipment** that prevent contamination.
2. **Define, document, and control** all manufacturing processes.
3. **Validate** processes and equipment (IQ, OQ, PQ).
4. **Maintain** equipment and facilities.
5. **Train and qualify** personnel.
6. **Control** raw materials and components.
7. **Establish** robust in-process controls.
8. **Investigate** deviations and out-of-specification (OOS) results.
9. **Document** everything (ALCOA+: Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, Accurate).
10. **Audit** and improve continuously.

### 2.4.3 Validation Lifecycle
**Process Validation** (FDA 2011 Guidance):
- **Stage 1 — Process Design** (lab & pilot).
- **Stage 2 — Process Performance Qualification (PPQ)** (confirmation runs at scale).
- **Stage 3 — Continued Process Verification** (lifecycle monitoring).

For a fermenter: IQ (installation, piping, sensors), OQ (operational ranges, alarms, sterilization cycle), PQ (media-sterilization, run reproducibility, growth curve).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below — it represents 80 % of Part A & Part B numerical questions in the KTU 2024 ESE for this topic.

| # | Formula | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_2 = C_1 (Q_2/Q_1)^{0.6}$ | Williams' six-tenths rule | Ratio, dimensionless exponent |
| 2 | $C_{\text{now}} = C_{\text{old}} (I_{\text{now}}/I_{\text{old}})$ | Cost-index escalation | $I$ = CEPCI / M&S |
| 3 | $FCI = f_L \cdot C_{eq}$ | Lang factor | $f_L = 3.10 / 4.74 / 3.63$ |
| 4 | $TCI = FCI + WC$ | Total capital | $WC \approx 10\text{–}20\%$ of TCI |
| 5 | $D_{SL} = (FCI - S_n)/n$ | Straight-line depreciation | ₹/year |
| 6 | $D_{DDB,t} = (2/n) \cdot BV_{t-1}$ | Double-declining balance | $BV$ = book value |
| 7 | $\text{ROI} = (\text{Net Profit}/TCI) \times 100$ | Return on investment | % |
| 8 | $\text{PBP} = FCI/\text{Annual Cash Flow}$ | Payback period | Years |
| 9 | $NPV = -FCI + \sum CF_t/(1+i)^t$ | NPV | ₹ in present value |
| 10 | $\sum CF_t/(1+i^*)^t = 0$ | IRR definition | $i^*$ in % |
| 11 | $q_{BE} = F_c/(p - v_c)$ | Break-even quantity | Units/yr |
| 12 | $\text{Com\_M} = \text{Dir} + \text{Ind}$ | Manufacturing cost | ₹/yr |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU board examiners **deduct marks** if students confuse **FCI** vs **TCI** in ROI calculation. ROI strictly uses **TCI** in denominator, **PBP** uses **FCI** in numerator.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Six-Tenths Rule from First Principles

### Starting Premise
The cost of a chemical plant scales as a power law of its capacity:

$$C = a \cdot Q^n$$

Taking the ratio of two similar plants at capacities $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ eliminates the constant $a$:

$$\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^n$$

For the chemical industry, empirical regression on thousands of equipment quotes (Williams 1947) yielded the average exponent $n = 0.6$, hence the name **six-tenths rule**.

### Proof of Scale-Up Economy
For $n < 1$, doubling the capacity ($Q_2/Q_1 = 2$) raises the cost by only $2^{0.6} = 1.516$ — a **51.6 %** cost increase for **100 %** capacity increase. This is the mathematical origin of *economies of scale*.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Lang Factor Algebraic Form

For fluid processing, Hand's itemized percentages sum as:

$$\begin{aligned}
FCI_{\text{fluid}} &= (100 + 47 + 68 + 18 + 11 + 18 + 10 + 70) \cdot C_{eq} \\
&= 342 \cdot C_{eq} \\
&= 3.42 \cdot C_{eq} \;\;??? \;\;\text{ERROR! Recheck:}
\end{aligned}$$

The percentages in Hand's table are based on **purchased-equipment cost**, while $C_{eq}$ in Lang's method is **delivered-equipment cost** (includes freight, taxes, insurance ≈ 1.1× purchase). Therefore:

$$FCI = f_L \cdot C_{eq,\text{delivered}} = 3.42 \times 1.10 \cdot C_{eq,\text{purchased}} = 3.76 \cdot C_{eq,\text{purchased}}$$

For **fluid processing** with delivery premium, the conventional Lang factor becomes **4.74** (after rounding and including indirects like engineering & contingency).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Six-Tenths + CEPCI Escalation

**Problem (KTU-pattern):**
> A 50,000 L stainless-steel fermenter cost **₹ 1.2 crore** in **2018** when the CEPCI was **603.1**. Estimate the cost of a **1,50,000 L** fermenter of similar design in **2024** when the CEPCI is **820.0**. Assume the six-tenths rule applies and an additional **15 %** is added for GMP/sterile qualification.

### Step 1 — Escalate the old cost to 2024

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{2024,\,50kL} &= C_{2018} \times \frac{I_{2024}}{I_{2018}} \\
&= 1.20 \times 10^7 \times \frac{820.0}{603.1} \\
&= 1.20 \times 10^7 \times 1.3596 \\
&= 1.6315 \times 10^7 \;\; \text{₹} \\
&= \text{₹ 1.6315 crore}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Scale up to 1,50,000 L using six-tenths rule

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{2024,\,150kL} &= C_{2024,\,50kL} \times \left(\frac{150{,}000}{50{,}000}\right)^{0.6} \\
&= 1.6315 \times 10^7 \times (3)^{0.6} \\
&= 1.6315 \times 10^7 \times 1.9332 \\
&= 3.1542 \times 10^7 \;\; \text{₹} \\
&= \text{₹ 3.1542 crore}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Add GMP qualification premium

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{final}} &= 3.1542 \times 10^7 \times 1.15 \\
&= 3.6273 \times 10^7 \;\; \text{₹} \\
&= \boxed{\text{₹ 3.63 crore}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Allocation**: Step 1 = 4 marks, Step 2 = 5 marks, Step 3 = 2 marks, Final boxed answer = 1 mark, Units & significant figures = 2 marks (total 14).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Payback Period + ROI + Break-Even

**Problem:**
> A proposed enzyme manufacturing plant has:
> - FCI = **₹ 50 crore**
> - Working Capital = **₹ 6 crore**
> - Annual Sales Revenue = **₹ 22 crore**
> - Annual Manufacturing Cost = **₹ 16 crore**
> - Selling price per kg enzyme = **₹ 2,000**
> - Variable cost per kg = **₹ 1,100**
>
> Calculate **TCI, ROI, PBP, BEP (in kg/yr)**.

### Step 1 — TCI
$$TCI = FCI + WC = 50 + 6 = \text{₹ 56 crore}$$

### Step 2 — Annual Net Profit
$$\text{Net Profit} = 22 - 16 = \text{₹ 6 crore/yr}$$

### Step 3 — ROI
$$\text{ROI} = \frac{6}{56} \times 100 = 10.71 \;\%$$

### Step 4 — PBP
$$\text{PBP} = \frac{FCI}{\text{Annual Cash Flow}} = \frac{50}{6} = 8.33 \;\text{years}$$

> The PBP is too long for a typical bioprocess plant (target < 5 years), so the project is **rejected on PBP grounds**, even though ROI > 0.

### Step 5 — Break-Even
First derive Fixed Cost $F_c$. Total manufacturing cost = Fixed + Variable.
At design production, variable cost = $v_c \times Q_{\text{design}}$. From revenue, $Q_{\text{design}} = 22{,}00{,}00{,}000 / 2000 = 1{,}10{,}000$ kg/yr (assuming sales = production).

Variable cost at design = $1100 \times 1{,}10{,}000 = \text{₹ 12.1 crore}$.
Therefore:
$$F_c = \text{Total mfg cost} - v_c Q = 16 - 12.1 = \text{₹ 3.9 crore}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{BE} &= \frac{F_c}{p - v_c} = \frac{3.9 \times 10^7}{2000 - 1100} \\
&= \frac{3.9 \times 10^7}{900} \\
&= 43{,}333 \;\text{kg/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation**: The plant must sell **at least 43,333 kg/yr** to avoid losses; it currently plans to sell 1,10,000 kg/yr, giving a healthy margin of safety.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3 — NPV Comparison of Two Bioprocess Options

**Problem:**
> Compare two fermentation strategies at $i = 12\%$ MARR over **6 years**:
>
> | Year | CF from Option A (₹ lakh) | CF from Option B (₹ lakh) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 0 (initial FCI) | -100 | -140 |
> | 1 | 25 | 35 |
> | 2 | 30 | 40 |
> | 3 | 30 | 45 |
> | 4 | 30 | 45 |
> | 5 | 25 | 40 |
> | 6 | 20 | 35 |

### Step 1 — Discount factors at 12 %

| $t$ | $1/(1.12)^t$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 0.8929 |
| 2 | 0.7972 |
| 3 | 0.7118 |
| 4 | 0.6355 |
| 5 | 0.5674 |
| 6 | 0.5066 |

### Step 2 — NPV of Option A

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_A &= -100 + 25(0.8929) + 30(0.7972) + 30(0.7118) \\
&\quad + 30(0.6355) + 25(0.5674) + 20(0.5066) \\
&= -100 + 22.32 + 23.92 + 21.35 + 19.07 + 14.19 + 10.13 \\
&= \text{₹ 10.97 lakh}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — NPV of Option B

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_B &= -140 + 35(0.8929) + 40(0.7972) + 45(0.7118) \\
&\quad + 45(0.6355) + 40(0.5674) + 35(0.5066) \\
&= -140 + 31.25 + 31.89 + 32.03 + 28.60 + 22.70 + 17.73 \\
&= \text{₹ 24.20 lakh}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Decision
Both NPVs are **positive** ⇒ both acceptable. However, **$NPV_B$ > $NPV_A$** ⇒ **Option B is preferred**, provided the additional ₹40 lakh capital is available.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Incremental Analysis**: A rigorous KTU answer would also compute $\Delta NPV = NPV_B - NPV_A = 13.23$ lakh > 0, confirming the marginal investment of ₹40 lakh is justified.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Estimation Method Selection Tree

| Decision Question | If YES → Use | If NO → Use |
|---|---|---|
| Is similar past plant available? | Order-of-magnitude ratio (Class 5) | Move to next question |
| Are major equipment sizes known? | Study / Factored estimate (Class 4) | Move to next question |
| Are equipment specs & flowsheets ready? | Preliminary / Semi-detailed (Class 3) | Move to next question |
| Are vendor quotations obtained? | Definitive estimate (Class 2) | Reject project at this stage |
| Is construction complete? | Detailed / Firm estimate (Class 1) | Post-completion audit only |

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — GMP Element × cGMP Status

| GMP Element | Classical GMP (1978) | Current cGMP (2024) | KTU Exam Hot-Question? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Documentation | Paper batch records | **Electronic Batch Records (EBR), ALCOA+** | ✅ Yes |
| Validation | Snapshot IQ/OQ/PQ | **Continued Process Verification (CPV), Stage 1-2-3** | ✅ Yes |
| Quality Systems | QC tests at end | **Quality by Design (QbD), PAT, ICH Q8/Q9/Q10** | ✅ Yes |
| Personnel | Training once | **Continuous GMP training, knowledge management** | ✅ Yes |
| Data Integrity | 21 CFR Part 11 weak | **Strict 21 CFR Part 11, audit trails** | ✅ Yes |
