## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ethanol Fermentation — Detailed Operational Theory

#### 2.1.1 Microbial Catalyst

**Primary organism:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* — a Crabtree-positive, facultatively anaerobic yeast.

- **Advantages:** GRAS status, high ethanol tolerance (~120 g/L), high specific productivity (Qₚ ≈ 2–4 g/L·h), tolerates low pH (3.5–4.5 → low contamination risk).
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot ferment pentoses (xylose, arabinose) — a major bottleneck for lignocellulosic ethanol.

**Secondary organism:** *Zymomonas mobilis* — a Gram-negative, anaerobic, ethanologenic bacterium.

- **Advantages:** Higher specific glucose uptake rate (Entner–Doudoroff pathway), higher ethanol yield (>95 % theoretical), grows on sucrose and glucose.
- **Disadvantages:** Narrow substrate range (only glucose, fructose, sucrose), lower ethanol tolerance (~80–100 g/L).

**For lignocellulose (xylose-rich) hydrolysates:** genetically engineered *S. cerevisiae* expressing bacterial xylose isomerase, or *Pichia stipitis*.

#### 2.1.2 Biochemistry — The EMP-to-Ethanol Pipeline

Step-by-step, in plain words:

1. **Glucose uptake** via facilitated diffusion / hexose transporters.
2. **Glycolysis (EMP pathway):** Glucose → 2 Pyruvate + 2 ATP + 2 NADH.
3. **Pyruvate decarboxylase (PDC, EC 4.1.1.1)** decarboxylates pyruvate → acetaldehyde + CO₂.
4. **Alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH, EC 1.1.1.1)** reduces acetaldehyde → ethanol, regenerating NAD⁺.

The 2 ATP from glycolysis drive the cell's maintenance and biosynthesis; the 2 NADH are re-oxidised by ADH. **No net ATP is gained** — the cell survives by glycolysis alone under anaerobic conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this yield capped at ~0.51 g/g?**
> Half the carbon of glucose is lost as CO₂ (2 of 6 C atoms). This is the **theoretical Gay-Lussac maximum** — no microbial process can exceed it. In practice, the **industrial practical yield** is **0.46–0.48 g ethanol / g glucose** (≈90–95 % of theoretical) because some carbon is diverted to biomass (~5 %) and minor by-products (glycerol, higher alcohols, organic acids).

#### 2.1.3 Process Modes

| Mode | Description | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch** | All substrate added at t=0; product removed at end | Small/traditional distilleries, fuel ethanol in some plants |
| **Fed-batch** | Substrate fed gradually to keep [glucose] < 100 g/L (avoid Crabtree repression) | Standard Brazilian/Indian cane molasses plants |
| **Continuous (CSTR)** | Steady-state inflow = outflow | High-volume fuel ethanol (e.g., COFCO, Novozymes cell-recycle systems) |
| **Continuous with cell recycle** | Membrane/centrifuge returns cells to reactor | High productivity (Qₚ > 30 g/L·h) |
| **SSF** | Simultaneous Saccharification & Fermentation | Lignocellulosic ethanol — cellulase + yeast in one vessel |

#### 2.1.4 Process Conditions (Canonical Industrial Setpoints)

| Variable | Typical Range | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 28–35 °C (S. cerevisiae), 30 °C (Z. mobilis) | Optimal growth; above 37 °C, ethanol evaporation and stress |
| **pH** | 3.5–4.5 (self-regulating by CO₂) | Inhibits contaminating bacteria |
| **Substrate concentration (initial)** | 150–250 g/L sugars (fed-batch) | Hyperosmotic stress above 250 g/L |
| **Dissolved oxygen** | < 0.5 % saturation (microaerobic to anaerobic) | Pasteur effect — diverts carbon to ethanol |
| **Fermentation time** | 24–72 h (batch) | Depends on initial sugar & target titer |
| **Final ethanol titer** | 80–120 g/L (8–12 % v/v) | Distillation economic threshold ~ 4 % v/v in beer |

#### 2.1.5 Recovery — Distillation and Dehydration

A typical **fuel-grade ethanol** purification train:

1. **Beer column** — separates ethanol + water (overhead) from solids/yeast (bottom).
2. **Rectification column** — concentrates to ~95 % v/v (azeotrope with water at 95.6 % v/v, 78.2 °C).
3. **Molecular sieve (zeolite 3Å)** — adsorbs the residual water → **anhydrous 99.5–99.9 % v/v** "absolute" ethanol.
4. **Optional: Azeotropic distillation** with benzene or cyclohexane (older plants).

For **beverage/bioethanol**: the beer column overhead (~50 % v/v ethanol) may pass through a **rectifier → extractive column → polisher**, depending on grade required.

#### 2.1.6 Material Balance — Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 200,000 L fermenter is charged with cane molasses at 220 g/L total reducing sugars. Final ethanol titer = 90 g/L. Fermentation time = 36 h. Calculate **(i)** sugar consumed, **(ii)** % theoretical yield, **(iii)** overall productivity, **(iv)** CO₂ produced.

**Solution Logic:** Use the Gay-Lussac stoichiometry, MW conversions, and the productivity definition.

### 2.2 ABE Fermentation — Detailed Operational Theory

#### 2.2.1 Microbial Catalyst

**Primary organism:** *Clostridium acetobutylicum* ATCC 824

- Gram-positive, spore-forming, strictly anaerobic rod.
- Optimum growth: 35–37 °C, pH 6.0–6.5 (acidogenic); pH 4.5–5.0 (solventogenic).
- Produces the **typical Weizmann ratio A : B : E = 3 : 6 : 1** (by mass).
- Solvent-tolerant strains can reach **30–35 g/L** butanol.

**Secondary organism:** *Clostridium beijerinckii* NCIMB 8052 — broader substrate range (inulin, starch), gives slightly different solvent ratios.

#### 2.2.2 Biochemistry — The Clostridial Pathway

A simplified four-step narrative:

1. **EMP glycolysis:** Glucose → 2 Pyruvate + 2 NADH + 2 ATP.
2. **Pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA** (pyruvate:ferredoxin oxidoreductase, PFOR), generating CO₂ + H₂.
3. **Acidogenesis (early exponential, pH > 5):** 
   - 2 Acetyl-CoA → Acetoacetyl-CoA → **Acetoacetate** → **Acetone** (via acetoacetate decarboxylase, ADC) — *irreversible carbon loss*.
   - 2 Acetyl-CoA → **Butyryl-CoA** → **Butyrate** (with CoA transferase cycling).
4. **Solventogenesis (stationary, pH < 5):**
   - Acetone remains (the CoA is shunted away to acetone).
   - **Butyrate + ethanol (or acetate) → Butanol + acetate (or ethanol)** (CoA transferase, reverse).
   - **Acetaldehyde → Ethanol** (alcohol dehydrogenase, ADH).
   - Net: acids are re-assimilated, butanol and ethanol accumulate.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "pH switch" is the master regulator.**
> When undissociated butyric/acetic acids accumulate and diffuse back into the cell, they collapse the membrane proton gradient (ΔpH). The cell responds by **inducing solventogenic genes** (*sol* operon, *adc*, *ctfA/B*, *adhE*) — a classical **acid-stress survival response** that, in industrial terms, conveniently produces our desired solvents.

#### 2.2.3 Process Modes

- **Batch (traditional):** 40–60 h, two clear phases.
- **Fed-batch:** Sugar feed to maintain 60–80 g/L, suppresses acid shift; extends solvent phase.
- **Continuous (CSTR / chemostat):** Dilution rate 0.1–0.3 h⁻¹.
- **Cell-immobilised / biofilm reactors:** Continuous solvent removal, alleviates butanol inhibition.
- **In situ product recovery (ISPR):** Gas stripping, pervaporation, liquid–liquid extraction (oleyl alcohol) — coupled to fermenter to keep butanol < 8 g/L in broth.

#### 2.2.4 Process Conditions (Canonical ABE Setpoints)

| Variable | Acidogenic Phase | Solventogenic Phase |
|---|---|---|
| **pH** | 6.0–6.5 | 4.5–5.0 (set point ~5.0) |
| **Temperature** | 35–37 °C | 35–37 °C |
| **Redox potential** | < –200 mV (anaerobic) | < –200 mV |
| **Sugar** | 60–80 g/L initial | Continuous or fed |
| **O₂** | Strictly anaerobic (N₂/CO₂ sparging) | Same |
| **Final titer (ABE)** | — | 15–22 g/L (wild-type); 30+ g/L (mutant) |
| **Final solvent ratio** | — | A : B : E = 3 : 6 : 1 |

#### 2.2.5 Recovery — The Butanol Energy Penalty

**The fundamental engineering problem:** n-Butanol boils at **117.7 °C**, has a **water azeotrope at 55.5 % w/w butanol (b.p. 92.4 °C)**, and is present at only **~13–20 g/L** in the beer. Conventional distillation is therefore **prohibitive in steam cost** (~30–50 MJ/kg butanol vs. ~6–8 MJ/kg for ethanol).

**Modern ISPR options:**

1. **Gas stripping** — N₂ or fermentation-CO₂ bubbled through beer; butanol vapor recovered by condensation. Low cost, gentle, no toxicity to cells.
2. **Pervaporation** — Selective membrane (e.g., PDMS, silicone rubber) on fermenter overhead. Butanol preferentially permeates; condensed on permeate side.
3. **Liquid–liquid extraction** — Oleyl alcohol, decanol, or ionic liquids as immiscible extractant. Continuous removal of butanol.
4. **Vacuum fermentation** — Reduced pressure (50–100 mbar) lowers boiling point, removes butanol *in situ* as vapor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **"Butanol Paradox"** — a *fermentation* that is energetically cheap to run but energetically *expensive* to recover. This single line often fetches full marks in a 14-mark KTU question on ABE.

#### 2.2.6 Solvent Toxicity — Why ABE is Hard

Butanol disrupts the cell membrane by:
- Inserting into the lipid bilayer (hydrophobic tail).
- Increasing membrane fluidity.
- Dissolving membrane proteins.
- Collapsing ΔpH and ΔΨ.

**Order of toxicity:** Butanol > Acetone > Ethanol (correlates inversely with log Pₒw). Cells stop growing when [butanol] > ~13 g/L; viable production ceiling ~20 g/L in wild-type.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gay-Lussac (ethanol) | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$ | — | Theoretical max 0.511 g/g |
| 2 | Theoretical ethanol yield | $Y_{EtOH,\,th}^{GLC} = \dfrac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16} = 0.5114$ | g EtOH / g glucose | $Y_{sucrose} = 0.5384$ |
| 3 | Practical ethanol yield | $Y_{p} = \dfrac{P}{S_0 - S_f}$ | g/g | Industrial: 0.45–0.48 |
| 4 | Volumetric productivity | $Q_P = \dfrac{P_f - P_0}{t}$ | g/L·h | $P_f, P_0$ titers; $t$ = fermentation time |
| 5 | Sugar (°) Brix ↔ g/L | $^\circ \text{Brix} \approx 10\ \text{g/L}$ (approx.) | — | Sucrose equivalent |
| 6 | % v/v → g/L ethanol | $\rho_{EtOH} = 0.789\ \text{kg/L}$ at 20 °C | g/L = (%v/v) × 7.89 | E.g., 12 % v/v = 94.7 g/L |
| 7 | ABE theoretical yield | $Y_{th}^{ABE} = \dfrac{58 + 74 + 46}{180.16} = 0.986$ g solvents / g glucose (per balanced equation) | g/g | Practical: 0.30–0.40 |
| 8 | ABE practical solvent yield | $Y_p = \dfrac{3C_A + 6C_B + 1C_E}{S_0 - S_f}$ (in mass ratio) | g/g | 3:6:1 is the **Weizmann** mass ratio |
| 9 | Mass-based A/B/E ratio | $C_A : C_B : C_E = 3 : 6 : 1$ | — | Solventogenic wild-type |
| 10 | Dilution rate (CSTR) | $D = \dfrac{F}{V}$ | h⁻¹ | Steady state: $D = \mu$ |
| 11 | Carbon balance | $S_0 = S_f + X/Y_{X/S} + P/Y_{P/S} + C_{byprod}/Y_{..}$ | g/L | Mandatory in 14-mark problems |
| 12 | % Theoretical yield | $\%\,Y = \dfrac{Y_p}{Y_{th}} \times 100$ | % | Always reported in KTU |
| 13 | Ethanol boiling point | 78.37 °C; azeotrope 95.6 % v/v at 78.2 °C | — | Defines distillation limit |
| 14 | Butanol boiling point | 117.7 °C; azeotrope 55.5 % w/w with water at 92.4 °C | — | Defines "butanol paradox" |
| 15 | Distillation energy | $E_{dist} \approx 2.6\ \text{MJ/L}$ EtOH | MJ/L | Conventional |
| 16 | Anhydrous EtOH — sieve | 3 Å zeolite adsorption | — | Replaces azeotropic distillation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In 14-mark questions, you must show **all three** of (i) carbon/elemental balance, (ii) % theoretical yield, and (iii) volumetric productivity. Marks are reserved for each.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ethanol:** Biofuel blending (E10, E85 in India & Brazil), potable alcohol, pharmaceutical excipient, industrial solvent, intermediate for ethyl esters and ethylene.
- **n-Butanol:** Direct gasoline-blend (higher energy density than ethanol: 29 MJ/L vs. 21 MJ/L), solvent for paints/coatings, plasticiser precursor, aviation biofuel pathway (ATJ — alcohol-to-jet).
- **Acetone:** Solvent for nail polish remover, resins, pharmaceuticals, intermediate for MMA (methyl methacrylate) and bisphenol A.

> **Cross-discipline relevance:** ABE is the prototype **"microbial chemical factory"** that the modern **biorefinery** concept aims to replicate for many other products (1,3-propanediol, isoprene, succinate). KTU links ABE to biorefineries in Module 5.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (Gay-Lussac)

Starting from the balanced equation:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Step 1 — Tabulate molecular masses:**

$$\text{MW}_{glucose} = 6(12.01) + 12(1.008) + 6(16.00) = 180.16\ \text{g/mol}$$
$$\text{MW}_{EtOH} = 2(12.01) + 6(1.008) + 16.00 = 46.07\ \text{g/mol}$$
$$\text{MW}_{CO_2} = 12.01 + 2(16.00) = 44.01\ \text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Apply stoichiometric coefficients:**

The balanced equation shows **1 mol glucose → 2 mol ethanol**. Therefore mass-wise:

$$180.16\ \text{g glucose} \longrightarrow 2 \times 46.07\ \text{g EtOH} = 92.14\ \text{g EtOH}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gravimetric yield constant:**

$$Y_{th} = \frac{92.14}{180.16} = 0.5114\ \frac{\text{g EtOH}}{\text{g glucose}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to % theoretical basis (KTU convention):**

$$\%\,Y_{th} = 0.5114 \times 100 = 51.14\ \%\ \text{by mass of glucose}$$

**Step 5 — For sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁, MW = 342.30 g/mol), invert to 2 glucose + 2 fructose, then ferment both hexoses:**

$$Y_{th}^{sucrose} = \frac{4 \times 46.07}{342.30} = 0.5384\ \frac{\text{g EtOH}}{\text{g sucrose}}$$

> **Conclusion:** Theoretical yield of ethanol is **0.511 g/g glucose** and **0.538 g/g sucrose**. No microbial process can exceed these — these are the **upper bounds** used in KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Industrial Ethanol Material Balance (Worked Problem)

> **Problem:** A 200,000 L fermenter is charged with cane molasses (220 g/L total reducing sugars as invert). Final ethanol titer = 90 g/L. Fermentation time = 36 h. Assume: biomass yield 0.10 g/g sugar, glycerol by-product 0.04 g/g sugar. Working volume = 190,000 L. Compute:
> (a) Total sugar consumed (kg)
> (b) % Theoretical yield (sugar basis)
> (c) Volumetric productivity (g/L·h)
> (d) CO₂ produced (kg)
> (e) Verification of carbon balance.

**Step 1 — Total sugar charged:**

$$S_0 \cdot V = 220\ \text{g/L} \times 190{,}000\ \text{L} = 41{,}800{,}000\ \text{g} = 41{,}800\ \text{kg sugar}$$

**Step 2 — Total ethanol produced (working volume basis):**

$$P_f \cdot V = 90\ \text{g/L} \times 190{,}000\ \text{L} = 17{,}100{,}000\ \text{g} = 17{,}100\ \text{kg EtOH}$$

**Step 3 — Carbon balance to find residual sugar (S_f):**

Let $S_f$ be residual sugar (g/L). Carbon in input must equal carbon in outputs. Carbon content of each species:

$$C_{glc} = \frac{6 \times 12.01}{180.16} = 0.4000\ \text{(g C / g glucose)}$$
$$C_{EtOH} = \frac{2 \times 12.01}{46.07} = 0.5214$$
$$C_{biomass} \approx 0.45\ \text{(yeast CH}_{1.8}\text{O}_{0.5}\text{N}_{0.2}\text{)}$$
$$C_{glycerol} = \frac{3 \times 12.01}{92.09} = 0.3912$$
$$C_{CO_2} = 0.2729$$

Sugar consumed: $\Delta S = S_0 - S_f$ (in g/L). 

**Carbon balance per litre:**

$$0.40 \cdot (S_0 - S_f) = 0.5214 \cdot P_f + 0.45 \cdot X + 0.3912 \cdot G + 0.2729 \cdot C_{CO_2}$$

Where:
- $X = 0.10 \cdot (S_0 - S_f)$
- $G = 0.04 \cdot (S_0 - S_f)$
- $C_{CO_2}$ (g CO₂/L) = $\frac{44.01}{92.14} \times P_f = 0.4776 \times P_f$ (from stoichiometry).

Substitute:

$$0.40 \cdot \Delta S = 0.5214 \cdot 90 + 0.45 \cdot (0.10 \Delta S) + 0.3912 \cdot (0.04 \Delta S) + 0.2729 \cdot (0.4776 \cdot 90)$$

$$0.40 \cdot \Delta S = 46.93 + 0.045 \cdot \Delta S + 0.01565 \cdot \Delta S + 11.73$$

$$0.40 \cdot \Delta S - 0.06065 \cdot \Delta S = 58.66$$

$$0.33935 \cdot \Delta S = 58.66$$

$$\Delta S = 172.86\ \text{g/L sugar consumed}$$

**(a) Total sugar consumed:**

$$41.8 \times \frac{172.86}{220} = 32.85\ \text{tonnes (approx.)}\ \text{or}\ 190{,}000 \times 172.86 \times 10^{-3} = 32{,}843\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — % Theoretical yield:**

$$Y_p = \frac{P_f}{\Delta S} = \frac{90}{172.86} = 0.5206\ \frac{\text{g EtOH}}{\text{g sugar consumed}}$$

$$\%\,Y_{th} = \frac{0.5206}{0.5384} \times 100 = 96.7\ \%$$

> **Note:** > 95 % is "excellent" by industrial standards.

**(c) Volumetric productivity:**

$$Q_P = \frac{P_f - P_0}{t} = \frac{90 - 0}{36} = 2.5\ \text{g/L·h}$$

**(d) CO₂ produced:**

$$\text{CO}_2 = 0.4776 \times 90 \times 190{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 8{,}167\ \text{kg CO}_2$$

**(e) Carbon-balance verification (must close to ≤ 5 %):**

- **C in** (sugar consumed): $0.40 \times 32{,}843 = 13{,}137\ \text{kg C}$
- **C out:**
  - EtOH: $0.5214 \times 17{,}100 = 8{,}916\ \text{kg C}$
  - Biomass: $0.45 \times (0.10 \times 32{,}843) = 1{,}478\ \text{kg C}$
  - Glycerol: $0.3912 \times (0.04 \times 32{,}843) = 514\ \text{kg C}$
  - CO₂: $0.2729 \times 8{,}167 = 2{,}229\ \text{kg C}$
  - **Total out = 13,137 kg C** ✓

> **Answer summary:** (a) **32,843 kg** sugar consumed; (b) **96.7 %** theoretical yield; (c) **2.5 g/L·h** productivity; (d) **8,167 kg** CO₂.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — ABE Stoichiometry and Theoretical Yield

> **Problem:** A 5,000 L batch ABE fermenter is loaded with 70 g/L glucose. The final solvent concentrations are: acetone 5.0 g/L, butanol 12.0 g/L, ethanol 2.0 g/L. Residual glucose = 4 g/L. Fermentation time = 60 h. Compute:
> (a) Total ABE titer and **A : B : E** mass ratio
> (b) Total solvent produced (kg)
> (c) % theoretical yield
> (d) Volumetric productivity
> (e) Verify the solvent ratio matches the Weizmann ratio.

**Step 1 — Total ABE titer:**

$$C_{ABE} = 5.0 + 12.0 + 2.0 = 19.0\ \text{g/L}$$

**Step 2 — Mass ratio:**

$$A : B : E = 5.0 : 12.0 : 2.0 = 2.5 : 6.0 : 1.0 \approx 3 : 6 : 1 \text{(after scaling to 6:1 Butanol:Ethanol)}$$

Wait — the Weizmann canonical is 3:6:1. Our actual ratio = 2.5:6:1. **Close but not exact** — acceptable in industry, as strains and conditions shift it.

**Step 3 — Solvent produced (kg):**

$$P_{ABE} = 19.0 \times 5{,}000 = 95{,}000\ \text{g} = 95\ \text{kg total solvents}$$

**(a)** A:B:E = 2.5 : 6.0 : 1.0 (slightly low acetone; close to Weizmann).
**(b)** 95 kg.
**(c)** Sugar consumed: $\Delta S = 70 - 4 = 66\ \text{g/L}$.

$$Y_p = \frac{19.0}{66} = 0.288\ \frac{\text{g ABE}}{\text{g glucose}}$$

Theoretical yield from balanced equation (per 180.16 g glucose → 1 acetone + 1 butanol + 1 ethanol):

$$Y_{th}^{ABE} = \frac{58.08 + 74.12 + 46.07}{180.16} = \frac{178.27}{180.16} = 0.989\ \text{g/g}$$

But industrially, this is rarely reached because the actual stoichiometry is not 1:1:1 but 0.21 acetone : 0.55 butanol : 0.085 ethanol (in moles), with significant CO₂ and H₂O co-products. A more realistic literature "theoretical" is **~0.39 g total ABE / g glucose** based on 2 mol glucose → 1 mol butanol + 1 mol acetone + 1 mol ethanol + 2 CO₂.

$$\%\,Y_{th} = \frac{0.288}{0.39} \times 100 = 73.8\ \%$$

**(d) Volumetric productivity:**

$$Q_P = \frac{19.0 - 0}{60} = 0.317\ \text{g/L·h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compare to ethanol:** ABE $Q_P \approx 0.3\ \text{g/L·h}$ vs ethanol $Q_P \approx 2.5\ \text{g/L·h}$ — ABE is **~8× slower**, one of the main engineering challenges.

**(e) Ratio verdict:** A : B : E = **2.5 : 6.0 : 1.0** is **near the Weizmann 3 : 6 : 1** — *C. acetobutylicum* signature. Higher A fraction (3.0 instead of 2.5) would suggest hyper-solventogenic conditions (low pH, phosphate limitation).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Penalty for Butanol Distillation vs Ethanol

**Energy to concentrate a binary aqueous solution** (approximate, from Underwood equation for dilute alcohol):

$$E_{dist} \approx k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{x_f}{x_0}\right) \cdot \dfrac{MW \cdot C_p}{\Delta H_{vap}}$$

A simplified but KTU-acceptable rule of thumb:

- **Ethanol:** ~**2.6 MJ/L** to go from 10 % v/v beer to 99.5 % v/v absolute (azeotrope-breaking via molecular sieve adds ~0.5 MJ/L).
- **Butanol:** ~**30–50 MJ/kg** for steam-stripping or distillation due to high BP and low feed titer.

**Quantitative ratio:**

$$\frac{E_{BuOH}}{E_{EtOH}} = \frac{40\ \text{MJ/kg}}{21\ \text{MJ/kg} \times 0.789\ \text{kg/L}} \approx \frac{40}{16.6} \approx 2.4\times \text{per unit energy content}$$

> **Interpretation:** Butanol delivers more energy per kg (29 MJ/kg) than ethanol (21 MJ/kg), but the **fermentation-and-recovery energy** tilts the balance. This is the **"energy-return-on-investment"** argument that drove ABE from petrochemistry in the 1950s and is now driving its **renewed industrial interest via ISPR**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Industrial Comparison

| Dimension | Ethanol (Yeast) | ABE (Clostridia) |
|---|---|---|
| **Discovery / scale-up** | Prehistoric; industrial 1900s | Weizmann, 1916 |
| **Organism** | *S. cerevisiae* | *C. acetobutylicum* |
| **Substrate** | C6 sugars (some engineered for C5) | C6, starches, inulin |
| **Theoretical yield** | 0.511 g/g glucose | 0.39 g total ABE / g glucose |
| **Practical yield** | 0.45–0.48 g/g | 0.25–0.32 g/g |
| **Final titer (industrial)** | 80–120 g/L | 15–22 g/L |
| **Productivity** | 2–4 g/L·h | 0.3–0.5 g/L·h |
| **Phase** | Single phase | Biphasic (acidogenesis + solventogenesis) |
| **Inhibitory product** | Ethanol (~120 g/L) | Butanol (~13–20 g/L) |
| **Recovery energy** | 2.6 MJ/L (mature tech) | 30–50 MJ/kg butanol (still costly) |
| **Modern recovery** | Distillation + molecular sieve | Distillation + ISPR (gas-strip, pervap, extract.) |
| **Current global volume** | ~110 billion L/yr (bioethanol) | < 1 % of pre-1950s; resurging |
| **KTU emphasis** | Mass balance, recovery | Biphasic physiology, ISPR, biorefinery |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ethanol Process Block Diagram (Fuel-Ethanol Plant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["FEEDSTOCK\nSugarcane Molasses\nor Corn Mash"]
    nodeB["PRE-TREATMENT\nDilution to 200 g/L\npH 4.5\nHeat 90 C"]

    nodeD["BEER\n80-120 g/L EtOH\n+Yeast cells\n+Solids"]

    nodeI["DENATURATION\nAdd 2-5% MeOH\nor gasoline"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J
    E --> K["WET CAKE\nDDGS / Cattle Feed\nCo-product"]
```

```mermaid
    D --> E --> F --> G --> H
    E --> I
    I --> C
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["CLOSTRIDIUM CELL\nSTART STATE\nLog Phase Growth"]
    nodeB["ACIDOGENESIS\npH 6.0-6.5\nExponential Growth\nAcetate + Butyrate Excreted"]
    nodeC{"pH DROP\nAcid Stress\nMarker\nUndissociated Acid High"}
    nodeD["SOLVENTOGENESIS\npH 4.5-5.0\nStationary Phase\nsol Operon Induced"]
    nodeE["Acid Re-assimilation\nCoA Transferase\nReverse Direction"]

    C -- Yes --> D --> E --> F --> G
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the Gay-Lussac equation for ethanol fermentation. What is the theoretical yield of ethanol from glucose?**

**Model Answer:**
The Gay-Lussac equation for ethanol fermentation is:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + \text{energy (ATP)}$$

The theoretical maximum yield is calculated as follows:

$$Y_{th} = \frac{2 \times \text{MW}_{EtOH}}{\text{MW}_{glucose}} = \frac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16} = 0.511\ \frac{\text{g EtOH}}{\text{g glucose}}$$

This represents 51.14 % by mass conversion. In practice, industrial strains of *S. cerevisiae* achieve 90–95 % of this theoretical value (i.e., 0.45–0.48 g/g), with the residual carbon diverted to biomass (~5 %) and minor by-products like glycerol and higher alcohols.

---

**A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Weizmann ratio in ABE fermentation? Name the micro-organism and the two distinct phases of the fermentation.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Weizmann ratio** refers to the characteristic mass ratio of the three solvents produced in the Acetone-Butanol-Ethanol (ABE) fermentation:

$$\text{Acetone : n-Butanol : Ethanol} = 3 : 6 : 1\ \text{(w/w)}$$

The micro-organism principally responsible is ***Clostridium acetobutylicum*** (ATCC 824), a Gram-positive, strictly anaerobic, spore-forming rod discovered by Chaim Weizmann in 1916.

The two distinct phases of the fermentation are:

1. **Acidogenesis (acid-producing phase):** Occurs during the exponential growth phase at pH 6.0–6.5. Glucose is metabolised to acetic and butyric acids, which accumulate in the broth, causing a drop in pH.

2. **Solventogenesis (solvent-producing phase):** Triggered by low pH (4.5–5.0) and entry into the stationary phase. The cell re-assimilates the acids and produces the three neutral solvents (acetone, butanol, ethanol) in the Weizmann 3 : 6 : 1 ratio.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the industrial process for the production of ethanol by fermentation, including the organism, feedstocks, process conditions, and major recovery steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50,000 L fermenter is charged with cane molasses (200 g/L total reducing sugars). Final ethanol titer = 85 g/L after 40 h. Assume residual sugar = 8 g/L, biomass yield = 0.08 g/g, glycerol by-product = 0.03 g/g. Calculate:
  (i) % theoretical ethanol yield
  (ii) Volumetric productivity (g/L·h)
  (iii) Total CO₂ released (kg)
  (iv) Comment on the carbon balance closure. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Organism:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (yeast) — preferred for its high ethanol tolerance (~120 g/L), GRAS status, and ability to ferment sucrose, glucose, and fructose. For lignocellulosic hydrolysates, *Zymomonas mobilis* or engineered yeast with xylose isomerase is used.

**Feedstocks:** 
- 1st generation: Sugarcane juice, sugar beet juice, cane molasses (cheapest, India/Brazil).
- 1st-gen grain: Corn, sorghum, wheat (USA, China).
- 2nd generation: Lignocellulosic biomass (bagasse, straw, wood) — requires pretreatment + enzymatic hydrolysis.

**Process Conditions:**
- Temperature: 28–35 °C
- pH: 3.5–4.5 (self-acidifying, inhibits contaminants)
- Anaerobic / microaerobic
- Fermentation mode: Fed-batch (sugar kept < 100 g/L to avoid Crabtree effect)
- Time: 24–72 h

**Major Recovery Steps:**
1. **Beer column** — separates ethanol + water from yeast/solids.
2. **Rectification column** — concentrates to **95.6 % v/v** azeotrope with water.
3. **Molecular sieve** (3 Å zeolite) — adsorbs residual water → **anhydrous 99.8 % v/v** ethanol.
4. **Denaturation** (for fuel) — addition of 2–5 % methanol or gasoline.
5. Co-product: **DDGS** (dried distillers' grains with solubles) as cattle feed from grain-based plants.

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:** $V = 50{,}000$ L; $S_0 = 200$ g/L; $P_f = 85$ g/L; $t = 40$ h; $S_f = 8$ g/L; $Y_{X/S} = 0.08$; $Y_{Gly/S} = 0.03$.

**Step 1 — Sugar consumed:**

$$\Delta S = S_0 - S_f = 200 - 8 = 192\ \text{g/L}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric productivity (Q_P):**

$$Q_P = \frac{P_f - P_0}{t} = \frac{85}{40} = 2.125\ \text{g/L·h}$$

**Step 3 — Practical yield (sugar basis):**

$$Y_p = \frac{P_f}{\Delta S} = \frac{85}{192} = 0.4427\ \frac{\text{g EtOH}}{\text{g sugar consumed}}$$

**Step 4 — % theoretical yield:**

$$\%\,Y_{th} = \frac{Y_p}{Y_{th}^{sucrose}} \times 100 = \frac{0.4427}{0.5384} \times 100 = 82.23\ \%$$

> **Note:** A practical yield of 82 % of theoretical is *acceptable* for molasses (which contains non-fermentable sugars, inerts, and minerals that reduce the effective yield). For pure glucose, > 90 % is expected.

**Step 5 — CO₂ produced (from stoichiometry):**

By Gay-Lussac: 1 mol glucose → 2 mol CO₂. Therefore:

$$\text{CO}_2 = \frac{2 \times 44.01}{2 \times 46.07} \times P_f \times V \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 0.955 \times 85 \times 50{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 4{,}058\ \text{kg CO}_2$$

(More precisely, $0.4776 \times 85 \times 50{,}000 / 1000$ — same as $\frac{MW_{CO_2}}{MW_{EtOH}} \times P_f \times V / 1000 = \frac{44.01}{46.07} \times 0.5 \times 85 \times 50 = 4{,}058$ kg.)

**Step 6 — Carbon balance closure:**

- **C in** (sugar consumed): $0.40 \times 192 \times 50{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 3{,}840\ \text{kg C}$
- **C out:**
  - EtOH: $0.5214 \times 85 \times 50{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 2{,}216\ \text{kg C}$
  - Biomass: $0.45 \times 0.08 \times 192 \times 50{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 346\ \text{kg C}$
  - Glycerol: $0.3912 \times 0.03 \times 192 \times 50{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 113\ \text{kg C}$
  - CO₂: $0.2729 \times 4{,}058 = 1{,}108\ \text{kg C}$
  - **Total out = 3,783 kg C**

**Closure error = (3,840 − 3,783) / 3,840 × 100 = 1.5 %** ✓ — well within the 5 % industrial tolerance.

**Final Answers to A(b):** (i) **82.23 %**; (ii) **2.125 g/L·h**; (iii) **4,058 kg CO₂**; (iv) Closure is **excellent at 1.5 % error**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the ABE fermentation process in detail, covering the micro-organism, biphasic metabolism (acidogenesis and solventogenesis), and the role of the Weizmann ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major recovery challenges for butanol and the modern *in situ* product recovery (ISPR) techniques used to overcome the "butanol paradox." Compare the energy penalty of butanol distillation with that of ethanol distillation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Micro-organism:** *Clostridium acetobutylicum* (and *C. beijerinckii*) — Gram-positive, spore-forming, strictly anaerobic, motile rods. Discovered by Chaim Weizmann in 1916 for industrial acetone production in WWI (cordite manufacture).

**Biphasic Metabolism — The Two-Act Play:**

**Phase 1 — Acidogenesis (t = 0 to ~12 h, exponential growth, pH 6.0–6.5):**
- Glucose → 2 Pyruvate (EMP pathway, 2 ATP, 2 NADH).
- Pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA (PFOR), releasing CO₂ and H₂.
- Acetyl-CoA splits into two branches:
  - 2 Acetyl-CoA → Acetoacetyl-CoA → Acetoacetate → **Acetone** (via acetoacetate decarboxylase, ADC).
  - 2 Acetyl-CoA → Acetoacetyl-CoA → Butyryl-CoA → **Butyrate** (with CoA transfer, ATP generation).
- The cell **excretes** acetate and butyrate, dropping broth pH to ~4.5.

**Phase 2 — Solventogenesis (t = 12 to 50 h, stationary phase, pH 4.5–5.0):**
- Triggered by low pH, undissociated acid stress, and entry into stationary phase.
- The *sol* operon is induced: *ctfA*, *ctfB* (CoA transferase), *adc* (acetoacetate decarboxylase), *adhE* (alcohol dehydrogenase).
- Acids are **re-assimilated**: butyrate + acetyl-CoA → butyryl-CoA + acetate (CoA transferase, reverse).
- Butyryl-CoA → Butyraldehyde → **n-Butanol** (via ADH).
- Acetaldehyde → **Ethanol** (ADH).
- Acetone formation continues because ADC is irreversible.

**The Weizmann Ratio (3 : 6 : 1):**
At the end of a typical batch, the **mass** of the three solvents follows the 3:6:1 ratio. This is **diagnostic** of wild-type *C. acetobutylicum* and reflects:
- The 2 ATP yield of the acetate branch vs. the ATP-equivalent of butyrate.
- The irreversible loss of carbon as acetone.
- The thermodynamic drive to produce butanol for redox balance.

Genetic engineering can shift this ratio (e.g., knockout of ADC → "ABE-2" with 8:0:1 ratio favouring butanol).

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**The "Butanol Paradox" — Recovery Challenge:**

n-Butanol has a **boiling point of 117.7 °C**, a **water azeotrope at 55.5 % w/w (BP 92.4 °C)**, and is present in the broth at only **13–20 g/L** (much lower than ethanol's 80–120 g/L). Consequently, conventional distillation is **energy-prohibitive (30–50 MJ/kg butanol)**. The fermentation itself is cheap; the **recovery dominates the operating cost** — hence the paradox.

**ISPR Techniques (4 main options):**

1. **Gas Stripping:** N₂ or fermentation CO₂ sparged through the beer; butanol (and acetone/ethanol) vaporise and are recovered by condensation. Energy: ~7–10 MJ/kg butanol. Gentle, no toxicity. **KTU favourite.**

2. **Pervaporation:** Selective hydrophobic membrane (e.g., PDMS, silicone rubber) on the fermenter overhead. Butanol preferentially permeates; condensed as a concentrate. Energy: ~12–20 MJ/kg. Can be coupled to fermentation in situ.

3. **Liquid–Liquid Extraction:** Oleyl alcohol, decanol, or 2-ethyl-1-hexanol as an immiscible extractant. Continuous removal into the organic phase, which is then back-extracted or distilled. Risk: extractant toxicity to *Clostridia* (must be screened).

4. **Vacuum / Flash Fermentation:** Reduced pressure (50–100 mbar) lowers butanol's effective boiling point. Butanol flashes off and is condensed. Combined with high cell-density, gives the highest productivities (>1 g/L·h).

**Energy Comparison:**

| Process | Energy to Anhydrous Product |
|---|---|
| Ethanol distillation + sieve (10 % v/v → 99.8 % v/v) | **~3 MJ/L** ≈ 14 MJ/kg |
| Butanol distillation (20 g/L → 99.5 % w/w) | **~30–50 MJ/kg** |
| Butanol gas stripping (continuous) | **~7–10 MJ/kg** |
| Butanol pervaporation | **~12–20 MJ/kg** |

> **Conclusion:** Modern ISPR can reduce butanol recovery energy by **3–5×** compared to distillation, making the fermentation economically viable — and re-empowering the "microbial chemical factory" of the 21st century biorefinery.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up ABE ratio units.** A : B : E = 3 : 6 : 1 is **mass (w/w)**, not moles. Converting to moles gives ~5.2 : 8.1 : 1 — a favourite trick question.
> 2. **Using 0.511 g/g as the theoretical yield for ABE** — this is the **ethanol** maximum. ABE theoretical is **~0.39 g total solvent / g glucose**.
> 3. **Forgetting the azeotrope** in ethanol recovery — distillation alone stops at 95.6 % v/v; further dehydration requires molecular sieve or azeotropic distillation.
> 4. **Not showing units** in material balances. KTU deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit in numerical answers.
> 5. **Confusing the ABE phase trigger.** pH-drop (acid stress) induces solventogenesis — not entry into stationary phase alone. The two are coupled but pH is the master regulator.
> 6. **Omitting carbon balance verification** in 14-mark numerical questions. Always close to within 5 %.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Alcohols (Ethanol, ABE):**

- ☐ **Gay-Lussac equation** for ethanol — 1 mol glucose → 2 mol EtOH + 2 mol CO₂; **theoretical yield 0.511 g/g**.
- ☐ **S. cerevisiae** preferred for ethanol; **Z. mobilis** for high-specificity bacterial route; engineered strains for C5 sugars.
- ☐ **Ethanol recovery** = beer column → rectifier → molecular sieve → denaturant. Azeotrope at **95.6 % v/v, 78.2 °C**.
- ☐ **ABE fermentation** is **biphasic**: **acidogenesis (pH 6.0–6.5)** → **solventogenesis (pH 4.5–5.0)**.
- ☐ **Weizmann ratio** A : B : E = **3 : 6 : 1** by **mass**; *C. acetobutylicum* is the principal strain.
- ☐ **Butanol toxicity** caps the fermentation at **13–20 g/L** — the "butanol paradox."
- ☐ **Theoretical ABE yield** ≈ **0.39 g total solvent / g glucose** (practical 0.25–0.32).
- ☐ **ABE recovery** uses ISPR — **gas stripping, pervaporation, L–L extraction, vacuum** — not conventional distillation.
- ☐ **Distillation energy:** Ethanol ~3 MJ/L; Butanol ~30–50 MJ/kg.
- ☐ **Crabtree effect:** Excess glucose → aerobic yeast produces ethanol even with O₂ (relevant for feedstock design).
- ☐ **Acid-stress triggers solventogenesis** via the *sol* operon (*adc*, *ctfA/B*, *adhE*).
- ☐ **CoA transferase (CtfA/B)** is the key reversible enzyme that re-assimilates acids during solventogenesis.
- ☐ **Industrial E. coli** strains (e.g., KO11, LY01) and **engineered yeast** are modern workhorses for lignocellulosic ethanol.
- ☐ **ABE global history:** Discovered 1916 (Weizmann) → industrial scale-up 1920s–1940s → displaced by petrochemistry in 1950s → resurgent since 2005 as part of the **biorefinery / green-chemistry** movement.
- ☐ **ABE bottleneck** = solvent tolerance + recovery cost. Modern R&D focus: **(i)** hyper-butanol-tolerant strains, **(ii)** engineered gas-stripping membranes, **(iii)** consolidated bioprocessing.
- ☐ **Key co-products of ethanol industry:** DDGS (cattle feed), CO₂ (carbonated beverages, dry ice, algal biomass), fusel oils.
- ☐ **Carbon balance closure** must be within **5 %** in KTU material balance problems.
- ☐ **% theoretical yield** is always reported in KTU exam answers — never just the absolute yield.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Applications of Primary Metabolites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBTT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Industrial Applications of Primary Metabolites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **primary metabolite** is a low molecular weight compound that is produced as a direct consequence of the normal "energy metabolism" or "growth metabolism" of an organism during its **trophophase** (the active growth phase). According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, primary metabolites are the essential biochemicals whose synthesis is coupled to microbial growth via the central carbon metabolic pathways — **glycolysis, the TCA (Krebs) cycle, the pentose phosphate pathway, and the EMP-TCA-PPP nexus.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):**
> Primary metabolites include **amino acids, organic acids, nucleotides, vitamins, ethanol, polysaccharides, lipids/biosurfactants, and industrial enzymes** that are produced in tight association with biomass formation. They are essential for the cell's own growth, development, and reproduction.

### 1.2 Primary vs. Secondary Metabolites – The Critical Distinction

| Parameter | Primary Metabolites | Secondary Metabolites |
|---|---|---|
| Phase of production | **Trophophase** (growth phase) | **Idiophase** (stationary phase) |
| Coupling to growth | **Growth-associated** | **Non-growth associated** |
| Function in producer cell | Essential (structural/functional) | Ecological (defense, signaling) |
| Molecular weight | Generally low | Often higher |
| Yield kinetics | Direct function of μ (specific growth rate) | Independent of μ |
| Examples | Glutamate, Citric acid, Ethanol, Lysine | Penicillin, Streptomycin, Aflatoxin |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World):**
> Think of primary metabolites as the **"daily bread and groceries"** of a microorganism. Just as a working human being *must* buy rice, vegetables, and milk (essentials tied to daily survival), a growing microbial cell *must* produce amino acids to build proteins, nucleotides to replicate DNA, and organic acids to harvest energy. These are not luxuries — they are produced *because* the cell is alive and growing. When the cell shifts to producing "luxury items" like antibiotics (secondary metabolites), it's like a retiree gardening for pleasure — disconnected from survival needs.

### 1.3 Why Industrial Bioprocess Technology Focuses on Primary Metabolites

Primary metabolites represent the **highest-volume, highest-revenue segment** of industrial biotechnology. Global production scales for some of these compounds are staggering:

- **L-Glutamic acid (MSG):** ~3.7 million tonnes/year
- **L-Lysine:** ~2.8 million tonnes/year
- **Citric acid:** ~2.5 million tonnes/year
- **Lactic acid:** ~1.5 million tonnes/year
- **Ethanol (bioethanol):** ~100+ billion litres/year
- **Industrial enzymes:** Multi-billion USD market

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Key Metric for Board Examinations:**
> Primary metabolites are characterized by the **"Grams-per-Litre" productivity benchmark (g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹)**, where commercial viability typically demands **product titres > 50 g/L, yields > 0.3 g/g, and productivities > 1.0 g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categories of Industrial Primary Metabolites (Exam-Relevant Classification)

The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus groups primary metabolites into the following industrial categories:

**A. Amino Acids**
- *L-Glutamic acid* (MSG) — *Corynebacterium glutamicum*
- *L-Lysine* — *C. glutamicum* (auxotrophic/leaky mutants)
- *L-Threonine* — *E. coli* (engineered)
- *L-Tryptophan, L-Phenylalanine, L-Methionine, L-Aspartic acid*

**B. Organic Acids**
- *Citric acid* — *Aspergillus niger* (mold), *Candida tropicalis* (yeast)
- *Lactic acid* — *Lactobacillus* spp.
- *Gluconic acid* — *Aspergillus niger*, *Gluconobacter oxydans*
- *Itaconic acid* — *Aspergillus terreus*

**C. Nucleotides & Nucleosides**
- *IMP (Inosine monophosphate), GMP (Guanylic acid), XMP* — flavor enhancers
- *ATP, NAD⁺* — coenzymes

**D. Vitamins**
- *Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin)* — *Pseudomonas denitrificans, Propionibacterium shermanii*
- *Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin)* — *Ashbya gossypii, Bacillus subtilis*
- *Vitamin C precursor (2-keto-L-gulonic acid)* — *Gluconobacter oxydans*

**E. Solvents & Biofuels**
- *Ethanol* — *Saccharomyces cerevisiae, Zymomonas mobilis*
- *Butanol* — *Clostridium acetobutylicum*
- *1,3-Propanediol* — *Klebsiella, engineered E. coli*

**F. Polysaccharides**
- *Xanthan gum* — *Xanthomonas campestris*
- *Dextran* — *Leuconostoc mesenteroides*
- *Hyaluronic acid* — *Streptococcus, recombinant Bacillus*

**G. Lipids & Biosurfactants**
- *Single-cell oils (SCOs)* — *Cryptococcus, Mortierella*
- *Rhamnolipids, Surfactin* — *Pseudomonas, Bacillus*

**H. Industrial Enzymes**
- *α-Amylase, Glucoamylase, Protease, Lipase, Pectinase, Cellulase, Laccase*

### 2.2 Why Are These Produced Industrially — The "Why" Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale (Key Point for 7-mark questions):**
> 1. **Growth-associated production kinetics** → higher biomass = higher product → simple, scalable.
> 2. **Natural overproducers exist** — wild strains can already overproduce via *regulatory mutation*.
> 3. **Central metabolic pathways** are conserved — engineering tools (CRISPR, auxotrophy) are mature.
> 4. **Tight coupling to carbon flux** — flux balance analysis (FBA) is highly predictable.
> 5. **High titer, yield, productivity (TYP) targets** are commercially achievable in 3–7 days.

### 2.3 Industrial Bioprocess Logic — The "How" Steps

The standard industrial workflow for a primary metabolite follows this logic chain:

1. **Strain selection / improvement** (wild-type screening, mutagenesis, rDNA, CRISPR)
2. **Inoculum development** (working → pilot → production seed)
3. **Medium design** (carbon, nitrogen, minerals, vitamins, precursors)
4. **Sterilization** (batch sterilization: 121 °C for 20 min)
5. **Fermentation** (batch, fed-batch, or continuous mode)
6. **Downstream processing** (cell separation, concentration, purification, crystallization)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific growth rate | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Rate of biomass increase per unit biomass | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| Doubling time | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | Time for biomass to double | h |
| Product titre | $P = \text{max product concentration in broth}$ | Final concentration | $\text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}$ |
| Product yield (biomass) | $Y_{P/X} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta X}$ | Product per biomass | $\text{g}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}$ |
| Product yield (substrate) | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | Product per substrate consumed | $\text{g}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}$ |
| Volumetric productivity | $Q_P = \dfrac{P_f - P_i}{t}$ | Productivity per unit broth volume | $\text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| Growth-associated (Luedeking-Piret) | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \mu X + \beta X$ | Product formation rate (primary) | $\text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| Substrate consumption | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}}$ | Substrate uptake rate | $\text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| Monod kinetics | $\mu = \mu_{max}\dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Substrate-limited growth | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| Mass balance on bioreactor | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} V = r_X V - F X_{out}$ | General CSTR biomass balance | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Do not use `|` inside table cells.** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute values, e.g., $\vert S - K_s \vert$, not |S - Ks| which breaks the markdown table parser.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MSG production in 750 m³ stirred-tank reactors** with *C. glutamicum* — controlled biotin limitation to trigger glutamate secretion.
- **Citric acid by *A. niger*** in 200 m³ air-lift reactors — Mn²⁺ limitation, low pH 2.0, high dissolved oxygen.
- **Lysine fed-batch** — automated glucose feeding to prevent catabolite repression (Crabtree-like glucose effect).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Primary Metabolite Production Kinetics (Luedeking-Piret Equation)

The defining kinetic signature of a primary metabolite is that its production rate depends on **both the specific growth rate μ and the existing biomass X**. The Luedeking-Piret (1959) equation captures this:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \mu X + \beta X
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = growth-associated product formation coefficient (g product per g biomass formed)
- $\beta$ = non-growth-associated product formation coefficient (g product per g biomass per h)
- $X$ = biomass concentration (g·L⁻¹)
- $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹)

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1 — Empirical observation.**
In a primary-metabolite fermentation (e.g., lactic acid by *Lactobacillus*), it is observed experimentally that the product concentration $P$ rises linearly with biomass $X$ during exponential growth. Therefore, the rate $dP/dt$ must be proportional to biomass $X$:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} \propto X
$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the proportionality constants.**
Since $P$ formation is growth-coupled, we add a growth-associated constant $\alpha$ (which scales with $\mu$), and a non-growth-associated constant $\beta$ (which scales with biomass alone):

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \mu X + \beta X
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule for total production.**
Over a fermentation interval $0 \to t$, the total product is the time-integral:

$$
P(t) = P_0 + \int_0^t \left( \alpha \mu X + \beta X \right) dt
$$

**Step 4 — Special case: pure growth-associated product (typical of glutamate, lysine).**
If $\beta = 0$:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \mu X
$$

For exponential growth ($X = X_0 e^{\mu t}$):

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \mu X_0 e^{\mu t}
$$

Integrating:

$$
P(t) - P_0 = \alpha \left( X(t) - X_0 \right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\, Y_{P/X} = \alpha = \dfrac{P(t) - P_0}{X(t) - X_0} \,}
$$

This confirms that the **growth yield coefficient $Y_{P/X}$ is a constant** for a primary metabolite under balanced growth.

**Step 5 — Numerical example (typical KTU problem).**

*In a lysine fed-batch fermentation:*
- Initial biomass $X_0 = 2$ g·L⁻¹
- Final biomass $X_f = 35$ g·L⁻¹
- Initial lysine $P_0 = 0$ g·L⁻¹
- Final lysine $P_f = 170$ g·L⁻¹
- $\mu = 0.45$ h⁻¹, $\beta = 0.02$ g·g⁻¹·h⁻¹

Compute $Y_{P/X}$:

$$
Y_{P/X} = \dfrac{170 - 0}{35 - 2} = \dfrac{170}{33} \approx 5.15\ \text{g}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}
$$

Compute $\alpha$:

$$
\alpha = Y_{P/X} \approx 5.15\ \text{g}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}
$$

Compute volumetric productivity (if fermentation time = 60 h):

$$
Q_P = \dfrac{170 - 0}{60} \approx 2.83\ \text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}
$$

This is a **commercially viable value** for industrial lysine production.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Substrate Balance & Theoretical Yield

The **theoretical maximum product yield** from a given substrate is derived from stoichiometry.

For **ethanol from glucose** via *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*:

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2
$$

**Step 1 — Compute molecular weights.**

$$
MW_{\text{glucose}} = 180.16\ \text{g/mol},\quad MW_{\text{ethanol}} = 46.07\ \text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute theoretical mass yield.**

$$
Y_{P/S}^{theo} = \dfrac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16} = \dfrac{92.14}{180.16} \approx 0.511\ \text{g}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute theoretical carbon yield.**

$$
Y_{C}^{theo} = \dfrac{2 \times 2}{6} = 0.667\ \text{mol C per mol C}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical yield.**
Industrial ethanol fermentation achieves ~90–95% of theoretical:

$$
Y_{P/S}^{practical} = 0.511 \times 0.92 \approx 0.470\ \text{g}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}
$$

This corresponds to ~**12% v/v ethanol** in broth — the typical industrial endpoint.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass Balance on a CSTR for Steady-State Continuous Primary Metabolite Production

For a primary metabolite at **steady state** in a chemostat:

**Biomass balance:**

$$
F X_0 = F X + r_X V
$$

Since $X_0 = 0$ (sterile feed) and $r_X = \mu X$:

$$
0 = D X + \mu X
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\mu = D = \dfrac{F}{V} \,}
$$

**Substrate balance:**

$$
F(S_0 - S) = r_S V = \dfrac{\mu X V}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

Substituting $\mu = D$:

$$
S = K_s \dfrac{D}{\mu_{max} - D} \quad \text{(Monod-derived)}
$$

$$
X = Y_{X/S} \left( S_0 - S \right)
$$

**Product balance:**

$$
F P_0 = F P + r_P V
$$

With $P_0 = 0$ and $r_P = (\alpha \mu + \beta) X$:

$$
P = \dfrac{(\alpha D + \beta) X}{D}
$$

This equation is the **theoretical backbone for designing continuous primary-metabolite processes.**

---

|---|---|---|---|---|---|

| L-Lysine | *C. glutamicum* | Glucose, molasses | 170–200 | 0.50 | Leaky auxotroph, fed-batch |
| Citric acid | *A. niger* | Glucose, sucrose | 200+ | 0.80 | pH 2.0, Mn²⁺ limitation, high O₂ |
| Lactic acid | *Lactobacillus* spp. | Glucose, whey | 100–200 | 0.95 | pH 5.5–6.0, CaCO₃ neutralization |
| Ethanol | *S. cerevisiae* | Glucose, sucrose | 80–120 (g·L⁻¹) | 0.46 | Anaerobic, 30 °C |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generalized Block Diagram — Industrial Primary Metetabolite Bioprocess

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Materials Carbon Nitrogen Minerals] --> B[Medium Preparation Tank]
    B --> C[Sterilization 121C 20 min]
    C --> D[Inoculum Working Seed Production]
    D --> E[Bioreactor 50 to 500 m3 Stirred Tank or Airlift]
    E --> F[Centrifugation Microfiltration Cell Removal]
    F --> G[Concentration Evaporation Ultrafiltration]
    G --> H[Purification Ion Exchange Crystallization]
    H --> I[Final Product Drying Packaging]
    E --> J[Off gas Analysis CO2 O2]
    E --> K[Online Monitoring pH DO Temperature]
    K --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Carbon/nitrogen/mineral raw materials are compounded, heat-sterilized, inoculated with a production seed culture, fermented in a large bioreactor under controlled pH/DO/temperature, and the broth is clarified, concentrated, purified, and crystallized to the final product. Online sensors feed control loops for real-time process adjustment.

### 4.2 L-Glutamic Acid Production Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Strain C glutamicum biotin auxotroph] --> S1[Stage 1 Inoculum Build Up Shake Flask]
    S1 --> S2[Stage 2 Seed Fermenter 5 m3]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Production Fermenter 500 m3]
    M1[Medium Glucose Urea Minerals Biotin 30 ug per L] --> S3
    P1[Process Control pH 7.8 Temperature 30C DO 30 percent Tween 80] --> S3
    S3 --> C1[Cell Removal Centrifuge]
    C1 --> C2[Decolorization Activated Carbon]
    C2 --> C3[Ion Exchange Chromatography]
    C3 --> C4[Crystallization and Drying]
    C4 --> O1[Final Product MSG Crystals]
    S3 -.waste.-> W1[Biomass and Broth Waste]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Biotin limitation is the *key regulatory trigger* — under biotin deficiency, the cell wall of *C. glutamicum* becomes leaky, allowing glutamate (which is normally retained intracellularly for osmotic balance) to be secreted. The surfactant Tween-80 *replaces* the biotin effect at higher biotin levels, enabling the trick to work in nutrient-rich media.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Direct Recall)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern A1]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between primary and secondary metabolites with suitable examples.**
> *Model Answer (3 marks):*
> - (1 mark) **Primary metabolites** are low-molecular-weight compounds produced during the trophophase and are essential for cell growth (e.g., L-glutamate, ethanol, citric acid).
> - (1 mark) **Secondary metabolites** are produced during the idiophase, are not essential for growth, and serve ecological roles (e.g., penicillin, streptomycin).
> - (1 mark) Primary metabolite production is **growth-associated** (Luedeking–Piret, β ≈ 0); secondary metabolite production is **non-growth associated** (μ-independent).

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern A2]**
> **Q2. List any six industrial primary metabolites and their microbial producers.**
> *Model Answer (3 marks — half-mark each):*
> 1. L-Glutamic acid — *Corynebacterium glutamicum*
> 2. L-Lysine — *C. glutamicum* (auxotroph)
> 3. Citric acid — *Aspergillus niger*
> 4. Lactic acid — *Lactobacillus delbrueckii*
> 5. Ethanol — *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*
> 6. Xanthan gum — *Xanthomonas campestris*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern A3]**
> **Q3. Define volumetric productivity. Write its formula and SI units.**
> *Model Answer (3 marks):*
> Volumetric productivity is the mass of product formed per unit broth volume per unit time. Formula: $Q_P = \dfrac{P_f - P_i}{t}$. SI units: $\text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$ (1 mark each for definition, formula, units).

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Module-2)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A]**
> **Q. (a)** Describe in detail the industrial production of **L-glutamic acid (MSG)** using *Corynebacterium glutamicum*, covering the biochemistry, regulatory triggers, fermentation conditions, and downstream processing. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Derive the **Luedeking–Piret equation** for a primary metabolite and explain the meaning of the coefficients $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in the context of a **lysine** fed-batch fermentation. A lysine fermentation has the following data: $X_0 = 2$ g·L⁻¹, $X_f = 40$ g·L⁻¹, $P_0 = 0$ g·L⁻¹, $P_f = 180$ g·L⁻¹, $t = 60$ h. Calculate (i) $Y_{P/X}$, (ii) $\alpha$ (assuming $\beta = 0$), and (iii) the volumetric productivity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Organism and biochemistry (2 marks):** *Corynebacterium glutamicum* is a Gram-positive, biotin-auxotrophic, non-sporulating coryneform bacterium. L-Glutamic acid is the central amino acid of nitrogen metabolism, formed from α-ketoglutarate via NADPH-dependent glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH) or by transamination.

2. **Regulatory trigger (2 marks):** Under **biotin limitation (< 5 μg/L)** OR addition of **Tween-80 (polyoxyethylene sorbitan monooleate)**, the cell wall phospholipid composition changes, increasing membrane permeability. Glutamate, normally retained for osmotic balance, leaks out, which in turn *removes feedback inhibition* on GDH, enabling **massive overproduction (up to 200 g/L)**.

3. **Fermentation conditions (1.5 marks):** Submerged stirred-tank or airlift bioreactor; pH 7.8 (controlled with NH₃/NaOH); 30 °C; DO > 30 % saturation; glucose or sucrose feed (fed-batch to avoid catabolite repression); 50–60 h cycle.

4. **Downstream processing (1.5 marks):** Cell removal by centrifugation → activated carbon decolorization → ion-exchange (cation exchanger) → concentration by evaporation → crystallization as sodium salt (MSG crystals) → drying → packaging.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from observation that primary-metabolite formation rate is proportional to biomass:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \mu X + \beta X
$$

For $\beta = 0$ (pure growth-associated product like lysine):

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \mu X
$$

Integrating over fermentation time $0 \to t$:

$$
P(t) - P_0 = \alpha \left( X(t) - X_0 \right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
Y_{P/X} = \alpha = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta X}
$$

**Meanings (2 marks):**
- $\alpha$ = growth-associated product formation coefficient (g product formed per g biomass synthesized).
- $\beta$ = non-growth-associated coefficient (g product per g biomass per h, contributes during stationary phase).
- For lysine, $\alpha \gg \beta$ — confirming **growth-associated** behavior.

**Numerical calculations (2 marks):**

(i) Product yield on biomass:

$$
Y_{P/X} = \dfrac{180 - 0}{40 - 2} = \dfrac{180}{38} \approx 4.74\ \text{g}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}
$$

(ii) $\alpha$ (with $\beta = 0$):

$$
\alpha = Y_{P/X} \approx 4.74\ \text{g}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}
$$

(iii) Volumetric productivity:

$$
Q_P = \dfrac{180 - 0}{60} = 3.0\ \text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** All three metrics are within industrially viable ranges for lysine fermentation, confirming the fermentation is operating efficiently.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B]**
> **Q. (a)** Explain the **industrial production of citric acid** by *Aspergillus niger*. Discuss the role of **pH, Mn²⁺ limitation, dissolved oxygen, and the morphology of the fungus** in determining yield. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** A **continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR)** is used to produce lactic acid using *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* with the following data: $D = 0.3$ h⁻¹, $S_0 = 50$ g·L⁻¹, $K_s = 0.5$ g·L⁻¹, $\mu_{max} = 0.5$ h⁻¹, $Y_{X/S} = 0.15$ g·g⁻¹, $\alpha = 3.0$ g·g⁻¹, $\beta = 0.05$ g·g⁻¹·h⁻¹. Calculate the steady-state **substrate concentration (S), biomass (X), and product (P)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Process overview (1.5 marks):** *Aspergillus niger* is a filamentous fungus that produces citric acid via the TCA cycle. The process was pioneered by **Currie (1917)** — by-product of sugar metabolism; commercialized globally for food, beverage, detergent, and pharma industries.

2. **TCA cycle biochemistry (1.5 marks):** Glucose → glycolysis → pyruvate → mitochondrial acetyl-CoA + OAA → citrate (via citrate synthase) → transported to cytosol → cleaved by ATP-citrate lyase → OAA + acetyl-CoA. Citrate accumulates because the downstream aconitase is **inhibited by low pH and low Mn²⁺**.

3. **Role of pH (1 mark):** A very low pH of **2.0** is critical. It (a) inhibits aconitase, blocking citrate consumption, and (b) prevents contamination by other microorganisms.

4. **Role of Mn²⁺ limitation (1 mark):** Manganese is a cofactor for many TCA enzymes; its limitation inactivates aconitase and phosphofructokinase, redirecting flux to citrate. Commercial media use **trace Mn²⁺ < 0.02 ppm**.

5. **Role of dissolved oxygen (1 mark):** High O₂ tension (> 150 mbar) is required; hypoxia leads to ethanol/oxalic acid by-products. Industrial bioreactors use 0.5–1.0 vvm aeration.

6. **Morphology (1 mark):** The desired morphology is **small, uniform, freely dispersed pellets** (1–2 mm). Filamentous, clumpy growth reduces O₂ transfer and lowers yield.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Steady-state substrate concentration from Monod (2 marks):**

$$
S = \dfrac{K_s \cdot D}{\mu_{max} - D} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.3}{0.5 - 0.3} = \dfrac{0.15}{0.2} = 0.75\ \text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Biomass concentration (2 marks):**

$$
X = Y_{X/S} (S_0 - S) = 0.15 \times (50 - 0.75) = 0.15 \times 49.25 = 7.39\ \text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Product concentration (3 marks):**

Using Luedeking–Piret at steady state:

$$
D P = (\alpha D + \beta) X
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{(\alpha D + \beta) X}{D} = \left( \alpha + \dfrac{\beta}{D} \right) X
$$

$$
P = \left( 3.0 + \dfrac{0.05}{0.3} \right) \times 7.39
$$

$$
P = \left( 3.0 + 0.167 \right) \times 7.39 = 3.167 \times 7.39
$$

$$
P \approx 23.4\ \text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}
$$

**Verification:** Productivity $Q_P = D P = 0.3 \times 23.4 = 7.02\ \text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$ — a respectable industrial benchmark for lactic acid.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 2 — Primary Metabolites:**
> 1. **Confusing growth and non-growth association.** If asked "is lysine a primary metabolite and why?", you must explicitly state *growth-associated* and *trophophase production*. Partial marks are lost if you only say "it is essential for the cell."
> 2. **Forgetting regulatory triggers.** Glutamate production *requires* biotin limitation OR Tween-80; lysine production *requires* auxotrophy (e.g., Leu⁻/Thr⁻). Simply naming the organism is **not enough** for full marks.
> 3. **Mixing up units in productivity formula.** $Q_P = P_f / t$ has units of $\text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$. Students commonly write only $\text{g}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}$ or only $\text{h}^{-1}$ — examiner deducts 1 mark for missing units.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation steps.** In Luedeking–Piret derivations, jumping directly to the final equation without showing the empirical basis loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Incorrect Y_{P/X} sign or direction.** Always $\Delta P / \Delta X$, not the reverse.
> 6. **CSTR mass balance:** At steady state, the dilution rate $D$ equals $\mu$ — this is the central identity, not optional. Examiners will not give full marks without it.
