> ✅ **DSP sequence:** Centrifugation → Decolorization → Ion exchange → Crystallization → Drying.
>
> ✅ **Mnemonic — "GOLP E-VX":** Glutamate, Organic acids, Lysine, Polysaccharides, Ethanol, Vitamins, Xanthan — the seven major primary-metabolite groups to remember.
>
> ✅ **Bioreactor selection rule:** Aerobic fungal → airlift; aerobic bacterial → STR; anaerobic yeast/Clostridium → anaerobic STR with CO₂ recycling.
>
> ✅ **Regulatory cues (commonly asked):** Biotin-limitation (glutamate), Auxotrophy (lysine), Mn²⁺ limitation (citrate), pH 2.0 (citrate), Anaerobic (ethanol), pH-controlled CaCO₃ (lactate).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secondary Metabolites: Antibiotics (Penicillin, Streptomycin)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBTT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Secondary Metabolites: Antibiotics (Penicillin, Streptomycin)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Primary vs. Secondary Metabolites — The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Secondary metabolites are low-molecular-weight organic compounds produced by microorganisms, especially during the stationary phase (idiophase) of growth, that are not directly essential for cell growth, reproduction, or primary metabolic functions, but often possess significant ecological, pharmaceutical, and industrial value.*

The cell's metabolism is divided into two distinct phases of operation:

| Phase | Cell Activity | Metabolite Class | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trophophase** | Active growth, rapid cell division | **Primary metabolites** (amino acids, nucleotides, organic acids) | Essential for cell growth & maintenance |
| **Idiophase** | Slowed/no growth, nutrient limitation | **Secondary metabolites** (antibiotics, pigments, toxins) | Ecological/competitive advantages, not essential for growth |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Factory Worker vs. The Bonus Invention"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Think of a microbe as a **bicycle factory worker**. 
> - The **primary metabolites** are the bicycles themselves — *essential products the worker builds every day to keep the factory running and pay the bills*. 
> - The **secondary metabolites** (like antibiotics) are *clever bonus inventions* the worker designs only when business slows down (stationary phase) — they are not needed for the factory's survival, but they make the worker famous, fend off competitors (other microbes), and have tremendous commercial value when extracted.
> 
> Just like a factory might produce a unique gadget during slow periods, microbes synthesize antibiotics only after their rapid growth phase is over, triggered by nutrient depletion (especially nitrogen or phosphate limitation), even though the antibiotic has no role in their own cellular machinery.

### 1.3 Antibiotics — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Antibiotic (Waksman, 1942):** *A chemical substance produced by a microorganism that, even in low concentrations, has the capacity to inhibit the growth of, or destroy, other microorganisms.*

### 1.4 The Two Flagship Antibiotics — Overview

| Feature | **Penicillin** | **Streptomycin** |
|---|---|---|
| **Discoverer** | **Alexander Fleming (1928)** | **Albert Schatz & Selman Waksman (1943)** |
| **Producing Organism** | *Penicillium chrysogenum* (formerly *P. notatum*) | *Streptomyces griseus* |
| **Class** | **β-lactam antibiotic** | **Aminoglycoside antibiotic** |
| **Chemical Nature** | Contains a 4-membered **β-lactam ring** fused to a thiazolidine ring | Contains an **aminocyclitol (streptidine)** linked to two sugar units |
| **Spectrum** | Mainly Gram-positive bacteria | Broad — Gram-positive, Gram-negative, *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* |
| **Mechanism of Action** | Inhibits **bacterial cell wall** (peptidoglycan) synthesis | Inhibits **bacterial protein synthesis** (30S ribosome) |
| **Medical Use** | Pneumonia, strep throat, syphilis, etc. | Tuberculosis, plague, tularemia |
| **Type of Fermentation** | Aerobic, submerged, fed-batch | Aerobic, submerged, batch |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Secondary Metabolism — The "Why" Behind the Timing

Secondary metabolite production is tightly coupled to **nutrient imbalance**, not nutrient excess. The key regulatory triggers and their logic are:

- **Carbon source excess** (e.g., glucose) combined with **nitrogen limitation** → triggers antibiotic synthesis
- **Phosphate limitation** → strong trigger for many secondary metabolites
- **Slow growth rate** (trophophase → idiophase transition) → derepression of biosynthetic gene clusters
- **pH shifts** during late-log phase activate biosynthetic enzymes

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Industrial penicillin/streptomycin production deliberately uses *fed-batch* or *slow-feeding* strategies to maintain a delicate balance: enough carbon for energy, but limited nitrogen to keep cells in the *idiophase* — maximizing antibiotic yield.

### 2.2 Penicillin — Deep Mechanism & Production

#### 2.2.1 Chemical Structure & Mechanism of Action

The **β-lactam ring** is the pharmacologically active warhead. It structurally mimics the **D-Ala-D-Ala** terminus of the peptidoglycan precursor (the building block of bacterial cell walls).

**Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. Penicillin enters the bacterial periplasm.
2. The β-lactam ring **irreversibly acylates** the active-site serine of **DD-transpeptidase** (also called *penicillin-binding protein, PBP*).
3. The enzyme is now permanently blocked — it cannot crosslink peptidoglycan chains.
4. The bacterial cell wall weakens → **osmotic lysis** → bacterial death.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **Gram-positive bacteria** (with thick exposed peptidoglycan) are more susceptible, while **Gram-negative bacteria** (with an outer LPS membrane shielding their thin peptidoglycan) are intrinsically more resistant.

#### 2.2.2 Industrial Penicillin Production (Submerged Fermentation)

| Process Parameter | Optimum Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Microorganism** | *P. chrysogenum* (improved strains) | Yield: < 5 mg/L (Fleming) → > **50,000 mg/L** (modern) |
| **Inoculum** | Spores from agar slants → vegetative inoculum in seed tanks | Maintains genetic stability |
| **Carbon Source** | **Lactose** (slowly metabolized) preferred over glucose | Glucose causes **catabolite repression** of penicillin synthesis |
| **Nitrogen Source** | **Corn steep liquor (CSL)** | Cheap; provides amino acids, vitamins, minerals |
| **Precursor** | **Phenylacetic acid** (or phenoxyacetic acid) | Direct incorporation into the side chain of Penicillin G / V |
| **pH** | **6.0 – 6.5** | Drop below 5.5 → penicillin hydrolysis |
| **Temperature** | **25 – 27 °C** | Higher temperatures destabilize β-lactam ring |
| **Aeration** | High O₂ transfer; agitation + sparging | *P. chrysogenum* is an obligate aerobe |
| **Fermentation Mode** | **Fed-batch** | Maintains low glucose → avoids catabolite repression |
| **Cycle Time** | **120 – 200 hours** | Long idiophase |
| **Yield** | Modern strains: **30 – 100 g/L** | Roughly a 10,000-fold increase vs. original Fleming isolate |

#### 2.2.3 Penicillin Downstream Processing

1. **Filtration** (rotary vacuum filter) — separates mycelium from broth.
2. **pH adjustment to 2.0 – 2.5** with H₂SO₄ or H₃PO₄.
3. **Solvent extraction** into **butyl acetate or amyl acetate** at low pH.
4. **Back-extraction** into aqueous buffer (pH 7.0) using NaHCO₃.
5. **Activated carbon treatment** for decolorization.
6. **Crystallization** as **potassium or sodium salt** of Penicillin G.
7. **Lyophilization** (freeze-drying) for stable storage at 2 – 8 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Semisynthetic Penicillins:** Penicillin G is cleaved by **penicillin acylase** to yield **6-aminopenicillanic acid (6-APA)**, which is then chemically acylated with new side chains to produce **ampicillin, amoxicillin, methicillin**, and other clinically superior derivatives resistant to β-lactamases.

### 2.3 Streptomycin — Deep Mechanism & Production

#### 2.3.1 Chemical Structure & Mechanism of Action

Streptomycin belongs to the **aminoglycoside** class. It contains:
- **Streptidine** (an inositol derivative with two guanidino groups)
- **Streptose** (a 6-deoxyhexose sugar)
- **N-methyl-L-glucosamine**

**Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. Streptomycin diffuses through porin channels (in Gram-negatives) or is actively transported.
2. It **binds irreversibly to the 30S subunit** of the bacterial 70S ribosome.
3. This binding **distorts the A-site** (aminoacyl site) of the ribosome.
4. The ribosome **misreads the mRNA codons** — incorrect amino acids are incorporated.
5. The aberrant proteins disrupt membrane integrity and essential functions → **bacterial cell death**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Streptomycin is **bactericidal** (kills bacteria) and was the **first effective cure for tuberculosis** (1944). Waksman won the **1952 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine** for this discovery.

#### 2.3.2 Industrial Streptomycin Production (Submerged Fermentation)

| Process Parameter | Optimum Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Microorganism** | *Streptomyces griseus* | Filamentous, soil-dwelling actinomycete |
| **Carbon Source** | **Glucose** (or starch) | Rapid utilization supports growth |
| **Nitrogen Source** | **Soybean meal**, peptone, yeast extract | Complex organic N preferred |
| **Mineral Salts** | NaCl, K₂HPO₄, MgSO₄, FeSO₄ | Streptomycin biosynthesis needs Mg²⁺ |
| **pH** | **7.0 – 7.5** | Neutral to slightly alkaline |
| **Temperature** | **28 – 30 °C** | Standard for *Streptomyces* |
| **Aeration** | Vigorous | High oxygen demand during rapid growth |
| **Fermentation Mode** | **Batch** | Unlike penicillin, classical batch works well |
| **Cycle Time** | **72 – 120 hours** | Shorter than penicillin |
| **Yield** | **5 – 15 g/L** (modern strains) | Substantially improved by mutation & selection |

#### 2.3.3 Streptomycin Downstream Processing

1. **Filtration** of the broth (mycelium removal).
2. **Activated carbon adsorption** of streptomycin from filtrate.
3. **Elution** with acidic methanol or aqueous acid.
4. **Ion-exchange chromatography** (cation-exchange resin) — exploits streptomycin's strong basicity.
5. **Concentration** by vacuum evaporation.
6. **Crystallization** as the **sulfate, hydrochloride, or calcium chloride double salt**.
7. **Drying** and packaging.

> [!NOTE]
> Streptomycin is **highly water-soluble** and **basic** (positively charged at physiological pH), which is why **cation-exchange chromatography** is the workhorse of its purification — an important contrast to penicillin's organic-solvent-based extraction.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Quantitative Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are essential for solving KTU 2024 numerical problems on antibiotic fermentation productivity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Productivity ($P_{v}$)** | $P_{v} = \dfrac{C_{f} - C_{i}}{t_{f} - t_{i}}$ | g/(L·h) | Amount of antibiotic produced per litre of fermenter per hour |
| 2 | **Specific Product Formation Rate ($q_{p}$)** | $q_{p} = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ | g antibiotic / (g cells · h) | How efficiently each gram of biomass makes antibiotic |
| 3 | **Overall Yield on Substrate ($Y_{P/S}$)** | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | g product / g substrate | Grams of antibiotic per gram of sugar consumed |
| 4 | **Product Concentration ($C_{P}$)** | $C_{P} = \dfrac{m_{P}}{V}$ | g/L | Final titre in the broth |
| 5 | **Biomass Concentration ($X$)** | $X = \dfrac{m_{\text{cells}}}{V}$ | g/L | Cell density in broth |
| 6 | **Doubling Time ($t_{d}$)** | $t_{d} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu_{\max}}$ | h | Time for biomass to double at $\mu_{\max}$ |
| 7 | **Mass Balance on Product** | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = q_{p} \cdot X - k_{d} \cdot P$ | g/(L·h) | Net formation minus degradation |

> [!NOTE]
> **For penicillin's long idiophase**, $q_{p}$ is **low** (slow specific rate) but $P_{v}$ is **acceptable** because $X$ is high. **For streptomycin's shorter batch cycle**, $q_{p}$ is **higher** during the rapid production window.

### 2.5 Comparative Engineering Summary

| Engineering Aspect | **Penicillin** | **Streptomycin** |
|---|---|---|
| **Microbial Class** | Filamentous fungus (mold) | Filamentous bacterium (actinomycete) |
| **Cell Morphology** | Hyphae / mycelium | Substrate mycelium + aerial hyphae |
| **Fermenter Type** | Stirred-tank reactor (STR), baffled | STR with strong agitation |
| **Rheology of Broth** | **Highly non-Newtonian** (viscous, pseudoplastic) | Non-Newtonian but less viscous |
| **Oxygen Demand** | Very high | High |
| **Heat Removal** | Significant (long cycle) | Moderate |
| **Carbon Catabolite Repression** | **YES** (avoid glucose excess) | Minimal |
| **Critical Precursor** | Phenylacetic acid | None critical, but Mg²⁺ helps |
| **Recovery Chemistry** | Solvent extraction (low pH) | Ion-exchange (basicity) |
| **Storage Form** | Crystalline K⁺/Na⁺ salt, 2 – 8 °C | Sulfate or hydrochloride salt |
| **Cost Driver** | Precursor feeding & pH control | Carbon source & aeration |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Quantitative Analysis & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Derivation — Volumetric Productivity of Penicillin in a Fed-Batch Fermenter

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-style Problem Framework:** A 100,000 L working-volume fed-batch fermenter produces penicillin. Initial penicillin concentration = 200 mg/L at t = 24 h. Final concentration = 42,000 mg/L at t = 168 h. Compute the volumetric productivity in g/(L·h).

**Given data:**
- $C_{i}$ = 200 mg/L
- $C_{f}$ = 42,000 mg/L
- $t_{i}$ = 24 h
- $t_{f}$ = 168 h

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula** (from KTU Formula Sheet):

$$P_{v} = \dfrac{C_{f} - C_{i}}{t_{f} - t_{i}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (in consistent units).**

First, convert concentration difference to g/L:

$$\Delta C = C_{f} - C_{i} = 42{,}000 - 200 = 41{,}800 \;\text{mg/L}$$

$$\Delta C = \dfrac{41{,}800}{1000} = 41.8 \;\text{g/L}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the time interval:**

$$\Delta t = t_{f} - t_{i} = 168 - 24 = 144 \;\text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the volumetric productivity:**

$$P_{v} = \dfrac{41.8 \;\text{g/L}}{144 \;\text{h}}$$

$$P_{v} = 0.2903 \;\text{g/(L·h)}$$

**Step 5 — Express with appropriate significant figures and units:**

$$\boxed{P_{v} \approx 0.29 \;\text{g/(L·h)}}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:**

A $P_{v}$ of 0.29 g/(L·h) is characteristic of an industrial penicillin process. For a 100,000 L fermenter, the **total daily production** is:

$$\text{Daily output} = P_{v} \times V \times 24 \;\text{h}$$

$$\text{Daily output} = 0.29 \times 100{,}000 \times 24$$

$$\text{Daily output} = 696{,}000 \;\text{g/day} = 696 \;\text{kg/day}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This kind of computation is precisely how KTU tests whether students understand that volumetric productivity drives **plant capacity planning**, **fermenter count**, and ultimately **manufacturing cost per kg of API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient)**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Specific Product Formation Rate ($q_{p}$) for Streptomycin

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU type):** During streptomycin fermentation, biomass $X$ = 8 g/L, and the product formation rate $\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ measured over a 4-hour interval is 0.24 g/(L·h). Compute $q_{p}$.

**Given data:**
- $X$ = 8 g/L
- $\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ = 0.24 g/(L·h)

**Step 1 — Apply the $q_{p}$ formula:**

$$q_{p} = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$q_{p} = \dfrac{0.24 \;\text{g/(L·h)}}{8 \;\text{g/L}}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the units carefully:**

$$q_{p} = \dfrac{0.24}{8} \;\dfrac{\text{g/(L·h)}}{\text{g/L}}$$

$$q_{p} = 0.03 \;\dfrac{1}{\text{h}}$$

$$\boxed{q_{p} = 0.03 \;\text{h}^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

This means every gram of *Streptomyces griseus* biomass produces 0.03 g of streptomycin per hour. Comparing across different strains or conditions:
- Higher $q_{p}$ → more efficient producer
- Used in **strain ranking** during mutation/screening programs

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Overall Yield Coefficient $Y_{P/S}$ for Penicillin

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** 50,000 L of fermentation broth yields 1,500 kg of pure penicillin. The total glucose consumed during the run was 30,000 kg. Compute the yield coefficient $Y_{P/S}$.

**Step 1 — Compute final product concentration:**

$$C_{P} = \dfrac{m_{P}}{V} = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000 \;\text{g}}{50{,}000 \;\text{L}} = 30 \;\text{g/L}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the substrate consumed per litre:**

$$S_{\text{used}} = \dfrac{30{,}000{,}000 \;\text{g}}{50{,}000 \;\text{L}} = 600 \;\text{g/L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the yield formula:**

$$Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} = \dfrac{30 \;\text{g/L}}{600 \;\text{g/L}}$$

$$\boxed{Y_{P/S} = 0.05 \;\dfrac{\text{g penicillin}}{\text{g glucose}}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering meaning:**

Only **5% of the glucose mass** ends up as penicillin — the rest goes to biomass, CO₂, maintenance energy, and by-products. This is **typical for secondary metabolites**, which have inherently low carbon yields. **Improving $Y_{P/S}$** is a major target of strain improvement.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Doubling Time of *P. chrysogenum* During Trophophase

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** The maximum specific growth rate of *P. chrysogenum* in a seed fermenter is $\mu_{\max} = 0.20 \;\text{h}^{-1}$. Compute the doubling time.

**Step 1 — Use the exponential growth relation:**

$$X = X_{0}\, e^{\mu_{\max} t}$$

**Step 2 — Setting $X = 2X_{0}$ (one doubling):**

$$2X_{0} = X_{0}\, e^{\mu_{\max} t_{d}}$$

$$2 = e^{\mu_{\max} t_{d}}$$

$$\ln 2 = \mu_{\max} t_{d}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t_{d}$:**

$$t_{d} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu_{\max}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute value:**

$$t_{d} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.20 \;\text{h}^{-1}}$$

$$\boxed{t_{d} = 3.47 \;\text{h}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

A 3.5-hour doubling time is brisk for a filamentous fungus. Seed tanks are designed to operate in this **trophophase window** to build sufficient inoculum mass before transfer to the main production fermenter (where the long idiophase takes over).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Penicillin vs Streptomycin

| Analytical Parameter | **Penicillin** | **Streptomycin** |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu_{\max}$ (trophophase) | 0.15 – 0.25 h⁻¹ | 0.30 – 0.50 h⁻¹ |
| $t_{d}$ | ~3 – 5 h | ~1.5 – 2.5 h |
| Idio-phase duration | ~120 – 180 h | ~50 – 100 h |
| Final $C_{P}$ | 30 – 50 g/L | 5 – 15 g/L |
| $Y_{P/S}$ | 0.05 – 0.10 g/g | 0.02 – 0.05 g/g |
| $P_{v}$ | 0.20 – 0.30 g/(L·h) | 0.05 – 0.15 g/(L·h) |
| Dominant recovery method | Solvent extraction | Ion-exchange |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's View:** Penicillin compensates for its low $q_{p}$ with a **much higher final titre** and **longer productive window**. Streptomycin is faster but reaches lower titre — both are economically viable at industrial scale.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Industrial Penicillin Production (Submerged Fermentation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spore Suspension Agar Slant] --> B[First Stage Seed Tank]
    B --> C[Second Stage Seed Tank]
    C --> D[Production Fermenter Fed Batch]
    D --> E[Rotary Vacuum Filter]
    E --> F[Mycelium Waste Disposal]
    E --> G[Broth Filtrate]
    G --> H[Acidification pH 2 to 2.5 H2SO4]
    H --> I[Solvent Extraction Butyl Acetate]
    I --> J[Back Extraction Aqueous pH 7.0]
    J --> K[Activated Carbon Decolorization]
    K --> L[Vacuum Crystallization]
    L --> M[K Penicillin G Crystals]
    M --> N[Lyophilization Drying]
    N --> O[Finished API Storage 2 to 8 C]

    P[Air Sterile Filter] --> D
    Q[Corn Steep Liquor] --> D
    R[Lactose Feed] --> D
    S[Phenylacetic Acid Precursor] --> D

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style M fill:#d4f4dd
    style O fill:#d4f4dd
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Industrial Streptomycin Production

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sporulated Agar Slant Streptomyces griseus] --> B[Seed Fermenter]
    B --> C[Production Fermenter Batch Aerobic]
    C --> D[Broth Harvest]
    D --> E[Filtration Mycelium Removal]
    E --> F[Mycelium Waste]
    E --> G[Streptomycin Rich Filtrate]
    G --> H[Activated Carbon Adsorption]
    H --> I[Acid Methanol Elution]
    I --> J[Cation Exchange Chromatography]
    J --> K[Vacuum Evaporation]
    K --> L[Streptomycin Sulfate Crystallization]
    L --> M[Drying and Milling]
    M --> N[Quality Control Testing]
    N --> O[Packaged API]

    P[Sterile Air Supply] --> C
    Q[Glucose Feed] --> C
    R[Soybean Meal] --> C
    S[Mineral Salts Mg2plus] --> C

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style L fill:#d4f4dd
    style O fill:#d4f4dd
```

### 4.3 Mechanistic Block Diagram — Penicillin Action on Bacterial Cell Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Penicillin in Periplasm] --> B[Beta Lactam Ring Reaches PBP]
    B --> C[Acylation of Active Site Serine]
    C --> D[Transpeptidase Permanently Inactivated]
    D --> E[Peptidoglycan Crosslinking Blocked]
    E --> F[Cell Wall Weakening]
    F --> G[Osmotic Imbalance]
    G --> H[Cell Lysis and Death]

    style A fill:#fde2e2
    style H fill:#e2e2fd
```

### 4.4 Mechanistic Block Diagram — Streptomycin Action on Ribosome

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Streptomycin Enters Cell via Porin] --> B[Diffuses to Cytoplasm]
    B --> C[Binds to 30S Subunit of 70S Ribosome]
    C --> D[A site Distortion]
    D --> E[mRNA Codon Misreading]
    E --> F[Incorrect Amino Acid Incorporation]
    F --> G[Abortive Polypeptide Chains]
    G --> H[Membrane Damage and Cell Death]

    style A fill:#fde2e2
    style H fill:#e2e2fd
```

### 4.5 Conceptual Architecture — Trophophase vs Idiophase

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TROPHOPHASE
    A1[Active Growth] --> A2[Primary Metabolites]
    A2 --> A3[Biomass Accumulation]
    A3 --> A4[Energy and Biosynthesis]
    end

    A4 --> B1[Nutrient Limitation Signal]

    subgraph IDIOPHASE
    B1 --> B2[Stationary Phase Onset]
    B2 --> B3[Secondary Metabolite Gene Cluster ON]
    B3 --> B4[Penicillin Streptomycin Synthesis]
    end

    style A2 fill:#d4f4dd
    style B4 fill:#fff4e1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture (Adapted Schematic):** The above diagrams intentionally represent functional, block-level data flow rather than physical process layouts. This matches KTU's preference for **functional architecture flow mapping** when topic complexity makes physical drawings impractical to render in text-based diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Distinguish between primary and secondary metabolites with two examples each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **Primary Metabolites** | **Secondary Metabolites** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Compounds essential for cell growth, reproduction, and basic metabolism | Compounds produced after active growth; not essential for basic cellular functions |
| **Phase of Production** | Trophophase (log phase) | Idiophase (stationary phase) |
| **Examples** | Ethanol (yeast), L-glutamic acid (*Corynebacterium*), lactic acid | Penicillin (*P. chrysogenum*), streptomycin (*S. griseus*), rifamycin |
| **Yield** | Usually high (g/L to tens of g/L) | Usually lower (mg/L to a few g/L, except in improved strains) |

**Examples cited:**
- *Primary*: Ethanol, L-glutamic acid
- *Secondary*: Penicillin, Streptomycin

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **secondary metabolites with toxic by-products**. Remember: secondary metabolites have **defined ecological/pharmaceutical functions**, not just random "waste."

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Name the organism that produces streptomycin. Write the chemical class and the mechanism of action of streptomycin.*

**Model Answer (in 3 points):**

1. **Producing Organism:** *Streptomyces griseus*
2. **Chemical Class:** Aminoglycoside antibiotic (contains streptidine, streptose, and N-methyl-L-glucosamine moieties)
3. **Mechanism of Action:** Binds to the **30S subunit** of the bacterial 70S ribosome → causes **misreading of mRNA codons** → faulty proteins → bacterial cell death (bactericidal)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly say "inhibits cell wall synthesis" for streptomycin. That is **penicillin's** mechanism. Streptomycin acts on **protein synthesis machinery**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule Reminder:** Part B questions of Module 3 offer an internal choice. You may answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks, typically split as 7 + 7 across sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Penicillin-Focused

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the industrial production of **penicillin** by *Penicillium chrysogenum* using submerged fermentation, highlighting the critical process parameters and the role of **phenylacetic acid** as a precursor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of action** of penicillin on bacterial cells. How does this explain its selective toxicity against Gram-positive bacteria? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Industrial Production of Penicillin — Step-by-Step Model Answer (7 marks)**

1. **Strain & Inoculum Development (1 mark):** 
   Use of high-yielding *Penicillium chrysogenum* (e.g., NRRL 1951 / improved strains from mutation-screening programs). Spores from agar slants are germinated progressively through **first-stage and second-stage seed tanks** to build sufficient inoculum biomass.

2. **Production Fermenter (1.5 marks):** 
   The inoculum is transferred to large **stirred-tank fermenters (50,000 – 200,000 L)** operated in **fed-batch mode** at:
   - **pH 6.0 – 6.5** (controlled via ammonia/NaOH)
   - **Temperature 25 – 27 °C** (β-lactam ring is heat-labile)
   - **High aeration and agitation** (maintain dissolved O₂ > 30% saturation; *P. chrysogenum* is an obligate aerobe)

3. **Medium Composition (1.5 marks):** 
   - **Carbon source:** Lactose (preferred) or glucose fed slowly — *glucose excess causes catabolite repression* of penicillin biosynthesis
   - **Nitrogen source:** **Corn steep liquor (CSL)** — a cheap complex source of amino acids, vitamins, and minerals
   - **Inorganic salts:** KH₂PO₄, MgSO₄, trace metals
   - **Precursor:** **Phenylacetic acid** (or its derivatives) is fed continuously during idiophase

4. **Role of Phenylacetic Acid as Precursor (2 marks):** 
   Phenylacetic acid serves as the **side-chain precursor** that is directly incorporated into the **β-lactam nucleus** to form **Penicillin G (benzylpenicillin)**. By feeding excess precursor:
   - Side-chain incorporation is enhanced
   - Side reactions producing unwanted penicillins (e.g., Penicillin K with longer side chains) are minimized
   - Final titre can be boosted by 30 – 50%

5. **Cycle Time & Yield (1 mark):** 
   Fermentation runs **120 – 200 hours**. Modern strains yield **30 – 50 g/L**, with volumetric productivity of 0.2 – 0.3 g/(L·h).

**(b) Mechanism of Action & Gram-Positive Selectivity (7 marks)**

1. **The β-Lactam Ring (1.5 marks):** 
   Penicillin contains a strained, four-membered **β-lactam ring** fused to a thiazolidine ring. The ring mimics the **D-Ala-D-Ala dipeptide terminus** of the peptidoglycan precursor (UDP-MurNAc-pentapeptide).

2. **PBP Inactivation (2 marks):** 
   In dividing bacteria, the enzyme **DD-transpeptidase** (also called **penicillin-binding protein, PBP**) catalyzes the crosslinking of peptidoglycan chains. Penicillin **irreversibly acylates the active-site serine** of this enzyme via the β-lactam ring. The PBP is permanently inactivated.

3. **Cell Wall Failure (1.5 marks):** 
   With crosslinking halted, the growing peptidoglycan layer cannot form a stable mesh. The cell wall becomes mechanically weak.

4. **Lysis & Death (1 mark):** 
   As the bacterium continues to take up water by osmosis, the weakened wall **ruptures** → **osmotic lysis** → bacterial death. This is why penicillin is **bactericidal** (kills, not just inhibits).

5. **Gram-Positive Selectivity (1 mark):** 
   - **Gram-positive bacteria** have a **thick, exposed peptidoglycan layer (~20 – 80 nm)** directly accessible to penicillin in the periplasm — hence high susceptibility.
   - **Gram-negative bacteria** have a **thin peptidoglycan layer (~2 – 7 nm)** shielded by an outer **lipopolysaccharide (LPS) membrane** that restricts penicillin entry — hence intrinsic resistance. (Mutations producing β-lactamase enzymes also confer acquired resistance.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question A):** Students often forget to mention:
> (i) **catabolite repression by glucose** (deduct 1 mark),
> (ii) **why the β-lactam ring is heat-labile** (deduct 0.5 mark),
> (iii) the **role of penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs)** by name (deduct 1 mark).
> KTU expects the term "DD-transpeptidase / PBP" explicitly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Streptomycin-Focused + Comparative Depth

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the **industrial production of streptomycin** by *Streptomyces griseus*, including the medium composition, process parameters, and the key recovery steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **mechanisms of action** of penicillin and streptomycin. Explain why these two antibiotics can be used **synergistically** in clinical practice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Industrial Production of Streptomycin (7 marks)**

1. **Strain & Inoculum (1 mark):** 
   *Streptomyces griseus* is maintained as a sporulated agar slant. Spores are germinated in **first-stage seed fermenters** before scale-up to **production fermenters** of 30,000 – 100,000 L working volume.

2. **Medium Composition (2 marks):** 
   - **Carbon source:** Glucose (rapidly metabolized; catabolite repression is not as severe as in penicillin)
   - **Nitrogen source:** **Soybean meal** + peptone + yeast extract (complex organic nitrogen)
   - **Inorganic salts:** NaCl, K₂HPO₄, MgSO₄, FeSO₄, CaCO₃ (for pH buffering)
   - **Trace elements:** Zn²⁺, Mn²⁺

3. **Process Parameters (1.5 marks):** 
   - **pH 7.0 – 7.5** (neutral-to-slightly-alkaline)
   - **Temperature 28 – 30 °C** (standard for *Streptomyces*)
   - **Aeration:** High — vigorous agitation + sparging
   - **Mode:** **Batch fermentation** (no need for fed-batch like penicillin)
   - **Cycle:** 72 – 120 hours (shorter than penicillin)

4. **Recovery — Key Steps (2.5 marks):** 
   - **Filtration** of the broth to remove *Streptomyces* mycelium
   - **Activated carbon adsorption** of streptomycin from the filtrate
   - **Elution** with acidic methanol/aqueous acid
   - **Cation-exchange chromatography** (exploits the strong basicity of streptomycin's guanidino and amino groups)
   - **Vacuum evaporation** to concentrate
   - **Crystallization** as **streptomycin sulfate** or **streptomycin hydrochloride**
   - **Drying, milling, quality control, packaging**

5. **Yield & Productivity (1 mark):** 
   Modern strains yield 5 – 15 g/L streptomycin; volumetric productivity 0.05 – 0.15 g/(L·h).

**(b) Comparative Mechanism of Action & Synergy (7 marks)**

1. **Penicillin Mechanism (1.5 marks):** 
   Inhibits **bacterial cell wall (peptidoglycan) synthesis** by acylating the active-site serine of **DD-transpeptidase / PBP**, blocking peptidoglycan crosslinking → osmotic lysis. **Bactericidal against actively dividing cells.**

2. **Streptomycin Mechanism (1.5 marks):** 
   Inhibits **bacterial protein synthesis** by binding **irreversibly to the 30S subunit** of the 70S ribosome, distorting the A-site and causing **mRNA misreading** → faulty, non-functional proteins → membrane damage and cell death. **Bactericidal against both dividing and non-dividing cells.**

3. **Key Differences in Target (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | **Penicillin** | **Streptomycin** |
|---|---|---|
| Target | Cell wall synthesis (PBP) | Protein synthesis (30S ribosome) |
| Effective against | Actively dividing cells | Both dividing and resting cells |
| Spectrum | Mostly Gram-positive | Broad — Gram-positive, Gram-negative, *M. tuberculosis* |
| Toxicity (human) | Low (no peptidoglycan in humans) | Higher (ototoxicity, nephrotoxicity — mammalian ribosomes differ but mitochondria share some features) |

4. **Why They Are Synergistic (2.5 marks):** 
   - **Different molecular targets** — bacteria resistant to one (e.g., by altered PBP) are not necessarily resistant to the other.
   - **Different physiological states** — penicillin kills actively dividing cells; streptomycin also kills non-dividing persisters, so together they provide broader coverage.
   - **Cell wall penetration synergy** — by weakening the cell wall, penicillin can sometimes allow streptomycin to penetrate more efficiently into Gram-positive cells.
   - **Clinical example:** Penicillin + streptomycin is a classical combination for **enterococcal endocarditis** and some severe Gram-negative infections.
   - **Reduced resistance development** — combinations lower the statistical probability of a single bacterium acquiring both resistance mechanisms simultaneously.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):** Common mistakes costing marks:
> (i) Confusing 30S vs 50S ribosomal subunits (deduct 1 mark),
> (ii) Not justifying **why** synergy occurs mechanistically (deduct 2 marks — just listing the combination is not enough),
> (iii) Omitting the **clinical example** like enterococcal endocarditis (deduct 0.5 mark),
> (iv) Confusing **streptomycin's** target with **chloramphenicol's** (50S) — KTU examiners specifically test this confusion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-density summary — read before exam)**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Secondary metabolites are produced during **idiophase** (stationary phase), not trophophase
- ☐ Antibiotics are **not essential** for microbial growth — they are **ecological weapons**
- ☐ Penicillin = **β-lactam**; Streptomycin = **aminoglycoside**
- ☐ Penicillin mechanism → **cell wall synthesis inhibition** (PBP/DD-transpeptidase)
- ☐ Streptomycin mechanism → **protein synthesis inhibition** (30S ribosomal binding)

**Key Producers:**
- ☐ Penicillin → ***Penicillium chrysogenum*** (mold)
- ☐ Streptomycin → ***Streptomyces griseus*** (actinomycete / filamentous bacterium)

**Industrial Process — Penicillin:**
- ☐ Fed-batch submerged fermentation
- ☐ 25 – 27 °C, pH 6.0 – 6.5
- ☐ Carbon: **Lactose** (avoid glucose repression)
- ☐ Nitrogen: **Corn steep liquor**
- ☐ Precursor: **Phenylacetic acid** → forms Penicillin G
- ☐ Recovery: **Solvent extraction** (butyl acetate at pH 2 – 2.5)
- ☐ 120 – 200 h cycle; titre 30 – 50 g/L

**Industrial Process — Streptomycin:**
- ☐ Batch submerged fermentation
- ☐ 28 – 30 °C, pH 7.0 – 7.5
- ☐ Carbon: **Glucose**; Nitrogen: **Soybean meal**
- ☐ Recovery: **Cation-exchange chromatography**
- ☐ 72 – 120 h cycle; titre 5 – 15 g/L

**Key Formula Recap:**
- ☐ $P_{v} = \dfrac{C_{f} - C_{i}}{t_{f} - t_{i}}$ → g/(L·h)
- ☐ $q_{p} = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ → h⁻¹
- ☐ $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ → g/g
- ☐ $t_{d} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu_{\max}}$ → h

**Common Examiner Traps:**
- ☐ Streptomycin binds **30S**, not 50S (avoid confusing with chloramphenicol/macrolides)
- ☐ Penicillin is bactericidal **only against dividing cells** (resting cells are not making peptidoglycan)
- ☐ Glucose **represses** penicillin — lactose does not (repression ≠ inhibition)
- ☐ Penicillin recovery uses **organic solvent extraction**; streptomycin recovery uses **ion exchange**
- ☐ Fleming discovered penicillin (1928); Waksman discovered streptomycin (1943) and won the 1952 Nobel Prize
- ☐ Penicillin is selectively toxic to bacteria because **humans lack peptidoglycan**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU frequently pairs Module 3 questions with **Module 4 (downstream processing)** in integrated problems. Be ready to compute **mass balance, recovery yield, and overall process economy** when given a multi-step fermentation + recovery problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vitamins (Vitamin B2, Vitamin B12)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBTT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vitamins (Vitamin B2, Vitamin B12)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Industrial Vitamin Bioprocessing

**Vitamins** are low-molecular-weight organic compounds that act as **coenzymes** or **precursors of coenzymes** and are essential micronutrients required in trace amounts for normal metabolic functioning of living organisms. Since humans and animals cannot synthesize most vitamins *de novo* (or in sufficient quantities), they must be obtained through diet or via **industrial bioprocess technology** — which is the focus of Module 3 of the PBBTT304 syllabus.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 specifically emphasizes the **microbial production of vitamins** using fermentation technology, including strain improvement, fermentation process design, downstream processing, and applications in the food, pharmaceutical, feed, and nutraceutical industries.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — Vitamins):** The course outcome emphasizes understanding the *production strains*, *fermentation strategies*, *downstream recovery*, and *industrial applications* of vitamins B2 (Riboflavin) and B12 (Cyanocobalamin) using microbial bioprocesses.

---

## 1.2 Vitamin B2 — Riboflavin (The "Yellow Vitamin")

### Formal Definition
**Riboflavin (Vitamin B2)** is a water-soluble vitamin belonging to the flavin group. Its chemical name is **7,8-dimethyl-10-(D-ribityl)isoalloxazine**, with the molecular formula **C₁₇H₂₀N₄O₆** and a molecular weight of **376.36 g/mol**. The molecule consists of an **isoalloxazine ring system** (a tricyclic heterocycle) attached to a **ribitol side chain** (a sugar alcohol derived from ribose).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Riboflavin is the precursor of two essential coenzymes: **Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN)** and **Flavin Adenine Dinucleotide (FAD)**. Both are crucial for **redox reactions**, **electron transport chain**, and **energy metabolism**.

### Key Physical & Chemical Properties
- **Appearance:** Yellow to orange-yellow crystalline powder
- **Solubility:** Sparingly soluble in water (about 12 mg/100 mL at 25°C), practically insoluble in most organic solvents
- **Optical Property:** Exhibits intense **yellow-green fluorescence** under UV light (λ_emission ≈ 530 nm) — a unique identifying feature
- **Stability:** Sensitive to light (photodegradation → lumiflavin in alkaline medium, lumichrome in neutral/acidic medium), but relatively heat-stable
- **Melting Point:** Decomposes at ~280°C

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Yellow Battery Charger" 🔋
> Imagine your body as a smartphone. Glucose is the electrical current, but **Riboflavin is the fast-charging chip** that efficiently converts that current into usable energy (ATP). Without it, the battery charges slowly, overheats, and the device (your body) becomes sluggish. Microbial factories (like *Ashbya gossypii*) are like the "charging stations" producing this yellow vitamin in massive fermentation tanks.

---

## 1.3 Vitamin B12 — Cyanocobalamin (The "Red Vitamin")

### Formal Definition
**Vitamin B12** is a water-soluble vitamin that contains the trace element **cobalt** at its core — making it the only vitamin with a metal ion. The most common industrial form is **Cyanocobalamin**, with the molecular formula **C₆₃H₈₈CoN₁₄O₁₄P** and a molecular weight of **1355.37 g/mol**. Its structure is built around a **corrin ring** (similar to porphyrin but with one less methine bridge), a central cobalt atom, a 5,6-dimethylbenzimidazole nucleotide loop, and a variable upper axial ligand (–CN in cyanocobalamin, –CH₃ in methylcobalamin, –5′-deoxyadenosyl in adenosylcobalamin).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cyanocobalamin is the **industrial standard** form of Vitamin B12 used in supplements and food fortification. The other biologically active forms include **methylcobalamin** and **adenosylcobalamin (cobamamide)**, which act as coenzymes in **methyl transfer reactions** and **intramolecular rearrangements**, respectively.

### Key Physical & Chemical Properties
- **Appearance:** Dark red crystalline powder (hygroscopic)
- **Solubility:** Soluble in water (~1 g/100 mL), soluble in alcohols, insoluble in acetone, chloroform, and ether
- **Color Origin:** The intense red color arises from the **Co(III) center** coordinated within the corrin macrocycle
- **Stability:** Sensitive to strong acids, alkalis, oxidizing/reducing agents, and light; stable to heat in neutral aqueous solution
- **Biosynthesis:** Only produced by **bacteria and archaea** (no fungi, plants, or animals can synthesize it)

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Master Controller of Nerves & Blood" 🧠
> Think of Vitamin B12 as the **CEO of the body's red blood cell factory and nerve-maintenance crew**. It issues executive orders (methyl group transfers) to keep DNA synthesis running and myelin sheaths (insulation around nerves) intact. A deficiency is like a CEO suddenly going on vacation — chaos ensues: **anemia** (factory shuts down) and **neurological disorders** (nerves fray). The microbial world (*Pseudomonas denitrificans*) is the corporate HQ that produces this irreplaceable executive.

---

## 1.4 Why Microbial Production? (The KTU Context)

> [!NOTE]
> Historically, Vitamin B2 was chemically synthesized, and B12 was extracted from liver (1 ton of liver ≈ 25 mg of B12). However, **microbial fermentation** has become the **dominant industrial route** because it offers:
> - **Sustainability** (renewable feedstocks like glucose, molasses, corn-steep liquor)
> - **Stereochemical purity** (no racemic mixtures)
> - **Lower cost at scale** (especially for B2)
> - **Regulatory acceptance** (GRAS organisms)
> - **Reduced environmental burden** vs. chemical synthesis (no heavy metal catalysts for B2)

### Industrial Production Microorganisms — Quick Reference

| Vitamin | Primary Industrial Producer | Type of Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin)** | *Ashbya gossypii* (overproducer) | Aerobic fungal fermentation |
| **Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin)** | *Eremothecium ashbyii* (formerly *Ashbyia gossypii*) | Aerobic fungal fermentation |
| **Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin)** | *Bacillus subtilis* (recombinant) | Submerged bacterial fermentation |
| **Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin)** | *Candida famata* (recombinant) | Yeast fermentation |
| **Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin)** | *Pseudomonas denitrificans* | Aerobic bacterial fermentation (most common) |
| **Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin)** | *Propionibacterium shermanii* | Anaerobic + aerobic two-stage fermentation |
| **Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin)** | *Bacillus megaterium* | Aerobic bacterial fermentation |
| **Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin)** | *Streptomyces griseus*, *Micromonospora* sp. | Aerobic actinomycete fermentation (by-product of antibiotics) |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry, Biochemistry & Metabolic Role

### 2.1.1 Vitamin B2 — Riboflavin: Biochemical Significance
Riboflavin functions as the **obligate precursor of FMN and FAD**, which serve as coenzymes for a wide class of enzymes collectively termed **flavoenzymes** (or flavoproteins). These enzymes catalyze critical redox reactions including:

- **Succinate dehydrogenase** (TCA cycle) → uses FAD
- **NADH dehydrogenase (Complex I)** → uses FMN
- **Acyl-CoA dehydrogenase** (β-oxidation of fatty acids) → uses FAD
- **Glutathione reductase** → uses FAD
- **Xanthine oxidase** → uses FAD
- **Dihydrolipoamide dehydrogenase** (pyruvate dehydrogenase complex) → uses FAD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deficiency symptoms (Ariboflavinosis):** Cheilosis (cracks at corners of mouth), glossitis (inflamed tongue), corneal vascularization, seborrheic dermatitis, and anemia. Riboflavin deficiency also impairs the metabolism of other B vitamins (B6, folate, niacin), leading to cascading deficiencies.

### 2.1.2 Vitamin B12 — Cyanocobalamin: Biochemical Significance
Vitamin B12 is a cofactor for only **two known enzymes in humans**, yet its deficiency has severe systemic consequences:

1. **Methionine Synthase (MS)** — uses **methylcobalamin** as cofactor
   - Catalyzes transfer of methyl group from **5-methyltetrahydrofolate (5-MTHF)** to homocysteine → producing **methionine** and regenerating **tetrahydrofolate (THF)**
   - Critical for **folate cycle**, **DNA synthesis**, and **methylation reactions**

2. **L-Methylmalonyl-CoA Mutase** — uses **5′-deoxyadenosylcobalamin** as cofactor
   - Converts L-methylmalonyl-CoA → succinyl-CoA
   - Essential for metabolism of **odd-chain fatty acids** and certain amino acids (valine, isoleucine, methionine, threonine — "VIMT" amino acids)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deficiency symptoms:** **Megaloblastic (pernicious) anemia** (due to impaired DNA synthesis), **neurological deficits** (subacute combined degeneration of spinal cord — demyelination), and elevated **homocysteine** and **methylmalonic acid** — the diagnostic biochemical markers.

---

## 2.2 Microbial Biosynthesis Pathways (Engineering Perspective)

### 2.2.1 Riboflavin Biosynthetic Pathway (Purine → Riboflavin)

Riboflavin biosynthesis starts from **GTP (guanosine triphosphate)** and **ribulose-5-phosphate**, both derived from central carbon metabolism. The pathway involves **seven enzymatic steps** in the committed pathway:

| Step | Substrate | Enzyme | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GTP | GTP cyclohydrolase II | 2,5-diamino-6-ribosylamino-4(3H)-pyrimidinone 5′-phosphate (DAROPP) |
| 2 | DAROPP | Pyrimidine deaminase | 5-amino-6-ribosylamino-2,4(1H,3H)-pyrimidinedione 5′-phosphate (ArPP) |
| 3 | ArPP | Riboflavin-specific deaminase / reductase | 5-amino-6-ribitylamino-2,4(1H,3H)-pyrimidinedione 5′-phosphate (ArPP reduced) |
| 4 | ArPP | Lumazine synthase (LS / RibH) | 6,7-dimethyl-8-ribityllumazine (DMRL) |
| 5 | Ribulose-5-P | 3,4-dihydroxy-2-butanone-4-phosphate (DHBP) synthase (RibB) | DHBP |
| 6 | DMRL + DHBP | Riboflavin synthase (RibE / RS) | Riboflavin + ArPP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Target:** In *Ashbya gossypii* and *Bacillus subtilis*, industrial strains are mutated or engineered to:
> - **Overexpress** riboflavin pathway enzymes (RibA, RibB, RibH, RibE, RibD)
> - **Derepress** pathway regulation (purine catabolism repression is removed)
> - **Knock out** competing pathways (e.g., flavin repressor *ribC* or *ribR*)
> - **Increase** precursor supply (GTP and ribulose-5-P pools)

### 2.2.2 Vitamin B12 Biosynthetic Pathway (Aerobic vs. Anaerobic)

Vitamin B12 is one of the most **structurally complex** and **biosynthetically expensive** natural products — requiring approximately **30 enzymatic steps** for complete *de novo* assembly, plus additional steps for the unique **ring contraction** step that converts porphyrinogen III to **cobalt-precorrin**.

**Universal early steps (Uroporphyrinogen III → Precorrin-2):**
1. Succinyl-CoA + Glycine → δ-Aminolevulinic acid (ALA) [ALA synthase]
2. 2 ALA → Porphobilinogen (PBG) [PBG synthase]
3. 4 PBG → Hydroxymethylbilane (HMB) [HMB synthase]
4. HMB → Uroporphyrinogen III [Uroporphyrinogen III synthase]
5. Uroporphyrinogen III → Precorrin-2 (via S-adenosylmethionine methylations)

**Pathways then diverge:**

| Feature | **Aerobic Pathway** (*Pseudomonas denitrificans*, *Rhodobacter*) | **Anaerobic Pathway** (*Propionibacterium*, *Salmonella*) |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen requirement | Strictly aerobic | Strictly anaerobic (early steps) |
| Ring contraction | Late stage (after Co insertion) | Early stage (before Co insertion) |
| Cobalt insertion | Early (in precorrin stage) | Late |
| Methylation timing | Uses SAM, post-corrin | Uses SAM, post-corrin |
| Final cofactor form | Adenosylcobalamin (coenzyme B12) predominantly | Adenosylcobalamin predominantly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Implication:** The aerobic pathway in *P. denitrificans* is preferred industrially because:
> - Higher volumetric productivity
> - Easier aeration control
> - Better tolerance of high glucose
> - **Betaine** (trimethylglycine) is required as an osmoprotectant and methyl donor source — added to fermentation media

---

## 2.3 Fermentation Process Design

### 2.3.1 Riboflavin Fermentation — *Ashbya gossypii* (Gold Standard)

| Parameter | Value / Specification |
|---|---|
| **Organism** | *Ashbya gossypii* (genetically improved strain, e.g., ATCC 10895) |
| **Inoculum age** | 24–48 h vegetative mycelium |
| **Carbon source** | Glucose, sucrose, or vegetable oil (e.g., rapeseed oil 1–3%) |
| **Nitrogen source** | Corn-steep liquor, soy flour, yeast extract, urea |
| **pH** | 6.5 – 7.5 (controlled with ammonia/NaOH) |
| **Temperature** | 28 – 32°C (mesophilic) |
| **Aeration** | High OTR — 0.5–1.0 vvm; dissolved O₂ > 30% saturation |
| **Fermentation type** | Submerged aerobic batch / fed-batch |
| **Cycle time** | 5 – 8 days |
| **Yield** | 15 – 25 g/L (industrial) |
| **Titer** | Up to 40 g/L in optimized fed-batch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Morphological Note:** *A. gossypii* grows as **multinucleate hyphae** producing **sporangia** at maturity. Riboflavin is typically **excreted into the broth**, simplifying recovery. Yellow color of the broth is a direct visual indicator of riboflavin accumulation.

### 2.3.2 Vitamin B12 Fermentation — *Pseudomonas denitrificans* (Industrial Leader)

| Parameter | Value / Specification |
|---|---|
| **Organism** | *Pseudomonas denitrificans* (improved strain, e.g., DSM 1248) |
| **Inoculum age** | 18 – 24 h |
| **Carbon source** | Sucrose, glucose, or beet molasses (2 – 5%) |
| **Nitrogen source** | Corn-steep liquor, yeast extract, ammonia |
| **Special additives** | **Cobalt chloride (CoCl₂)** 10–100 mg/L, **betaine** 1–5 g/L, **5,6-dimethylbenzimidazole (DMBI)** precursor, **choline** |
| **pH** | 6.5 – 7.2 |
| **Temperature** | 28 – 30°C |
| **Aeration** | Strictly aerobic, 1 vvm, dissolved O₂ > 40% |
| **Fermentation type** | Fed-batch (preferred) for high-cell-density |
| **Cycle time** | 3 – 7 days |
| **Yield** | 100 – 300 mg/L intracellular B12 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Downstream Note:** In *P. denitrificans* (and most bacteria), Vitamin B12 is **predominantly intracellular** (85–95%), making cell disruption (thermal, chemical, or mechanical) a mandatory step before extraction — a major cost driver.

### 2.3.3 Vitamin B12 — *Propionibacterium shermanii* (Two-Stage Process)
- **Stage 1 (Anaerobic, 2–3 days):** Cells grow and produce porphyrinoid intermediates; high cell mass accumulation.
- **Stage 2 (Aerobic, 2–3 days):** Aerobic conditions activate **corrin ring methylation and cobalt insertion**, dramatically boosting B12 titer (often called the "switching" step).
- **Net advantage:** Higher final titer in some strains; **disadvantage:** complex two-stage process control.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Engineering Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are essential for **yield, productivity, and mass balance** calculations in KTU exams. Master these for full marks.

### 2.4.1 Fermentation Performance Metrics

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{P/S} &= \frac{\Delta C_P}{\Delta C_S} \quad \text{[g product / g substrate]} \\
Y_{X/S} &= \frac{\Delta C_X}{\Delta C_S} \quad \text{[g biomass / g substrate]} \\
P_v    &= \frac{C_{P,\text{final}}}{t_f} \quad \text{[g/L/h]} \\
Q_{O_2} &= k_L a \cdot (C^{*}_{O_2} - C_{O_2}) \quad \text{[mol/L/h]} \\
OUR    &= \frac{\mu}{Y_{X/O_2}} \quad \text{[mol O}_2\text{/L/h]} \\
OUR    &= q_{O_2} \cdot C_X \quad \text{(oxygen uptake rate)} \\
\text{RQ}    &= \frac{\text{CER}}{\text{OUR}} = \frac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{O}_2 \text{ consumed}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $Y_{P/S}$ = Product yield on substrate
- $Y_{X/S}$ = Biomass yield on substrate
- $P_v$ = Volumetric productivity
- $C_P$ = Product concentration
- $C_S$ = Substrate concentration
- $C_X$ = Biomass concentration
- $q_{O_2}$ = Specific oxygen uptake rate
- $k_L a$ = Volumetric mass transfer coefficient
- $C^{*}_{O_2}$ = Saturation dissolved oxygen
- RQ = Respiratory quotient (for Riboflavin fermentation, RQ ≈ 1.0; for B12 ≈ 0.9–1.0)

### 2.4.2 B12 Specific Yield Calculations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{extraction}} &= \frac{m_{\text{B12,recovered}}}{m_{\text{B12,fermented}}} \times 100\% \\
\text{Specific Productivity } (q_P) &= \frac{C_P - C_{P,0}}{C_X \cdot (t_f - t_0)} \quad \text{[mg/g biomass/h]} \\
\text{Cobalt utilization factor } (\eta_{Co}) &= \frac{m_{\text{B12,produced}}}{m_{\text{Co,added}}} \quad \text{[mg B12 / mg Co]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4.3 Downstream Recovery Equations (Cyanocobalamin)

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_D &= \frac{C_{\text{organic}}}{C_{\text{aq}}} \quad \text{(partition coefficient in solvent extraction)} \\
R   &= 1 - \left(\frac{V_{\text{aq}}}{V_{\text{aq}} + K_D V_{\text{org}}}\right)^n \quad \text{(fractional recovery, multi-stage)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4.4 Quick-Reference Specifications Table

| Vitamin | Best Producer | Process Type | Titer (g/L or mg/L) | Cycle (days) | In-cell or Secreted? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B2** | *A. gossypii* | Aerobic fed-batch | 15–25 g/L | 5–8 | Mostly **secreted** |
| **B2** | *B. subtilis* (rec.) | Aerobic batch | 10–15 g/L | 3–4 | Mostly **secreted** |
| **B2** | *C. famata* (rec.) | Aerobic batch | 5–10 g/L | 4–6 | Mostly **secreted** |
| **B12** | *P. denitrificans* | Aerobic fed-batch | 100–300 mg/L | 3–7 | Mostly **intracellular** |
| **B12** | *P. shermanii* | Anaerobic + aerobic | 40–100 mg/L | 5–7 | Mostly **intracellular** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe-Symbol Reminder (Kerala Examiners):** In all written equations, use **\vert x \vert** or **\mid x \mid** to denote absolute value — **never** the bare $\vert$ character, as it can interfere with exam-answer scanning or grader markup.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Volumetric Productivity of Riboflavin Fermentation

> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> An *Ashbya gossypii* fermentation in a 50,000 L bioreactor produces **riboflavin** with a final concentration of **18.5 g/L** after **150 hours** of fermentation. The initial riboflavin concentration is negligible. The initial glucose concentration is 80 g/L, and the residual glucose at harvest is 12 g/L.
> Calculate:
> (a) The product yield coefficient $Y_{P/S}$
> (b) The volumetric productivity $P_v$
> (c) The total mass of riboflavin produced in one batch

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $V$ = 50,000 L
- $C_{P,\text{final}}$ = 18.5 g/L
- $C_{P,0}$ ≈ 0 g/L
- $t_f$ = 150 h
- $C_{S,0}$ = 80 g/L
- $C_{S,\text{final}}$ = 12 g/L

#### (a) Product Yield Coefficient $Y_{P/S}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{P/S} &= \frac{\Delta C_P}{\Delta C_S} \\
\Delta C_P &= C_{P,\text{final}} - C_{P,0} = 18.5 - 0 = 18.5 \;\text{g/L} \\
\Delta C_S &= C_{S,0} - C_{S,\text{final}} = 80 - 12 = 68 \;\text{g/L} \\
Y_{P/S} &= \frac{18.5 \;\text{g/L}}{68 \;\text{g/L}} \\
        &= 0.2721 \;\text{g product / g substrate}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every gram of glucose consumed, the culture produces 0.2721 g of riboflavin — a high yield reflecting the strong redirection of carbon flux in overproducing strains.

#### (b) Volumetric Productivity $P_v$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_v &= \frac{C_{P,\text{final}}}{t_f} \\
    &= \frac{18.5 \;\text{g/L}}{150 \;\text{h}} \\
    &= 0.1233 \;\text{g/L/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

In more intuitive units:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_v &= 0.1233 \;\text{g/L/h} \times 24 \;\text{h/day} \\
    &= 2.96 \;\text{g/L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (c) Total Mass of Riboflavin Produced

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{P,\text{total}} &= C_{P,\text{final}} \times V \\
                   &= 18.5 \;\text{g/L} \times 50{,}000 \;\text{L} \\
                   &= 925{,}000 \;\text{g} \\
                   &= 925 \;\text{kg per batch}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- (a) $Y_{P/S}$ ≈ **0.272 g/g**
- (b) $P_v$ ≈ **0.123 g/L/h** (or 2.96 g/L/day)
- (c) Total mass = **925 kg riboflavin per batch**

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Vitamin B12 Mass Balance & Cobalt Utilization

> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> A 20,000 L fermentation of *Pseudomonas denitrificans* yields a final cell dry weight (CDW) of **35 g/L** and an intracellular B12 content of **8.5 mg per g of dry cells**. The fermentation received **80 kg of cobalt chloride (CoCl₂)** as the cobalt source. Calculate:
> (a) Total B12 produced in the batch (in kg)
> (b) Volumetric productivity, given $t_f$ = 120 h
> (c) Cobalt utilization factor $\eta_{Co}$

**Atomic mass data:** Co = 58.93 g/mol; CoCl₂ = 129.84 g/mol; Co fraction in CoCl₂ = 58.93/129.84 = 0.4538

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### (a) Total B12 in Batch

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specific B12 content} &= 8.5 \;\text{mg/g CDW} = 8.5 \;\text{mg/g} \\
\text{Total CDW in batch} &= 35 \;\text{g/L} \times 20{,}000 \;\text{L} = 700{,}000 \;\text{g} = 700 \;\text{kg} \\
\text{Total B12} &= 8.5 \;\text{mg/g} \times 700{,}000 \;\text{g} \\
                  &= 5{,}950{,}000 \;\text{mg} \\
                  &= 5950 \;\text{g} = 5.95 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Volumetric Productivity

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{P,\text{final}} &= \frac{m_{\text{B12}}}{V} = \frac{5950 \;\text{g}}{20{,}000 \;\text{L}} = 0.2975 \;\text{g/L} = 297.5 \;\text{mg/L} \\
P_v &= \frac{C_{P,\text{final}}}{t_f} = \frac{297.5 \;\text{mg/L}}{120 \;\text{h}} \\
    &= 2.48 \;\text{mg/L/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (c) Cobalt Utilization Factor $\eta_{Co}$

First, calculate cobalt content in 80 kg CoCl₂:
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{Co} &= 80 \;\text{kg} \times 0.4538 = 36.30 \;\text{kg} = 36{,}300 \;\text{g} \\
\eta_{Co} &= \frac{m_{\text{B12,produced}}}{m_{Co,\text{added}}} \\
          &= \frac{5950 \;\text{g}}{36{,}300 \;\text{g}} \\
          &= 0.164 \;\text{g B12 / g Co}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- (a) Total B12 = **5.95 kg**
- (b) $P_v$ = **2.48 mg/L/h** (which is typical for industrial B12)
- (c) $\eta_{Co}$ = **0.164 g/g**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** The theoretical $\eta_{Co}$ max is 1 mole B12 per 1 mole Co = (1355.37 g/mol B12) / (58.93 g/mol Co) = **23.0 g B12 per g Co**. Our actual 0.164 g/g is far below this — confirming the value of further strain improvement to enhance cobalt incorporation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Downstream Recovery Loss Analysis

> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> After fermentation, the **intracellular B12** is released by thermal lysis. The lysate (volume 18,000 L) contains **280 mg/L** B12. The solvent extraction uses 4,000 L of benzyl alcohol with a partition coefficient $K_D$ = 8.5. After extraction, the aqueous phase retains 4,200 L (some lost to entrained organic). What is the recovery percentage from a **single-stage** extraction?

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 1 - \left(\frac{V_{\text{aq}}}{V_{\text{aq}} + K_D V_{\text{org}}}\right)^n \\
\text{with } n &= 1, \; V_{\text{aq}} = 4{,}200 \;\text{L}, \; V_{\text{org}} = 4{,}000 \;\text{L}, \; K_D = 8.5 \\
R &= 1 - \left(\frac{4{,}200}{4{,}200 + 8.5 \times 4{,}000}\right)^1 \\
  &= 1 - \left(\frac{4{,}200}{4{,}200 + 34{,}000}\right) \\
  &= 1 - \left(\frac{4{,}200}{38{,}200}\right) \\
  &= 1 - 0.1099 \\
  &= 0.8901 = 89.01\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Single-stage recovery = **~89%**. For >95% recovery, two or three counter-current stages would be needed industrially.

---

| Industrial revenue (approx) | ~$20–40/kg | **~$20,000–40,000/kg** |
| Production process | Mostly direct fermentation | Mostly fermentation + extraction |
| DSM/BASF/Cap production scale | ~5,000 tonnes/year | ~30–50 tonnes/year |
