# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Set B]
*List any four properties that make an enzyme suitable for industrial application.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **High catalytic activity** — required in small quantities per unit substrate processed.
2. **Stability** at operational pH, temperature, and in the presence of solvents/oxidants.
3. **Substrate specificity** — minimizes side reactions and by-product formation.
4. **Non-toxicity and GRAS status** — safe for use in food and pharmaceutical sectors.
*(Acceptable additional points: long shelf-life, broad substrate range, ease of recovery/reusability.)*

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary Exam]
*Differentiate between free enzymes and immobilized enzymes in industrial use.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Free Enzyme | Immobilized Enzyme |
|---|---|---|
| **Reusability** | Single use; lost in product | Multiple cycles; easily separated |
| **Product contamination** | Possible (residual protein) | Negligible (enzyme retained) |
| **Reactor type** | Batch (mostly) | Batch, CSTR, or continuous PBR |
| **Stability** | Lower; susceptible to denaturation | Higher; carrier confers protection |
| **Cost per kg product** | High | Low after amortization |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question 3 (Choice A)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **industrial production of High Fructose Corn Syrup (HFCS)** using microbial enzymes. Enumerate the enzymes, their sources, and the biochemical reactions involved. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain **enzyme immobilization** techniques. Compare covalent binding with entrapment, citing industrial examples. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) HFCS Production (7 marks)**

HFCS is a liquid sweetener made by enzymatically converting corn starch into fructose-rich syrup. The process is one of the **largest industrial enzyme applications** in the world, consuming thousands of tonnes of enzyme annually.

**Step 1 — Liquefaction (7 marks collectively):**
- Substrate: Corn starch slurry (30–35% w/w).
- Enzyme: **Bacterial $\alpha$-amylase** (EC 3.2.1.1) from *Bacillus licheniformis*, stable at **95–105 °C** and pH 6.0–6.5.
- Action: Hydrolyzes $\alpha$-1,4-glycosidic bonds randomly, reducing viscosity and producing maltodextrins (DE 8–12).

**Step 2 — Saccharification:**
- Enzyme: **Glucoamylase** (EC 3.2.1.3) from *Aspergillus niger*.
- Action: Hydrolyzes both $\alpha$-1,4 and $\alpha$-1,6 bonds, releasing free glucose (DE > 95).
- Conditions: 60 °C, pH 4.0–4.5, 48–72 h.

**Step 3 — Isomerization:**
- Enzyme: **Glucose isomerase** (EC 5.3.1.5), commonly from *Streptomyces murinus* or engineered *Actinoplanes missouriensis*.
- Action: Converts D-glucose to D-fructose via an aldose–ketose isomerization.
- Conditions: 55–60 °C, pH 7.5–8.0, presence of **Mg²⁺** (activator) and **Co²⁺** (stabilizer).
- Reactor: **Immobilized enzyme packed-bed column** operating continuously for **100–200 days** before catalyst replacement.
- Product: HFCS-42 (42% fructose) or further enriched to HFCS-55 via chromatographic separation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why enzymes instead of acid hydrolysis?**
> Acid hydrolysis requires high T (140 °C) and corrodes equipment, produces off-flavors (e.g., hydroxyl-methyl-furfural), and is non-specific. Enzymes operate at milder T (60 °C), give higher yields, and generate a cleaner, purer product.

**(b) Enzyme Immobilization Techniques (7 marks)**

Immobilization refers to confining an enzyme within or on a solid support, allowing it to retain its catalytic activity and be reused continuously.

**Major Techniques:**

1. **Adsorption:** Physical/ionic binding of enzyme onto a carrier (e.g., activated carbon, ion-exchange resins, cellulose). Simple and inexpensive but the enzyme may leach.
2. **Covalent Binding:** Enzyme is covalently linked to functional groups (e.g., –NH₂, –COOH) on carriers such as **Eupergit® C** (epoxy-activated polyacrylamide beads), CNBr-activated Sepharose, or glutaraldehyde-cross-linked supports. **Example:** Penicillin G acylase immobilized on Eupergit® for 6-APA production.
3. **Entrapment:** Enzyme is trapped within a porous polymer matrix (e.g., calcium alginate beads, polyacrylamide gel, sol-gel silica). Substrates/products diffuse in and out. **Example:** $\alpha$-Amylase entrapped in alginate beads for starch hydrolysis.
4. **Cross-Linking (CLEAs / CLEs):** Carrier-free method using bifunctional reagents like glutaraldehyde to cross-link enzyme molecules into insoluble aggregates. **Example:** CLEAs of lipases for biodiesel synthesis.
5. **Encapsulation:** Enzyme enclosed within semi-permeable membranes or liposomes.

**Comparison: Covalent vs. Entrapment**

| Feature | Covalent Binding | Entrapment |
|---|---|---|
| **Bond strength** | Strong, often irreversible | Mechanical confinement only |
| **Leakage** | Negligible | Possible at high shear |
| **Thermal stability** | Enhanced | Slightly enhanced |
| **Reusability** | Excellent (months) | Good (weeks) |
| **Industrial example** | Glucose isomerase on DEAE-cellulose | Invertase in alginate beads |
| **Cost** | Higher (special carriers) | Lower (cheap polymers) |

---

### **Question 3 (Choice B — Alternative)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Exam]

**(a)** Describe the **application of enzymes in the detergent industry**. Explain the role of alkaline proteases, lipases, and cellulases. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **application of enzymes in the pharmaceutical industry** with reference to penicillin acylase, asparaginase, and lipase-catalyzed chiral synthesis. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Detergent Industry (7 marks)**

Enzymes in detergents constitute approximately **30% of the global industrial enzyme market**. They enable **low-temperature washing** (30–60 °C), reduce energy and water consumption, and replace harsh chemical additives.

**Key Enzymes:**

1. **Alkaline Proteases (e.g., Subtilisin from *Bacillus lentus*):**
   - **Function:** Hydrolyze protein-based stains (blood, milk, grass, sweat, egg) into soluble peptides.
   - **Industrial challenge:** Bleach (sodium perborate) oxidizes the active-site methionine; **protein engineering** (Met222 → Ser, Ala) produces bleach-resistant variants (e.g., **Durazym®, Savinase®**).

2. **Lipases (e.g., from *Thermomyces lanuginosus*):**
   - **Function:** Hydrolyze triglyceride-based stains (oils, butter, sauces, cosmetics) into fatty acids + glycerol.
   - **Industrial example:** **Lipolase®** (Novozymes) — the first recombinant lipase in laundry detergents, produced in *Aspergillus oryzae*.

3. **Cellulases:**
   - **Function:** Remove micro-fibrils of damaged cotton from fabric surface, restoring color brightness and softness.
   - **Mode of action:** Endoglucanases attack amorphous regions, releasing particulate dirt.

4. **Amylases:**
   - **Function:** Remove starch-based food stains (chocolate, gravy, baby food).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiners Love This Fact:**
> A modern compact detergent powder may contain **four or more enzymes** in microencapsulated forms to prevent dust generation and ensure shelf stability.

**(b) Pharmaceutical Industry (7 marks)**

**1. Penicillin G Acylase (PGA, EC 3.5.1.11):**
- Used to cleave the **phenylacetyl side chain** of penicillin G, producing **6-Aminopenicillanic Acid (6-APA)**, the key intermediate for semi-synthetic penicillins.
- Reaction: Penicillin G + H₂O $\xrightarrow{\text{PGA}}$ 6-APA + Phenylacetic acid.
- Industrial strains: *Escherichia coli* (recombinant), *Arthrobacter viscosus*.
- Process: **Immobilized PGA** in packed-bed reactors at 30–37 °C, pH 7.5–8.0. Productivity: >500 kg 6-APA per kg enzyme.
- Downstream products: **Ampicillin, Amoxicillin, Cephalexin**.

**2. Asparaginase (EC 3.5.1.1):**
- Enzyme from *E. coli* or *Erwinia chrysanthemi*.
- Therapeutic use: Hydrolyzes L-asparagine (an amino acid essential for leukemic cell growth) into aspartate + NH₃, starving **Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL)** cells.
- Marketed as **Elspar®** and **Oncaspar®** (PEGylated form for longer half-life).

**3. Lipase-Catalyzed Chiral Synthesis:**
- Lipases (e.g., *Candida antarctica* lipase B — **CALB / Novozym 435**) catalyze enantioselective esterification/transesterification.
- **Industrial relevance:** Production of single-enantiomer drugs (e.g., **(S)-ibuprofen**, **(R)-naproxen**) — chirally pure drugs are safer and more effective than racemates.
- Chiral alcohols and amines resolved at scales of **multi-tonne per year**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus KTU Point:** Enzyme-based drug synthesis is a major thrust of the **"white biotechnology"** revolution — replacing stoichiometric, waste-generating chemical routes with catalytic, atom-economical, and environmentally benign processes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing amylase with glucoamylase:** Amylase gives maltodextrins (random hydrolysis); glucoamylase gives free glucose (sequential release from non-reducing end). Many students mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting cofactor requirements:** Glucose isomerase needs Mg²⁺; some dehydrogenases need NAD⁺/NADPH. Examiners love to test this.
> 3. **Missing operational conditions:** Always quote **temperature, pH, and reactor type** in enzyme-based process questions.
> 4. **Naming an enzyme without its source organism:** Full credit requires both the enzyme and the producing strain (e.g., "*Aspergillus niger* glucoamylase", not just "glucoamylase").
> 5. **Not mentioning immobilization advantages:** For any continuous-process question, mention reusability, reduced downstream processing, and ease of product separation.
> 6. **Writing penicillin *amidase* instead of penicillin *acylase*:** The enzyme is officially EC 3.5.1.11 — Penicillin G acylase. Examiners may deduct a mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Industrial enzymes** = biocatalysts produced at scale for use in commercial processes.
- ✅ **Microbial sources** (*Aspergillus*, *Bacillus*, *Trichoderma*, *Streptomyces*) dominate over plant/animal.
- ✅ **GRAS status**, **stability**, and **specificity** are the three most-cited ideal properties.
- ✅ **Hydrolases** (amylases, proteases, lipases) form the largest industrial enzyme class.
- ✅ **HFCS production** uses a 3-enzyme cascade: $\alpha$-amylase → glucoamylase → glucose isomerase.
- ✅ **Detergent enzymes** (subtilisin, lipase, cellulase, amylase) enable low-temperature, energy-efficient washing.
- ✅ **Penicillin acylase** produces 6-APA, the gateway to semi-synthetic penicillins (ampicillin, amoxicillin).
- ✅ **Asparaginase** is an anticancer enzyme for treating ALL.
- ✅ **Immobilization** methods: adsorption, covalent, entrapment, cross-linking (CLEAs), encapsulation.
- ✅ **Covalent binding** is most stable; **entrapment** is cheapest.
- ✅ **Michaelis–Menten equation** $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ is the fundamental kinetic model.
- ✅ **Turnover number ($k_{cat}$)** and **catalytic efficiency ($k_{cat}/K_m$)** quantify enzyme performance.
- ✅ **Enzyme deactivation** is first-order: $U_t = U_0 e^{-k_d t}$; half-life $= 0.693/k_d$.
- ✅ **Glucose isomerase half-life** of 100–200 days in HFCS columns makes industrial economics viable.
- ✅ **Engineered subtilisin** (Met222→Ser/Ala) is bleach-resistant — a classic example of industrial enzyme engineering.
- ✅ **White biotechnology** = industrial application of enzymes/microbes for sustainable manufacturing.
- ✅ Always write **enzyme name + EC number + source organism + substrate + product + reaction conditions** in exam answers for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of Recombinant Proteins: Manufacture of Human Insulin, Hepatitis B Vaccine, Monoclonal Antibody
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBTT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Production of Recombinant Proteins: Manufacture of Human Insulin, Hepatitis B Vaccine, Monoclonal Antibody

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Recombinant Proteins?

A **recombinant protein** is a protein produced by an organism (host) into which a gene encoding the protein of interest has been inserted using **recombinant DNA (rDNA) technology**. The host is then cultured at industrial scale using bioprocess engineering principles to manufacture the heterologous protein in large, purified quantities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBBTT304 – Module 3):**
> "Recombinant proteins are engineered proteins expressed in heterologous host systems (bacterial, yeast, mammalian, or plant cells) by inserting a cloned gene downstream of a suitable promoter. Industrial bioprocess technology is concerned with translating bench-scale rDNA constructs into cGMP-compliant, scalable, cost-effective manufacturing processes."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Foreign Recipe in a Local Kitchen"

Imagine you want a cake (the **protein of interest**) but only have a foreign-language recipe book (the **human gene**). You:
1. **Translate the recipe** (clone the gene)
2. **Photocopy the page** (insert into a vector/plasmid)
3. **Hand the page to a local baker** (transform into a microbial/mammalian host)
4. **Bake in the baker's oven** (fermentation/cell culture)
5. **Filter and decorate the cake** (downstream processing & purification)

The baker doesn't naturally make that cake — but with the recipe, they can. This is precisely the logic of recombinant protein production.

## 1.3 The Three Case-Study Products at a Glance

| Product | Class | Typical Host | Year of First Approval | Therapeutic Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Human Insulin (Humulin)** | Hormone (51 aa, 2 chains) | *E. coli* / *S. cerevisiae* | 1982 (Lilly) | Type I & II Diabetes mellitus |
| **Hepatitis B Vaccine (HBsAg)** | Subunit vaccine (viral surface antigen) | *S. cerevisiae* (Merck) / *P. pastoris* | 1986 (Recombivax) | Prophylaxis against HBV infection |
| **Monoclonal Antibodies (mAbs)** | Immunoglobulin (IgG, ~150 kDa) | CHO / NS0 / SP2/0 hybridoma | 1986 (Orthoclone OKT3) | Cancer, autoimmune, infectious disease |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why These Three?**
> These three are KTU "high-yield" examples because they span three different bioprocess paradigms:
> - **Insulin** → Bacterial intracellular expression with refolding
> - **HBV vaccine** → Yeast intracellular expression with self-assembly into VLPs
> - **mAb** → Mammalian cell secretion with complex N-glycosylation
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Universal Bioprocess Architecture for Recombinant Proteins

Every recombinant protein manufacturing process — regardless of the product — follows the canonical **USP (Upstream Processing) → DSP (Downstream Processing) → Formulation** chain:

### Upstream Processing (USP)
- **Strain/Cell Line Development:** Cloning, transformation/transfection, selection, banking (MCB → WCB).
- **Inoculum Expansion:** Shake flask → small bioreactor → production bioreactor.
- **Production Bioreactor:** Operated in batch, fed-batch (most common for mAb/insulin), or perfusion mode.
- **Critical Process Parameters (CPP):** pH (~7.0), DO (~30–60% saturation), temperature (30–37 °C), agitation (RPM), feed composition, induction timing (for inducible promoters like *lac* or *AOX1*).

### Downstream Processing (DSP)
- **Primary Recovery:** Centrifugation, microfiltration, depth filtration, cell disruption (for intracellular products).
- **Purification:** Chromatography — affinity (Protein A for mAb), ion-exchange (IEX), hydrophobic interaction (HIC), mixed-mode, size-exclusion (SEC).
- **Polishing & Formulation:** Virus inactivation (solvent/detergent, low pH), viral filtration, UF/DF, final fill-finish.

### Formulation & Fill-Finish
- Stabilization with excipients (trehalose, sucrose, polysorbate-80), sterile filtration (0.22 µm), lyophilization if needed.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic:** **"C-C-C-D-F"** — Clone → Cultivate → Capture → (Chromatograph) → Formulate.

## 2.2 The Three Host Systems — Comparative Matrix

| Feature | *E. coli* | *S. cerevisiae* | CHO (Mammalian) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Growth rate** | ~20 min doubling | ~90 min doubling | ~24 h doubling |
| **Expression level** | 1–5 g/L (often IBs) | 0.1–1 g/L | 0.5–5 g/L (fed-batch) |
| **Post-translational mods** | None (no glycosylation) | High-mannose N-glycans | Human-like (sialylated) |
| **Secretion** | Periplasm or cytoplasm | Secreted | Secreted |
| **Product folding** | Often misfolded → inclusion bodies | Mostly correct | Correct |
| **Cost** | **Low** | Moderate | **High** |
| **Typical product** | Insulin, IFN-α, GH | HBV vaccine, Insulin | Monoclonal antibodies, EPO, Factor VIII |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb (KTU favourite):** "If the protein requires complex human-like glycosylation, you must use a mammalian host. If it does not, a microbial host is faster and cheaper."

## 2.3 Key Equations and Metrics (KTU Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not memorise these formulae mechanically; understand the engineering trade-off each one represents.**

| Symbol | Definition | Formula / Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q_p$ | Volumetric productivity | $Q_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{final}} \cdot V}{t \cdot V} = \dfrac{P}{t}$ [mg L⁻¹ h⁻¹] | Drives plant capacity & COGS |
| $Y_{P/X}$ | Product yield on biomass | $Y_{P/X} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta X}$ [mg g⁻¹] | Strain robustness indicator |
| $Y_{P/S}$ | Product yield on substrate | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ [g g⁻¹] | Raw-material efficiency |
| $\mu$ | Specific growth rate | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ [h⁻¹] | Affects recombinant instability |
| $\sigma$ | Plasmid/insert loss rate | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{Frac^+_{t}}{Frac^+_{0}}$ per generation | Non-segregational instability |
| IVCD | Integral viable cell density | $IVCD = \int X_v \, dt$ [cells·h·mL⁻¹] | Predicts mAb titre & lactate |
| Specific productivity | $q_P$ | $q_P = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ [pg cell⁻¹ day⁻¹] | Cell-line intrinsic capacity |
| Recovery Yield | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{purified}}}{P_{\text{clarified}}} \times 100\%$ [%] | DSP efficiency benchmark |
| Mass Balance | — | $P_{\text{USP}} \cdot \eta_{\text{overall}} = P_{\text{drug\,substance}}$ | Process-economics lever |
| Diafiltration Washout | — | $N = \ln\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_f}\right)$ DV | Buffer exchange volume |
| Resin Capacity | — | $Q_{max} = \dfrac{m_{P,\,bound}}{V_{resin}}$ [g L⁻¹] | DSP column sizing |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often award marks for *naming* the right parameter even if the numerical answer is approximate. Always state the unit.

## 2.4 Why Recombinant? — Problems with the "Old" Methods

| Product | Pre-recombinant Source | Critical Limitation | Recombinant Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Insulin | Bovine/porcine pancreas | 3-amino-acid difference → immunogenicity; ~50 kg pancreas per patient-year | Authentic human sequence, unlimited supply |
| HBV Vaccine | Chronic carrier plasma | Risk of HIV/HBV contamination; plasma supply scarce | Pure HBsAg, no human pathogens |
| mAb | Polyclonal antisera (mice/rabbits) | Batch-to-batch variability, non-specific, finite supply | Single specificity, renewable cell line |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Process Flows for Each Product

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is **exhaustive** by design. KTU ESE questions on these products frequently ask for the full process flow chart with the role of each step. Memorize the *order* and the *engineering rationale* of each step.

## 3.1 Manufacture of Recombinant Human Insulin (Humulin – Eli Lilly, 1982)

### 3.1.1 Pre-Recombinant Context

Insulin is a 51-amino-acid heterodimer of an **A-chain (21 aa, acidic)** and a **B-chain (30 aa, basic)** connected by two inter-chain and one intra-chain disulfide bond. Pre-1982, insulin was extracted from bovine/porcine pancreas using acid–ethanol extraction, crystallization, and chromatographic purification. Porcine insulin differs from human at one position (B30: Ala → Thr); bovine at two (A8: Thr→Ala, A10: Ile→Val). These differences caused **anti-insulin antibody formation** in patients.

### 3.1.2 The Two Classical rDNA Strategies

**Strategy A — Separate-Chain Approach (Genentech, 1982; Lilly)**
1. **Synthesize the A-chain gene and B-chain gene** (51 and 90 bp) using solid-phase phosphoramidite chemistry. (Insulin is too small to need cDNA; total chemical synthesis is practical.)
2. **Clone each gene separately** into plasmid pBR322 downstream of the *lac* promoter (e.g., pBR322-λpL or trp promoter). The peptide is expressed as a **fusion with β-galactosidase** (to protect from proteolysis and to be detected by antibody).
3. **Transform *E. coli* K12** separately for each chain. Ferment in complex medium (glucose + casamino acids) under **IPTG induction at mid-log phase**.
4. **Harvest cells, disrupt** (lysozyme + sonication), isolate **inclusion bodies** by centrifugation. Inclusion bodies protect the fusion protein from proteases.
5. **Solubilize IBs** in 8 M urea + β-mercaptoethanol (reduces disulfides, denatures).
6. **CNBr cleavage** at Met residue engineered between β-gal and insulin chain — releases the free insulin chain.
7. **Purify each chain** by RP-HPLC or ion-exchange.
8. **Controlled air-oxidation (glutathione redox pair)** in the presence of A-chain, B-chain at defined molar ratio → **correct disulfide pairing** → active human insulin.
9. **Crystallize as zinc-insulin hexamers** (final drug substance).

**Strategy B — Proinsulin Approach (Lilly's Humulin)**
Because refolding two chains separately is inefficient, Lilly developed the **proinsulin route**:
1. Clone the entire **proinsulin cDNA** (encoding the natural A-chain – C-peptide – B-chain precursor) into *E. coli* as a fusion protein.
2. Express, recover IBs, solubilize, cleave the fusion tag.
3. **Fold** the proinsulin precursor in vitro — the internal C-peptide holds A and B chains in correct spatial orientation, allowing **spontaneous, correct disulfide bond formation** (3 disulfides).
4. **Enzymatic excision of C-peptide** using trypsin + carboxypeptidase B (mimics in vivo processing).
5. The mature insulin is purified, crystallized with Zn²⁺.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If asked "Why use proinsulin rather than A+B chains?" — answer: *Higher refolding yield because the C-peptide acts as a covalent template that geometrically constrains A and B chains, allowing statistically favourable correct disulfide pairing.*

### 3.1.3 Modern Industrial Host — *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (Novo Nordisk, 1987)

To avoid endotoxin from *E. coli* and improve folding:
- Clone preproinsulin gene into the yeast **2µ plasmid** under the **ADH1** or **GAL1** promoter.
- Insulin is secreted as a **prepro-leader + proinsulin** → signal peptide is cleaved in the ER → proinsulin folds in the ER with correct disulfides → secreted to periplasm.
- Recovery from medium (rather than cells) → simpler DSP, no endotoxin.
- Transpeptidation / transpeptidase is used in some Novo processes (mini-proinsulin → mature insulin).

### 3.1.4 Downstream Purification of Insulin

| Step | Technique | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugation | Cell removal (microbial) |
| 2 | Isoelectric precipitation | Bulk removal of impurities |
| 3 | Reverse-phase HPLC / IEX | Intermediate purification |
| 4 | Crystallization (Zn²⁺) | Polishing + formulation form |
| 5 | Lyophilization | Final drug substance stability |

### 3.1.5 Insulin Analogs (Next-Generation Knowledge)

- **Lispro (Humalog):** Swap Pro-Lys at B28-B29 → faster absorption.
- **Glargine (Lantus):** Glycine substitutions shifting pI → basal insulin.
- **Aspart, Detemir, Degludec, Glulisine:** All made by rDNA in *S. cerevisiae* or *P. pastoris*.

## 3.2 Manufacture of Recombinant Hepatitis B Vaccine

### 3.2.1 Pre-Recombinant Context

The original HBV vaccine (Hilleman, Merck, 1981) was prepared from **HBsAg (22-nm subviral particles)** purified from the plasma of chronic HBV carriers. Each dose required liters of high-titre plasma, raising fears of HIV/AIDS contamination (which materialized in some early plasma-derived lots).

### 3.2.2 The HBsAg Gene

- The **HBsAg (hepatitis B surface antigen)** is encoded by the *S* gene (small surface protein, 226 aa) of HBV.
- When expressed alone in heterologous hosts, it spontaneously assembles into **22-nm spherical subviral particles** (the same shape as the natural surface antigen, but lacking the viral core) — these are the immunogenic component.

### 3.2.3 Manufacturing Process (Recombivax HB – Merck; Engerix-B – GSK)

**Step 1 — Strain Construction**
- The HBsAg S-gene is cloned into a yeast shuttle vector (e.g., pMVL-1, pBSAG).
- Promoter: **ADH1** (constitutive) or **GAL1/GAL10** (inducible).
- Selection: *LEU2* or *URA3* autotrophy markers.
- Host: ***Saccharomyces cerevisiae* strain 2150-2-3** (Merck) or **GSK**: *Hansenula polymorpha* / *Pichia pastoris*.

**Step 2 — Fermentation**
- Fed-batch in complex YPD-like medium, pH ~5.0, 30 °C, DO 30–50 %.
- Typical final cell density: OD₆₀₀ ≈ 30–50.
- HBsAg expression levels: ~0.1–1 % of total cell protein.
- *(In Pichia pastoris, methanol-inducible AOX1 promoter can yield 5–10 g/L of HBsAg.)*

**Step 3 — Cell Disruption & Primary Recovery**
- Bead mill (Dyno-Mill) or high-pressure homogenizer → >90 % cell disruption.
- Removal of nucleic acids by **polyethyleneimine (PEI) precipitation**.
- Acid precipitation (pH 4.5) of HBsAg + impurities.
- CsCl **density-gradient ultracentrifugation** was originally used; modern processes use immunoaffinity with anti-HBs mAb.

**Step 4 — Purification Sequence (KTU-favourite flowchart)**

| Step | Method | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Microfiltration | Cell debris removal |
| 2 | PEI + acid ppt. | Bulk nucleic acid & impurity removal |
| 3 | Hydroxyapatite chromatography | Removes endotoxin & host proteins |
| 4 | Gel filtration (Sepharose) | Monomer/aggregate separation |
| 5 | CsCl gradient (historical) or RP-HPLC | Polishing |
| 6 | 0.22 µm sterile filter | Sterility |
| 7 | Adsorption to Al(OH)₃ gel | Formulation as **adjuvanted vaccine** |

**Step 5 — Formulation**
- 5–40 µg HBsAg/dose
- **Aluminium hydroxide (Al(OH)₃) or aluminium phosphate** as adjuvant (0.5 mg Al³⁺/dose) → Th2-skewed, depot-effect immune response.
- Thiomersal (preservative) in multi-dose vials.
- Stored at 2–8 °C.

### 3.2.4 Quality Control (Critical)

- **Purity > 99 %** by SDS-PAGE and RP-HPLC.
- **Host-cell DNA** < 10 pg/dose (WHO limit).
- **Endotoxin** < 5 EU/kg body weight.
- **Potency:** Mouse immunogenicity assay (ED₅₀).
- **Identity:** ELISA against anti-HBs; Western blot; HBsAg-specific.
- **Glycosylation:** Yeast-derived HBsAg is **non-glycosylated**; plasma-derived is glycosylated. Yeast is preferred because the glycans did not affect efficacy and could be removed consistently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Yeast? Why not E. coli?**
> - E. coli cannot glycosylate (HBsAg's natural glycosylation is non-essential for immunogenicity, BUT …)
> - E. coli HBsAg does not assemble into the correct 22-nm particle as efficiently as yeast or mammalian cells.
> - Yeast has a long safety record, low-cost fermentation, and proven scalability.

## 3.3 Manufacture of Monoclonal Antibodies (mAb)

### 3.3.1 The Hybridoma Revolution (Köhler & Milstein, 1975 — Nobel 1984)

Monoclonal antibodies are produced by a single B-cell clone, so every antibody molecule is **identical** in sequence, specificity, and affinity.

**Classical hybridoma (mouse) process:**

1. **Immunize BALB/c mice** (3–4 doses over 4–6 weeks) with the target antigen + adjuvant (Freund's complete → incomplete).
2. **Harvest spleen** → isolate B-lymphocytes producing the desired antibody.
3. **Fuse splenocytes with myeloma cells** (P3-X63-Ag8 or SP2/0) using **polyethylene glycol (PEG 1450)** or **electrofusion**.
4. **HAT-medium selection:**
   - H (hypoxanthine), A (aminopterin), T (thymidine).
   - Aminopterin blocks the *de novo* nucleotide synthesis pathway.
   - Cells must use the **salvage pathway** (HGPRT enzyme) to synthesize nucleotides.
   - Myeloma cells lack HGPRT → die.
   - B-cells have HGPRT but cannot divide indefinitely → die.
   - **Only fused hybridomas survive** (HGPRT from B-cell + immortality from myeloma).
5. **Screening for desired specificity** — typically by **ELISA** on the target antigen. Clone positive hybridomas by **limiting dilution** (0.3 cells/well) twice to ensure monoclonality.
6. **Expand** clones → freeze master cell bank (MCB) and working cell bank (WCB).
7. **Production:** Originally *in vivo* (ascites in mice) — now largely *in vitro* in stirred-tank or hollow-fibre bioreactors using serum-free media.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ethical Note for KTU:** *In vivo* ascites production in mice is discouraged (3R principle) and replaced by **in-vitro bioreactor hybridoma culture**. KTU often asks for an ethical comparison.

### 3.3.2 The "Antibody Problem" — Human Anti-Mouse Antibody (HAMA) Response

Murine mAbs, when injected into humans, are recognized as foreign → HAMA response → rapid clearance, allergic reactions, loss of efficacy. This drove the development of "humanized" mAbs:

| Type | Composition | % Human | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Murine | 100 % mouse | 0 % | OKT3 (anti-CD3) |
| Chimeric | Mouse V regions grafted onto human constant regions | ~65 % | Rituximab (Rituxan) |
| Humanized (CDR-grafted) | Only mouse CDRs grafted into human framework | ~95 % | Trastuzumab (Herceptin) |
| Fully human | From phage-display libraries or humanized mice (XenoMouse) | ~100 % | Adalimumab (Humira) |

### 3.3.3 Industrial Production of mAbs (CHO Cell Fed-Batch)

The dominant industrial host is **CHO (Chinese Hamster Ovary)** cells, either via:
- Stable transfection of the heavy + light chain genes into CHO-DG44 or CHO-K1, or
- Generation of **CHO-K1 SV40 LT** immortalized lines.

**Process Flow:**

1. **Cell-line development:** Transfect heavy + light chain gene cassettes → select (methotrexate for DHFR or puromycin) → screen for high producers via **automated single-cell cloning** (e.g., Berkeley Lights Beacon, FACS).
2. **MCB/WCB cryopreservation** in liquid nitrogen.
3. **Inoculum train:** Vial (1 mL) → T-flask (50 mL) → shake flask (1 L) → seed bioreactor (10–50 L) → production bioreactor (10 000–20 000 L).
4. **Production bioreactor:** **Fed-batch** in chemically defined, serum-free medium.
   - Day 0–3: Batch phase (glucose + glutamine)
   - Day 3–14: Fed-batch (concentrated feeds of glucose, amino acids; temperature shift 37 → 32 °C to arrest growth and extend production phase)
   - Target: **3–10 g/L** titre, **IVCD ~ 2–3 × 10⁷ cell·days/mL**
5. **Harvest:** Centrifugation + depth filtration (e.g., Millipore A1HC) → 0.2 µm clarification filter.

### 3.3.4 DSP of mAbs — The "Platinum Standard" Platform

| Step | Method | Purpose | Typical Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Protein A affinity | Capture (>95 % purity) | 85–95 % |
| 2 | Low-pH viral inactivation | Virus kill (pH 3.5, 60 min) | 90–95 % |
| 3 | Cation exchange (CEX) | Intermediate purification | 90 % |
| 4 | Anion exchange (AEX) flow-through | DNA & endotoxin reduction | 95 % |
| 5 | Viral filtration (Planova 20N) | Parvovirus clearance | 95 % |
| 6 | UF/DF + formulation | Buffer exchange & concentration | 95 % |

Overall DSP yield: ~65–80 %.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Protein A first?**
> Protein A from *Staphylococcus aureus* binds the Fc region of IgG with nanomolar affinity at neutral pH, then elutes at pH ~3.0–3.5. This single step replaces multiple classical chromatography steps and provides >95 % purity in one bind–wash–elute cycle. It is the "backbone" of every modern mAb platform.

### 3.3.5 Critical Quality Attributes (CQA) of a mAb Drug Substance

- **Identity** (Intact mass, peptide map, N-terminal sequencing)
- **Purity** (SEC-HPLC for aggregates, IEC for charge variants)
- **Potency** (cell-based bioassay, e.g., ADCC, CDC, Fab-mediated neutralization)
- **Glycosylation profile** (N-glycan analysis, UPLC-MS)
- **Host-cell DNA, protein, endotoxin, bioburden** (all < specified limits)
- **Viral safety** (in vivo + in vitro assays)

## 3.4 Comparative Summary Table (KTU Quick-Recall)

| Parameter | Insulin | HBV Vaccine | mAb |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Host** | *E. coli* (Lilly) / *S. cerevisiae* (Novo) | *S. cerevisiae* / *P. pastoris* | CHO (mammalian) |
| **Promoter** | *lac* / trp / ADH1 | ADH1 / AOX1 | CMV / EF1α |
| **Product location** | Intracellular (IB) / Secreted | Intracellular (membrane) | Secreted |
| **Glycosylation?** | No (recombinant is unglycosylated) | No | **Yes (essential)** |
| **Key DSP step** | Refolding + RP-HPLC + Zn-crystallization | Hydroxyapatite + gel filtration + Al(OH)₃ formulation | Protein A + AEX + CEX + viral filtration |
| **Final form** | Injectable solution / suspension | Suspension with Al(OH)₃ adjuvant | Liquid or lyophilized IV/SC |
| **Product scale** | Tons/year (world market ~$30 B) | Hundreds of kg HBsAg | Multi-kg/clinical batch (10 000 L → ~5 kg mAb) |
| **Cost per dose** | $4–10 (analog) | $0.50–5 (GAVI) | $1000–10000 (cancer mAb) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **Q1. What are recombinant proteins? Name any two industrial recombinant products with their host organisms.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Recombinant proteins are proteins produced by a heterologous host into which a gene encoding the desired protein has been inserted using rDNA technology (1 mark). Examples: (a) Human insulin — produced in *Escherichia coli* (Lilly's Humulin) and *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (Novo Nordisk) (1 mark). (b) Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) — produced in *S. cerevisiae* (Merck's Recombivax HB) (1 mark).

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between inclusion bodies and soluble protein expression in *E. coli*.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Inclusion bodies (IBs) are insoluble, misfolded protein aggregates that accumulate in the cytoplasm of *E. coli* when heterologous expression is high (1 mark). Advantages: high yield, easy isolation by centrifugation, protection from proteases; disadvantages: need solubilisation (8 M urea) and in-vitro refolding (1 mark). Soluble expression (e.g., periplasmic via *ompA* leader, or use of chaperone co-expression) yields correctly folded, active protein directly; lower yield and complex purification but avoids refolding (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

### ⭐ Question A: Manufacture of Recombinant Human Insulin

**(a)** Describe in detail the upstream processing steps involved in the manufacture of recombinant human insulin using *E. coli* as the host system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the downstream processing, refolding strategy, and quality control of human insulin drug substance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — USP of Human Insulin in *E. coli* (7 marks)

1. **Gene design and synthesis:** The 51-amino-acid insulin heterodimer is encoded by two short genes. The A-chain (21 aa) and B-chain (30 aa) genes are chemically synthesised as oligonucleotides and ligated into the polylinker of plasmid **pBR322** downstream of the *lac* (or trp) promoter (1 mark).
2. **Construction of fusion protein:** The insulin gene is fused to the **β-galactosidase** gene (or TrpE) to protect the small peptide from intracellular proteolysis and to enable immunological detection during screening (1 mark).
3. **Transformation:** Recombinant plasmid is transformed into *E. coli* K12 (or K-12 RV308) by CaCl₂ heat-shock, selected on ampicillin (1 mark).
4. **Master/Working Cell Bank:** Confirmed clones are preserved as MCB and WCB in 20 % glycerol at –80 °C (1 mark).
5. **Inoculum train:** A single vial is expanded in shake flasks in Luria-Bertani or modified M9 medium at 37 °C, 200 rpm, then transferred to seed and production bioreactors (5 L → 50 L → 500 L) (1 mark).
6. **Production fermentation:** Fed-batch in complex medium (glucose, yeast extract, casamino acids). At OD₆₀₀ ≈ 1.0, **induction with 1 mM IPTG** (or thermal shift to 42 °C for λpL promoter) for 4–6 h (1 mark).
7. **Harvest:** Cells are recovered by centrifugation; pellets are stored at –20 °C pending DSP. (0.5 mark)
8. **Quality of host strain:** *E. coli* is acceptable because insulin is non-glycosylated and lacks any requirement for mammalian-style PTMs. (0.5 mark)

#### Model Solution (b) — DSP, Refolding, QC of Human Insulin (7 marks)

1. **Cell disruption and IB isolation:** Cells are lysed by **lysozyme + sonication** or high-pressure homogenizer; IBs are collected by centrifugation at 10 000 × g (1 mark).
2. **Solubilization:** IBs are solubilized in 8 M urea, 50 mM Tris pH 8.0, with **β-mercaptoethanol** to reduce disulfides (1 mark).
3. **Fusion protein cleavage:** **CNBr (cyanogen bromide)** cleaves selectively at methionine engineered between β-gal and insulin chain, releasing the free insulin chain (1 mark).
4. **Refolding — Proinsulin strategy (preferred):** Recombinant proinsulin (A–C–B) is folded in vitro; the C-peptide acts as a covalent template positioning A and B chains for correct disulfide pairing (3 disulfides). Refolding buffer: 50 mM glycine pH 10.5, 1 M urea, 1 mM GSH / 0.5 mM GSSG redox pair. Yield 30–60 % (2 marks).
5. **Enzymatic maturation:** **Trypsin + carboxypeptidase B** cleave the C-peptide, producing mature A+B insulin (1 mark).
6. **Final purification:** **RP-HPLC** (C4 or C8 silica, 0.1 % TFA–acetonitrile gradient) followed by **crystallization as zinc-insulin hexamer** at pH 6.0 with ZnCl₂ (1 mark).
7. **Quality control:** HPLC purity > 99 %; mass spectrometry to confirm mass (5807.6 Da); bioassay (mouse convulsion assay or receptor-binding assay); endotoxin < 5 EU/kg; sterility; identity by peptide map; potency (USP insulin RS) (1 mark).

---

### ⭐ Question B: Manufacture of Monoclonal Antibody (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Describe the principle and steps involved in the development of a hybridoma cell line for monoclonal antibody production. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the upstream and downstream processing strategy for industrial-scale manufacture of a humanized IgG monoclonal antibody in CHO cells. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Hybridoma Development (7 marks)

1. **Immunization:** BALB/c mice (4–6 weeks old) are injected intraperitoneally with antigen (50–100 µg) emulsified in **Freund's complete adjuvant** (FCA), followed by 2–3 booster doses in Freund's incomplete adjuvant (FIA) at 2–3 week intervals. Serum titre is monitored by ELISA (1 mark).
2. **Myeloma preparation:** SP2/0 (or P3-X63-Ag8.653) myeloma cells deficient in **HGPRT enzyme** are cultured in log phase (5–10 × 10⁵ cells/mL) (1 mark).
3. **Cell fusion:** Spleen lymphocytes are mixed with myeloma cells (5:1 ratio) in 50 % **polyethylene glycol (PEG 1450)** for 1 minute, then slowly diluted with serum-free medium. PEG destabilizes the membrane lipid bilayer, allowing cytoplasmic fusion (1 mark).
4. **HAT selection (the cornerstone of hybridoma technology):** Cells are resuspended in HAT medium (Hypoxanthine 100 µM, Aminopterin 0.4 µM, Thymidine 16 µM). Aminopterin blocks *de novo* purine/pyrimidine synthesis via DHFR. Cells must use the HGPRT-dependent salvage pathway. Myeloma cells lack HGPRT → die; unfused B-cells die in 7–10 days; only **HGPRT⁺ × immortality** hybridomas survive (2 marks).
5. **Screening by ELISA:** Day 10–14, hybridoma supernatants are tested by ELISA against the target antigen. Positive wells are identified (1 mark).
6. **Single-cell cloning by limiting dilution:** Positive cells are diluted to 0.3 cells/well, plated in 96-well plates with feeder layer (mouse peritoneal macrophages) or conditioned medium. Process repeated twice to ensure monoclonality (1 mark).
7. **Master Cell Bank:** Cloned hybridomas are expanded, frozen in 10 % DMSO at –196 °C (liquid N₂). Production scale-up by *in-vitro* bioreactor or *in-vivo* ascites (latter discouraged) (1 mark).

#### Model Solution (b) — Upstream & Downstream Processing of Humanized IgG in CHO (7 marks)

**USP (3.5 marks)**
1. **Stable transfection:** Heavy chain (HC) and light chain (LC) gene cassettes (codon-optimized, under CMV or EF1α promoters) are co-transfected into CHO-DG44 (DHFR⁻) or CHO-K1 (GS-knockout) by electroporation or lipofection (1 mark).
2. **Selection & amplification:** Methotrexate (MTX) pressure for DHFR system or methionine sulfoximine (MSX) for GS system progressively amplifies gene copy number. High-producing clones are identified by **automated single-cell cloning (Beacon/FACS)** and ranked by **titer + qP** (specific productivity) (1 mark).
3. **Fed-batch production:** Inoculum train (shake flask → 50 L → 500 L seed → 15 000 L production bioreactor) in **chemically defined, serum-free, animal-component-free medium** with glucose + glutamine base. pH 7.0 ± 0.1, DO 40–60 %, agitation 50–200 rpm. Process runs 10–14 days: 3-day batch, then continuous feeding of glucose (4 g/L day) and amino acids. **Temperature shift from 37 °C to 32 °C** at day 4–5 arrests growth, extends stationary production phase, and reduces protease activity. Final titre 5–10 g/L; IVCD 2 × 10⁷ cell·days/mL (1.5 marks).

**DSP (3.5 marks)**
4. **Harvest & clarification:** Continuous centrifugation (disc-stack) followed by depth filtration (A1HC or X0HC) and 0.2 µm guard filter to remove cells, debris, aggregates (0.5 mark).
5. **Capture by Protein A chromatography:** The clarified harvest is loaded on a Protein A (MabSelect SuRe or Praesto) column at pH 7.0; IgG binds via Fc. Elution at **pH 3.0–3.5 with 50 mM acetate buffer**. Yield 85–95 % with >95 % purity (1 mark).
6. **Low-pH viral inactivation:** Hold eluate at pH 3.5, 25 °C, ≥ 60 min → inactivates enveloped viruses (≥ 4 log reduction) (0.5 mark).
7. **Intermediate purification:** Cation-exchange (CEX) bind-elute removes aggregates, leached Protein A, and host-cell protein (0.5 mark).
8. **Polishing:** Anion-exchange (AEX) in **flow-through mode** (Capto Q) removes residual DNA, endotoxin, and viruses (0.5 mark).
9. **Viral filtration:** Planova 20N (20 nm) filter → ≥ 4 log parvovirus clearance (0.5 mark).
10. **UF/DF and formulation:** Tangential-flow filtration (30 kDa MWCO) concentrates and exchanges buffer to the final formulation (e.g., 20 mM citrate, 100 mM NaCl, pH 6.0, polysorbate-80). Bulk drug substance filled under aseptic conditions (0.5 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "chimeric" and "humanized":** Chimeric = whole mouse V region on human C region. Humanized = ONLY the mouse CDRs grafted into a human framework. Easy 2-mark loss.
> 2. **HAT mechanism:** Students write "HAT kills unfused cells" — but specifically **Aminopterin** blocks *de novo* synthesis; only HGPRT⁺ cells survive via salvage. Mention HGPRT.
> 3. **Why Protein A:** Many students say "it binds the antibody" — examiners want the **Fc-region** binding at **neutral pH, elution at acidic pH**.
> 4. **CHO over HeLa/BHK:** Justify the choice — CHO is preferred for human-compatible glycosylation (sialylated biantennary N-glycans, low Neu5Gc).
> 5. **Refolding of insulin:** Don't say "disulfide bonds form automatically." State the **redox pair (GSH/GSSG) and pH** that drive the thiol-disulfide exchange to the thermodynamically correct form.
> 6. **Viral safety in mAb DSP:** Don't skip low-pH hold and viral filtration — examiners specifically look for them.
> 7. **HBV vaccine formulation:** Saying "aluminium" is incomplete — specify **aluminium hydroxide gel (Al(OH)₃)** as adjuvant, with typical dose **0.5 mg Al³⁺ per dose**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Recombinant protein** = heterologous protein produced from cloned gene in a non-natural host.
- **Universal process = USP (Clone → Cultivate) + DSP (Capture → Polish → Formulate).**
- **Host selection rule:** Need human-like N-glycosylation → CHO/mammalian; otherwise → microbial (cheaper, faster).
- **Human Insulin:** *E. coli* (Lilly proinsulin route) or *S. cerevisiae* (Novo). Two-chain vs proinsulin strategies; proinsulin gives higher refolding yield.
- **Disulfide formation** requires in-vitro **GSH/GSSG redox pair** for insulin refolding; **Protein A** captures IgG via Fc at neutral pH.
- **HBsAg** auto-assembles into **22-nm subviral particles** in yeast; purified by hydroxyapatite + gel filtration; formulated with **Al(OH)₃ adjuvant**.
- **Hybridoma** = B-lymphocyte × myeloma (SP2/0) → HAT medium (aminopterin blocks *de novo*, HGPRT salvage required) → ELISA screening → limiting dilution cloning.
- **mAb evolution:** Murine → Chimeric (mouse V + human C) → Humanized (CDR-grafted) → Fully human (XenoMouse or phage display).
- **CHO fed-batch** is the industrial gold standard: 5–10 g/L titre, 10–14 days, temperature shift 37 → 32 °C on day 4–5.
- **mAb DSP platform:** Protein A → low-pH hold → CEX → AEX flow-through → 20-nm viral filter → UF/DF.
- **Critical Quality Attributes (CQA):** identity, purity, potency, glycosylation, viral safety, aggregates.
- **Endotoxin** is a concern only in microbial-derived products (insulin, HBV vaccine); not in CHO-derived mAb.
- **Why HBsAg in yeast not E. coli?** Yeast enables correct 22-nm VLP assembly; E. coli produces misfolded aggregates.
- **Why mAb in CHO not bacteria?** Bacteria cannot N-glycosylate, and IgG requires glycosylation (especially Fc Asn297) for effector function (ADCC, CDC, half-life).
- **Plasmid stability (σ):** Continuous selection pressure (antibiotic or auxotrophy) in production is essential; segregrational loss otherwise.
- **Productivity equation:** $Q_p = \dfrac{\text{Titre}}{\text{Cycle time}}$; increases by **higher cell density** (fed-batch) or **longer culture** (perfusion).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Applications of Recombinant Proteins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBTT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industrial Applications of Recombinant Proteins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Recombinant Protein (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A recombinant protein is a functional polypeptide encoded by a gene that has been artificially assembled by joining DNA sequences from two or more sources, typically through the use of molecular cloning and recombinant DNA (rDNA) technology, and subsequently expressed in a heterologous host organism (bacterial, yeast, insect, plant, or mammalian cell) under controlled bioprocess conditions for industrial, therapeutic, diagnostic, or research applications.

In the strict KTU 2024 scheme context, the term *"Industrial Applications of Recombinant Proteins"* refers to the **engineering scale-up, downstream processing, formulation, and commercial deployment** of proteins produced via genetically modified host cells. The applications are classified under four industrial domains:

1. **Pharmaceutical / Therapeutic** — drugs, vaccines, hormones, monoclonal antibodies
2. **Industrial Enzymes** — detergents, food, textile, biofuel
3. **Diagnostic** — ELISA reagents, pregnancy tests, biosensors
4. **Agricultural / Veterinary** — Bt toxins, animal growth hormones, feed enzymes

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Biological Photocopy Machine" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a photocopier (the *host cell*) that can normally only copy internal documents. A biotechnologist **sneaks in a new page from another manual** (the *foreign gene*), changes the internal language settings (the *expression vector*), and forces the copier to **mass-produce that new page continuously**. The *page* is the recombinant protein, the *copier* is the host (e.g., *E. coli*, *Pichia pastoris*, CHO cells), and the *paper output tray* is the bioreactor harvest. Industrial biotechnology is essentially the art of optimizing this "copier" for maximum output, minimum errors (correct folding), and lowest cost per page.

### 1.3 Why Recombinant Proteins Matter Industrially

| Property | Natural Source | Recombinant Source |
|----------|----------------|---------------------|
| Purity | Contaminated with similar molecules | Defined, ≥ 95% by HPLC |
| Supply | Limited by donor tissue/animal | Unlimited (scaling bioreactor) |
| Cost | High (e.g., pig insulin) | Reduced (~30–50% lower) |
| Safety | Pathogen transmission risk | Defined, viral-clearance validated |
| Modification | No engineering possible | Site-directed mutagenesis feasible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statistic:** As of the 2024 global biopharma market, **> 300 recombinant therapeutic proteins** are FDA/EMA-approved, with the monoclonal antibody (mAb) segment alone valued at **> USD 250 billion**, making recombinant proteins the largest single revenue-generating class in industrial biotechnology.

### 1.4 Major Milestones (Historical Context for Examiners)

- **1982** — First recombinant therapeutic: Human Insulin (Humulin, Eli Lilly, in *E. coli*)
- **1985** — Recombinant Hepatitis B vaccine (HBsAg in yeast)
- **1986** — Recombinant tPA (tissue plasminogen activator)
- **1989** — Recombinant Erythropoietin (EPO)
- **1997** — First monoclonal antibody (Rituximab/anti-CD20) approved
- **2010s–2020s** — Bispecific antibodies, antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs), CAR-T platforms

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Industrial Workflow: From Gene to Product

The complete industrial chain for a recombinant protein consists of **five interconnected engineering modules**. KTU examiners expect candidates to *describe and draw* this chain in 7- or 14-mark questions.

**Module A — Upstream (Strain & Vector Construction)**

- Gene isolation by PCR or cDNA library screening
- Codon optimization for the target host
- Cloning into expression vector (e.g., pET, pPICZ, pcDNA)
- Transformation/transfection into host
- Clone selection (antibiotic resistance, reporter markers)
- Master Cell Bank (MCB) and Working Cell Bank (WCB) preparation

**Module B — Upstream (Process Development)**

- Media optimization (carbon, nitrogen, trace elements, inducers like IPTG/methanol)
- Bioreactor selection (stirred-tank, airlift, perfusion)
- Process mode: **Batch / Fed-batch / Continuous / Perfusion**
- Critical Process Parameters (CPPs): pH, DO, temperature, agitation, feed rate
- Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs): titer, glycosylation, aggregates, charge variants

**Module C — Harvest & Recovery (Primary Downstream)**

- Cell separation: centrifugation, microfiltration
- Cell disruption: high-pressure homogenizer, bead mill, enzymatic lysis
- Inclusion body solubilization (urea, guanidine-HCl) — if expressed in *E. coli*
- Refolding (dilution, dialysis, on-column)

**Module D — Purification (Polishing Downstream)**

- Capture: **Protein A / G chromatography** (mAbs), IMAC (His-tag), IEX
- Intermediate: ion-exchange chromatography (CEX, AEX)
- Polishing: size-exclusion chromatography (SEC), hydrophobic interaction
- Viral clearance: low-pH incubation, nanofiltration
- Formulation: buffer exchange, concentration, excipient addition

**Module E — Fill-Finish & Quality Release**

- Sterile filtration (0.22 µm)
- Lyophilization or liquid fill
- QC: HPLC, MS, ELISA, bioassay, endotoxin, sterility
- GMP release, regulatory filing

### 2.2 Host System Selection Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule (KTU 2024):** The host dictates cost, PTMs (post-translational modifications), scalability, and regulatory pathway. *E. coli* is preferred for small, non-glycosylated proteins. CHO cells dominate for complex glycosylated mAbs (~70% of approved mAbs).

| Host | Strength | Weakness | Typical Product Example |
|------|----------|----------|--------------------------|
| *Escherichia coli* | Fast (doubling ~20 min), cheap, high titer, well-characterized | No glycosylation, inclusion bodies, endotoxin | Insulin, Somatotropin, IFN-α, Filgrastim |
| *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | Eukaryotic PTMs (some), GRAS, secreted | Hyper-glycosylation, low yield | Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg), Insulin (some) |
| *Pichia pastoris* (Komagataella phaffii) | High density, methanol-inducible (AOX1), secreted | Methanol hazard, limited glycosylation | Ocrelizumab biosimilar, trypsin, HSA |
| Insect cells / Baculovirus (Sf9, Hi5) | Complex folding, large proteins | Viral clearance, costly | Cervarix (HPV VLP), Virus-Like Particles |
| CHO (Chinese Hamster Ovary) | Human-like glycosylation, FDA-accepted | Expensive, slow (~24 h doubling), viral risk | Most mAbs (Rituximab, Trastuzumab, Adalimumab) |
| Transgenic plants / animals | Low cost, oral delivery possible | Regulatory hurdles, low expression | Taliglucerase alfa (carrot cells), ATryn (goat milk) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-relevant**. Memorize the units in **bold** — KTU examiners frequently award partial marks for correct units even when numerical errors occur.

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit |
|--------|---------|-------------|------|
| $T$ (Titer) | $T = \dfrac{m_P}{V_{final}}$ | Final product concentration in harvest | **g/L** or **mg/mL** |
| $Q_P$ (Volumetric Productivity) | $Q_P = \dfrac{T - T_0}{t}$ | Product formed per litre per hour | **mg/(L·h)** |
| $q_P$ (Specific Productivity) | $q_P = \dfrac{1}{X}\cdot\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ | Per-cell product formation rate | **pg/(cell·day)** or **mg/(g DCW·h)** |
| $Y_{X/S}$ (Biomass Yield) | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | Biomass from substrate | **g/g** |
| $Y_{P/S}$ (Product Yield) | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | Product from substrate | **g/g** |
| $Y_{P/X}$ (Product per biomass) | $Y_{P/X} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta X}$ | Cell-specific output | **g/g** |
| $Y_{overall}$ (Overall Process Yield) | $Y_{overall} = Y_{ferment} \times Y_{recovery} \times Y_{purif}$ | End-to-end yield | dimensionless (%) |
| $F$ (Purification Fold) | $F = \dfrac{Specific Activity_{final}}{Specific Activity_{initial}}$ | Fold purification | dimensionless |
| $\mu$ (Specific Growth Rate) | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\cdot\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Exponential phase rate | **h⁻¹** |
| $t_d$ (Doubling Time) | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | Time for biomass to double | **h** |
| $\eta_{refold}$ (Refolding Yield) | $\eta_{refold} = \dfrac{P_{soluble}}{P_{denatured}}$ | % IB correctly refolded | **%** |
| Mass Balance | $V_1 C_1 = V_2 C_2$ | Dilution/concentration check | — |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU numericals, *always* carry **dimensional consistency**. If titer is asked in **mg/L** and volume in **L**, multiplying them gives **mg** of product. Examiners check the unit chain.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cost-of-goods (COG) reduction:** A shift from stainless-steel fed-batch to single-use bioreactors has reduced mAb upstream COG from ~USD 100/g (2005) to ~USD 30–50/g (2024).
- **Biosimilar economics:** A recombinant protein whose originator patent expires (e.g., Adalimumab — 2023) opens a USD 20+ billion biosimilar market.
- **Continuous manufacturing:** Integrated continuous bioprocessing (ICBP) platforms (e.g., Merck, Sanofi) push $Q_P$ to **> 1 g/(L·day)** for stable mAbs.
- **Food & feed:** Recombinant chymosin replaced calf rennet in ~80% of cheese production globally, saving ~USD 200 million/year.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Overall Process Yield

The overall yield of a recombinant protein industrial process is the **product of independent stage yields**, because each stage operates on the material that survived the previous one.

Let:
- $P_0$ = Total protein mass entering the fermenter (substrate-bound potential)
- $P_F$ = Protein mass after fermentation (g)
- $P_R$ = Protein mass after recovery (g)
- $P_P$ = Protein mass after purification (g)
- $P_{final}$ = Protein mass in final vial (g)

**Stage yields** are defined as the fraction of input retained at each step:

$$Y_{ferment} = \frac{P_F}{P_0}$$

$$Y_{recovery} = \frac{P_R}{P_F}$$

$$Y_{purif} = \frac{P_P}{P_R}$$

**Multiplying stage by stage:**

$$Y_{ferment} \times Y_{recovery} \times Y_{purif} = \frac{P_F}{P_0} \cdot \frac{P_R}{P_F} \cdot \frac{P_P}{P_R}$$

**Cancelling intermediate masses ($P_F$ and $P_R$ cancel by telescoping):**

$$Y_{ferment} \times Y_{recovery} \times Y_{purif} = \frac{P_P}{P_0}$$

**Re-defining the overall yield:**

$$\boxed{Y_{overall} = \frac{P_{final}}{P_0} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i}$$

This telescopic cancellation is the basis of the *"multiplicative loss rule"* — a small drop in each stage compounds dramatically. A 90% yield in 5 sequential stages gives only $0.9^5 = 0.59$ (i.e., **59%**), but 80% in 5 stages gives $0.8^5 = 0.33$ (**33%**).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 10,000 L fed-batch bioreactor produces a recombinant mAb. Initial cell density $X_0 = 0.5 \times 10^6$ cells/mL; final $X_f = 12 \times 10^6$ cells/mL. Titer $T = 4.5$ g/L. Process time $t = 14$ days. Calculate: (i) specific productivity, (ii) volumetric productivity, (iii) overall yield if downstream recovery is 70% and purification is 85%, and (iv) total product mass in final vials.

**Given:**
- $V = 10{,}000$ L
- $X_0 = 0.5 \times 10^6$ cells/mL $= 0.5 \times 10^9$ cells/L
- $X_f = 12 \times 10^9$ cells/L
- $T = 4.5$ g/L
- $t = 14$ days $= 14 \times 24 = 336$ h
- $Y_{recovery} = 0.70$
- $Y_{purif} = 0.85$

**(i) Specific Productivity $q_P$:**

$$q_P = \frac{T}{X_{avg} \cdot t}$$

Average cell density (assuming linear approximation acceptable for KTU):

$$X_{avg} = \frac{X_0 + X_f}{2} = \frac{0.5 + 12}{2} \times 10^9 = 6.25 \times 10^9 \text{ cells/L}$$

Converting titer to pg (for unit consistency with cells):

$$T = 4.5 \text{ g/L} = 4.5 \times 10^{12} \text{ pg/L}$$

$$q_P = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{12} \text{ pg/L}}{6.25 \times 10^9 \text{ cells/L} \times 336 \text{ h}}$$

$$q_P = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{12}}{2.1 \times 10^{12}} = 2.14 \text{ pg/(cell·h)}$$

Converting to per-day basis:

$$q_P = 2.14 \times 24 = 51.4 \text{ pg/(cell·day)}$$

**(ii) Volumetric Productivity $Q_P$:**

$$Q_P = \frac{T - T_0}{t} = \frac{4.5 - 0}{336} = 0.0134 \text{ g/(L·h)}$$

In per-day units:

$$Q_P = 0.0134 \times 24 = 0.321 \text{ g/(L·day)}$$

**(iii) Overall Process Yield:**

$$Y_{overall} = 1.0 \times 0.70 \times 0.85 = 0.595 = 59.5\%$$

**(iv) Total Product Mass in Vials:**

Total product harvested:

$$m_{harvest} = T \times V = 4.5 \text{ g/L} \times 10{,}000 \text{ L} = 45{,}000 \text{ g} = 45 \text{ kg}$$

Mass after downstream:

$$m_{final} = 45{,}000 \times 0.70 \times 0.85 = 26{,}775 \text{ g} = 26.78 \text{ kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** Note that the fermenter "loss" is implicitly zero for titer-based calculation (we start measuring from harvest). The stage-yield cascade begins at the **centrifugation/clarification** step. KTU answer keys frequently dock 1 mark for confusing fermenter yield with overall yield.

### 3.3 Derivation: Inclusion Body Refolding Mass Balance

For *E. coli*-expressed proteins (e.g., insulin, IFN-α), the target accumulates as **inclusion bodies (IBs)** — insoluble, misfolded aggregates. The recovery chain is:

$$\text{IBs} \xrightarrow{\text{Solubilize (6M Guanidine-HCl)}} \text{Denatured} \xrightarrow{\text{Refold (dilution)}} \text{Native}$$

Mass balance with losses:

$$m_{native} = m_{IBs} \cdot \eta_{solub} \cdot \eta_{refold} \cdot \eta_{recovery}$$

**Where:**
- $\eta_{solub}$ ≈ 0.90 (solubilization efficiency)
- $\eta_{refold}$ ≈ 0.30–0.60 (refolding bottleneck)
- $\eta_{recovery}$ ≈ 0.85 (post-refold capture)

Hence for a 10 kg IB batch:

$$m_{native} = 10 \times 0.90 \times 0.45 \times 0.85 = 3.44 \text{ kg native protein}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The **refolding step is the single largest loss point** in bacterial recombinant processes — typically 40–70% of denatured protein aggregates irreversibly. KTU examiners award marks for *naming the step* and *quantifying its yield*.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Major Industrial Recombinant Proteins

| Product | Host | Class | Indication / Use | Global Market 2024 |
|---------|------|-------|------------------|---------------------|
| Insulin (Humulin/NovoLog) | *E. coli* / Yeast | Hormone | Diabetes mellitus | ~USD 25 billion |
| Erythropoietin (EPO) | CHO | Cytokine | Anemia | ~USD 5 billion |
| Adalimumab (Humira) | CHO | mAb (TNF-α) | RA, Crohn's, Psoriasis | ~USD 20 billion (declining post-biosimilar) |
| Trastuzumab (Herceptin) | CHO | mAb (HER2) | Breast cancer | ~USD 7 billion |
| Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) | CHO | mAb (PD-1) | Oncology (broad) | ~USD 25 billion (top-selling drug 2024) |
| Hepatitis B vaccine (HBsAg) | Yeast | VLP/subunit | HBV prevention | ~USD 3 billion |
| tPA (Alteplase) | CHO | Enzyme | Thrombolysis | ~USD 1.5 billion |
| Filgrastim (G-CSF) | *E. coli* | Cytokine | Neutropenia | ~USD 1 billion |
| coagulation Factor VIII | CHO / BHK | Coagulation | Hemophilia A | ~USD 5 billion |
| Glucocerebrosidase (imiglucerase) | CHO | Enzyme | Gaucher disease | ~USD 1.5 billion |
| Subtilisin / Lipase (detergents) | *Bacillus subtilis* | Enzyme | Detergent additive | ~USD 1 billion |
| Chymosin (cheese-making) | *Aspergillus niger / E. coli* | Enzyme | Milk coagulation | ~USD 200 million |
| Cellulase (bioethanol) | *Trichoderma reesei* | Enzyme | Biomass saccharification | ~USD 700 million |
| mRNA platform (COVID vaccines) | In vitro transcription (cell-free) | mRNA + LNPs | COVID-19, others | ~USD 50 billion (peak), still high |

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison: Whole-Cell vs Cell-Free

$$\text{Cell-Free Protein Synthesis (CFPS):} \quad Y_{CFPS} = \frac{P_{lysate}}{t \cdot V_{rxn}}$$

CFPS achieves **> 1 g/L** in a few hours but is **cost-prohibitive at industrial scale** (~$100–500 per mL lysate). Whole-cell remains dominant for products >10 kg/year.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Industrial Recombinant Protein Manufacturing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Gene of Interest] --> A1[PCR Amplification]
    A1 --> A2[Codon Optimization]
    A2 --> A3[Cloning into Expression Vector]
    A3 --> A4[Transformation into Host Cell]
    A4 --> A5[Master Cell Bank MCB]
    A5 --> A6[Working Cell Bank WCB]
    A6 --> B1[Seed Bioreactor]
    B1 --> B2[Production Bioreactor Fed Batch]
    B2 --> B3[Harvest Centrifugation MF]
    B3 --> C1[Cell Disruption or Direct Capture]
    C1 --> C2[Solubilization if Inclusion Body]
    C2 --> C3[Refolding Dilution Dialysis]
    C3 --> D1[Capture Chromatography Protein A or IMAC]
    D1 --> D2[Intermediate Purification IEX]
    D2 --> D3[Polishing SEC HIC]
    D3 --> D4[Viral Clearance Low pH Nanofiltration]
    D4 --> E1[Formulation Buffer Exchange]
    E1 --> E2[Sterile Filtration 0.22 micron]
    E2 --> E3[Fill Finish Vial or Syringe]
    E3 --> E4[QC Release HPLC MS Bioassay]
    E4 --> E5[Commercial Product]
```

### 4.2 Host Selection Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Decision] --> S1{Protein Size}
    S1 -- Less than 50 kDa --> S2{Glycosylation Needed}
    S1 -- More than 50 kDa --> S3{Complex Multimeric}
    S2 -- No --> S4[Choose E coli]
    S2 -- Yes --> S5[Choose Yeast or Pichia]
    S3 -- Yes --> S6[Choose CHO or HEK293]
    S3 -- No --> S7[Choose Insect Baculovirus]
    S4 --> S5A[Insulin Growth Hormone IFN alpha]
    S5 --> S5B[HBsAg HSA]
    S6 --> S5C[mAbs EPO Factor VIII]
    S7 --> S5D[VLPs HPV Vaccine]
```

### 4.3 Downstream Purification Cascade (Chromatographic Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Crude Harvest] --> H2[Capture Affinity Protein A]
    H2 --> H3[Low pH Viral Inactivation]
    H3 --> H4[Intermediate Cation Exchange]
    H4 --> H5[Anion Exchange Flow Through]
    H5 --> H6[Polishing Size Exclusion]
    H6 --> H7[Nanofiltration 20 nm]
    H7 --> H8[UF DF Formulation]
    H8 --> H9[Final Bulk Drug Substance]
```

### 4.4 Mass-Balance Telemetry of Process Yields

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0[Substrate Fed 100 units] --> M1[Biomass plus Product 95 percent retained]
    M1 --> M2[Recovery 70 percent retained]
    M2 --> M3[Purification 85 percent retained]
    M3 --> M4[Final Product 56.5 units]
    M1 -.->|Loss 5 percent| ML1[Cell Debris Metabolites]
    M2 -.->|Loss 30 percent| ML2[Solids Wastes Denatured Protein]
    M3 -.->|Loss 15 percent| ML3[Impurity Fractions HCP DNA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** Each labeled stream represents a *stage yield boundary*. The greyed side-arrows represent **waste / loss streams**, which together account for the 100% – $Y_{overall}$ fraction. KTU examiners expect candidates to be able to annotate the loss percentages on a similar diagram and to identify which downstream step dominates the loss (usually **purification** for soluble proteins, **refolding** for inclusion-body proteins).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> **Define recombinant protein. List any four industrially important recombinant proteins with their host organisms.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

A **recombinant protein** is a protein produced by inserting a gene from one organism into the genome of another (host) organism, which then expresses and often modifies the foreign protein using rDNA technology.

Four industrially important recombinant proteins (1/2 mark each):

| # | Protein | Host |
|---|---------|------|
| 1 | Human Insulin | *Escherichia coli* / *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* |
| 2 | Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) | *S. cerevisiae* / *Pichia pastoris* |
| 3 | Tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) | CHO (Chinese Hamster Ovary) cells |
| 4 | Adalimumab (anti-TNF-α mAb) | CHO cells |

> [!TIP]
> Format tip: KTU examiners award the definition (1 mark) + 4 items × 0.5 mark = total 3 marks.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
> **Why is *E. coli* the most widely used host for recombinant protein production despite its inability to perform post-translational modifications?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

*E. coli* is the preferred host for several industrial reasons (1 mark per point, any three of the following):

1. **Rapid growth** — doubling time ~20 min enables short fermentation cycles (3–5 days), lowering capital cost.
2. **High cell density** — fed-batch cultures achieve >100 g DCW/L, boosting volumetric productivity.
3. **Well-characterized genetics** — extensive vector libraries (pET, pBAD, pUC) and easy genetic manipulation.
4. **Low cost media** — defined, cheap carbon (glucose/glycerol) and nitrogen sources.
5. **High yield of small, non-glycosylated proteins** — insulin, somatotropin, IFN-α, G-CSF, all produced at multi-kg scale.

*E. coli* is unsuitable for products requiring glycosylation, complex disulphide isomerization, or specific human PTMs — in those cases CHO, yeast, or insect systems are used (1 mark for this concluding limitation).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Choice]**  
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **various host systems** used for the industrial production of recombinant proteins. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Explain the **monoclonal antibody (mAb) production platform** — from gene to purified drug substance — highlighting the critical upstream and downstream steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

The four major industrial host systems are:

**(i) *Escherichia coli* (Bacterial) — 1.5 marks**
- *Advantages:* Fastest growth, lowest cost, well-characterized genetics, high titer (5–10 g/L achievable for soluble products, up to 30% of total cell protein in inclusion bodies), no viral contamination risk for human therapeutics.
- *Limitations:* No glycosylation, no proper disulphide bond formation in cytoplasm, endotoxin (LPS) removal mandatory, inclusion body refolding burden, codons rarely used in *E. coli* (e.g., AGA in insulin precursors) require engineering.
- *Examples:* Insulin, Filgrastim, Somatotropin, IFN-α, IFN-γ, Hepatitis B core antigen.

**(ii) Yeast (*S. cerevisiae*, *Pichia pastoris*) — 1.5 marks**
- *Advantages:* Eukaryotic PTMs, GRAS status for food/feed, secreted expression simplifies downstream, *P. pastoris* can reach >100 g/L wet cells with methanol-induced AOX1 promoter.
- *Limitations:* Hypermannosylation in *S. cerevisiae* (immunogenic), methanol hazard in *Pichia*, lower product titres than CHO for full-length mAbs.
- *Examples:* HBsAg (Hepatitis B vaccine), insulin, albumin, trypsin.

**(iii) Insect cells / Baculovirus Expression Vector System (BEVS) — 1.5 marks**
- *Advantages:* Large protein capacity, complex folding, VLP formation possible, post-translational modifications similar to mammalian.
- *Limitations:* Viral clearance validation required, expensive media, lytic infection limits scalability.
- *Examples:* Cervarix (HPV VLP), Flublok (influenza HA), virus-like particle vaccines.

**(iv) Mammalian Cells (CHO, HEK293, NS0) — 1.5 marks**
- *Advantages:* Human-compatible glycosylation (especially CHO-derived biantennary N-glycans), FDA-accepted for therapeutics, capable of complex multimeric assembly.
- *Limitations:* Expensive media, slow doubling (~24 h), viral contamination risk, sensitive to shear.
- *Examples:* Rituximab, Trastuzumab, Adalimumab, Pembrolizumab, EPO, Factor VIII.

**Concluding comparison (1 mark):** CHO dominates ~70% of approved therapeutic mAbs due to its human-like glycosylation profile; *E. coli* dominates for non-glycosylated small proteins and is the cheapest route to industrial scale.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

The mAb production platform is a multi-stage industrial process:

**Upstream (3 marks):**
1. **Gene construction** — heavy chain (HC) and light chain (LC) variable regions are cloned from the hybridoma or phage-display library, fused to human IgG1 constant regions, and inserted into a **dual-expression vector** (or co-transfected).
2. **Cell line engineering** — plasmid transfected into CHO-K1 or CHO-S; selection by methotrexate (DHFR) or puromycin; clonal selection by limiting dilution ensures monoclonality.
3. **Master/Working Cell Banks** — MCB and WCB stored in liquid N₂ vapour phase; cell viability, sterility, and mycoplasma tested.
4. **Production bioreactor** — fed-batch stirred-tank (10,000–25,000 L), 12–18 days, with temperature shift (37°C → 32°C) to arrest growth and enhance productivity. Titer target: 5–10 g/L.

**Downstream (3 marks):**
1. **Harvest** — continuous centrifugation or depth filtration; clarification.
2. **Capture** — **Protein A affinity chromatography** — binds Fc region of IgG; elution at low pH (pH 3.0–3.5).
3. **Viral inactivation** — low-pH incubation (pH 3.5–3.7 for 30–60 min).
4. **Intermediate purification** — cation-exchange (CEX) bind-elute; anion-exchange (AEX) flow-through (removes DNA, HCP, endotoxin).
5. **Polishing** — size-exclusion chromatography removes aggregates.
6. **Viral filtration** — 20 nm (or 15 nm) nanofilter, then 0.22 µm sterile filter.
7. **UF/DF** — ultrafiltration/diafiltration into formulation buffer (e.g., histidine, sucrose, polysorbate-80).

**Formulation & Release (1 mark):** Bulk drug substance filled into vials, lyophilized or liquid; QC includes HPLC-SEC (aggregates), CEX (charge variants), glycan mapping, host-cell-protein ELISA, endotoxin (LAL), sterility, potency bioassay.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls (Valuation Warning):**  
> 1. Writing "Protein G" instead of **Protein A** for mAb capture — Protein G binds but is **not used industrially** for IgG1 due to cost; Protein A is the workhorse.  
> 2. Forgetting the **low-pH viral inactivation** step — KTU answer keys specifically award 0.5 mark for mentioning it.  
> 3. Stating titer values >10 g/L without mentioning the **temperature-shift to productivity phase** is incomplete.  
> 4. Confusing "mAb purification" with "small protein purification" — for mAbs, Protein A is mandatory.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Alternative Choice]**  
> **(a)** What are **inclusion bodies**? Describe the steps involved in the recovery and **refolding of recombinant proteins from inclusion bodies**, with a neat flow diagram. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **industrial production of recombinant insulin** in *E. coli*. Discuss the process economics and biosafety considerations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Inclusion bodies (IBs) are **insoluble, dense, refractile aggregates** of misfolded recombinant protein that accumulate in the cytoplasm (or periplasm) of *E. coli* when high-level expression saturates the cellular folding and chaperone machinery. They appear as bright spots under phase-contrast microscopy and contain ~80–95% recombinant protein in reduced, denatured, non-native form.

**Why they form (0.5 mark):** High transcription/translation rate, reducing cytoplasmic environment (incompatible with disulphide bond formation), lack of eukaryotic chaperones, hydrophobic protein patches.

**Recovery & Refolding Steps (5.5 marks):**

1. **Cell harvest** — centrifugation; cells may be stored frozen.
2. **Cell disruption** — high-pressure homogenizer (≥15,000 psi) or bead mill; release IBs.
3. **IB isolation** — differential centrifugation (5,000–10,000 × g); wash with Triton X-100 (0.5–1%) + EDTA to remove membrane and nucleic acid contaminants.
4. **Solubilization** — denature IBs with **6 M Guanidine-HCl** or **8 M Urea**, often with reducing agents (DTT, β-mercaptoethanol) to break disulphides; pH 8–9.
5. **Refolding** — *the critical step*:
   - **Rapid dilution** (1:50–1:100) into refolding buffer (Tris, L-arginine, GSH/GSSG redox pair, glycerol, pH 8–9).
   - **Diafiltration** against refolding buffer.
   - **On-column refolding** (immobilized on Ni-NTA for His-tagged proteins).
   - **Pulsed refolding** (small bolus additions to avoid aggregation).
6. **Concentration & capture** — UF or IMAC.
7. **Further purification** — IEX, SEC.

**Neat Flow Diagram (built into the answer — 0.5 mark bonus for explicit draw):**
> Cells → Disruption → IB Pellet → Wash → Solubilize (6M GuHCl + DTT) → Refold (dilution/dialysis) → Active Native Protein → Chromatography → Pure Product

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls:**  
> 1. Omitting the **wash step** — impurity carry-over fouls the refolding buffer.  
> 2. Using **L-arginine** in the answer is a high-value keyword (0.5 mark).  
> 3. Stating that *all* inclusion-body proteins refold spontaneously — the yield is typically **30–60%**, with the rest aggregating irreversibly.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Industrial Production of Recombinant Human Insulin in *E. coli*:**

**Historical context (1 mark):** Recombinant insulin (Humulin, Eli Lilly, 1982) was the **first FDA-approved recombinant therapeutic**, replacing animal-derived pork/beef insulin (which caused allergic reactions in 5–10% of patients).

**Two industrial routes (4 marks):**

**Route 1 — Separate-chain (Lilly process):**
- Two plasmid constructs: one encodes **insulin A-chain**, the other encodes **insulin B-chain**, each as a fusion with a small peptide leader (e.g., β-galactosidase or TrpE).
- Separate fermentations of *E. coli* → harvest → IB isolation → solubilization → refolding.
- After CNBr cleavage of the methionine linker, pure A and B chains are obtained.
- Chains are mixed in defined ratio with mild reduction, allowing correct disulphide bonds to form (**1 intra-A, 1 intra-B, 2 inter-A-B disulphides**).
- Final product is purified by RP-HPLC and crystallized as zinc-insulin hexamer.

**Route 2 — Proinsulin single-chain (Novo Nordisk process):**
- A single gene encodes human proinsulin (B-chain–C-peptide–A-chain).
- Expressed in *S. cerevisiae* (later also *E. coli*) as a secreted, soluble protein.
- Fermentation yield is high; proinsulin folds correctly with one disulphide topology.
- Enzymatic cleavage by **trypsin and carboxypeptidase B** removes the C-peptide, releasing mature insulin.
- Purification by RP-HPLC; formulation as zinc hexamer.

**Process economics (1.5 marks):**
- World insulin market: >USD 25 billion (2024).
- Production cost dropped from USD 50/g (animal insulin, 1980) to ~USD 2–5/g (recombinant, 2024).
- Fermentation yield: ~2–4 g/L of insulin; multi-ton annual production.
- Major cost contributors: fermentation media (40%), downstream purification (35%), QC and release (15%), facility overhead (10%).

**Biosafety considerations (0.5 mark):**
- *E. coli* K-12 derivatives are non-pathogenic, non-toxigenic, and require BSL-1 containment.
- Absence of endotoxin must be verified by LAL (Limulus Amebocyte Lysate) assay before human use.
- Insulin analogues (Lispro, Aspart, Glargine) are produced by site-directed mutagenesis — first example of an "engineered" drug on the market.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls:**  
> 1. Forgetting that recombinant insulin is **not** extracted from animal pancreas — examiners deduct 1 mark for this fundamental confusion.  
> 2. Missing the **disulphide bond topology** (1 intra-A, 1 intra-B, 2 inter-A-B = total 3) — important structural fact worth 0.5 mark.  
> 3. Conflating insulin with **interferon** (also from *E. coli* but different indications).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Aggregate Pitfalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **Aggregate marks loss zones (compiled from 2023–2024 KTU answer-key review):**
> 1. **Definition skipping** — A question worth 7 marks that begins with "Define/Explain…" requires a crisp 2–3-line definition **before** the elaboration. Marks are docked when the body jumps straight into details.
> 2. **No block diagram in 14-mark questions** — A flow diagram or process block chart is **mandatory** for ≥ 1 mark allocation. Always sketch even if not asked.
> 3. **Units inconsistency** — Mix of g/L with mg/mL in the same numerical loses 0.5–1 mark. Standardize to one unit.
> 4. **No yield cascade shown** — When asked about overall process, students often stop at the fermenter. The multiplicative formula $Y_{overall} = \prod Y_i$ must be **explicitly written**.
> 5. **Missing regulatory mention** — A single line about "FDA-approved" or "GMP-compliant" adds ~0.5 mark and signals industrial maturity of the answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Use before walking into the exam hall):**

- **Core definition (1 line):** Recombinant protein = foreign gene expressed in a heterologous host via rDNA technology.
- **Top 4 host systems & their flagship products:**
  - *E. coli* → Insulin, IFN-α, Filgrastim, Somatotropin
  - Yeast (*S. cerevisiae / Pichia*) → HBsAg, Insulin, HSA
  - Insect (BEVS) → HPV VLP, Influenza HA
  - CHO cells → ~70% of approved mAbs, EPO, Factor VIII
