- **mAb platform critical steps:** Protein A capture → Low-pH viral inactivation → CEX → AEX (flow-through) → SEC polishing → 20-nm nanofiltration → UF/DF formulation.
- **Overall yield formula:** $Y_{overall} = Y_{ferment} \times Y_{recovery} \times Y_{purif}$ (multiplicative loss).
- **Specific productivity unit:** $q_P$ in **pg/(cell·day)** for mammalian, **mg/(g DCW·h)** for microbial.
- **Inclusion body chain:** Cells → Disruption → IB Wash → Solubilize (6M GuHCl) → Refold (L-arginine buffer) → Native protein → Purification.
- **Insulin production:** Two industrial routes — separate A/B chains (Lilly, *E. coli*) vs proinsulin (Novo, yeast). 3 disulphide bonds, 51 amino acids.
- **Stat for answer enrichment:** ~70% of approved mAbs are produced in CHO cells; *E. coli* cannot perform glycosylation; HBsAg vaccine in yeast saved >USD 200 million/year over plasma-derived vaccine.
- **Mandatory diagrams:** Always include a *process flow chart* in 14-mark answers; annotate yields at each stage.
- **"What not to forget":** Endotoxin (LAL) test, viral clearance (low-pH + nanofiltration), MCB/WCB, GMP, FDA-acceptance of CHO for mAbs, codon optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of Xanthan Gum, Nisin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBTT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Production of Xanthan Gum, Nisin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — INDUSTRIAL BIOPRODUCTION OF MICROBIAL POLYMERS & ANTIMICROBIALS
## Topic: Production of Xanthan Gum & Nisin

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition 1 — Xanthan Gum (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> Xanthan gum is a high-molecular-weight, anionic **extracellular heteropolysaccharide (exopolysaccharide, EPS)** produced aerobically by the Gram-negative bacterium ***Xanthomonas campestris*** pv. campestris. Its repeating pentasaccharide backbone consists of **β-1,4-linked D-glucose units** with a trisaccharide side chain containing D-mannose, D-glucuronic acid, and pyruvylated mannose.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 2 — Nisin (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> Nisin is a **Class I bacteriocin (lantibiotic)** — a 34-amino-acid polycyclic antimicrobial peptide produced naturally by the Gram-positive lactic acid bacterium ***Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis***. It contains the unusual thioether amino acids lanthionine and methyllanthionine, hence the name "**lant**ibiotic."

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

- **Xanthan Gum Analogy → "The Molecular Sponge":** Imagine a tiny, curly, charged thread that you drop into water. It immediately grabs water molecules, swells, and forms a thick, sticky gel. That is exactly what xanthan gum does at a microscopic level. When *Xanthomonas campestris* is grown in sugar water, it secretes this "molecular thread" outside its cell wall to protect itself from drying out. We harvest that thread, dry it, and grind it into the white powder used in salad dressings, ice creams, and even oil-drilling fluids.

- **Nisin Analogy → "The Molecular Bodyguard for Cheese":** Picture a microscopic "smart bomb" produced by harmless bacteria in milk. When *Lactococcus lactis* grows, it releases nisin — a tiny peptide that punches holes in the cell membranes of *spoilage bacteria* like *Listeria* and *Clostridium*, killing them. We harvest this "bodyguard" peptide and add it to cheese, canned food, and pasteurized milk so that spoilage bacteria cannot grow.

### 1.2 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Xanthan Gum | Nisin |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Weight | **2 × 10⁶ to 20 × 10⁶ Da** | **~3,350 Da (3.5 kDa)** |
| Number of Amino Acids / Sugar Units | Pentasaccharide repeating unit | **34 amino acids** |
| Producer Organism | *Xanthomonas campestris* | *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* |
| GRAS Status | **Yes (FDA, 1969)** | **Yes (FDA, 1988; E234 in EU)** |
| Global Market Value | **> USD 1.2 billion (2023)** | **> USD 600 million (2023)** |
| Typical Industrial Yield | **20–30 g/L** (industrial batch) | **Nisaplin™ = 2.5% w/w pure nisin** |
| Approx. Industrial Titer | **~25–50 g/L** (high-performance strains) | **~400–1000 IU/mL broth** |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** Xanthan = "Xantho-**X**tra-thick" (thickener) and Nisin = "**N**aturally **I**nhibits **S**poilage organisms **I**n milk products."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

---

## 2.1 XANTHAN GUM — Production Process (Step-by-Step Logic)

### A. The Microbial Producer
- **Strain:** *Xanthomonas campestris* pv. *campestris* (NRRL B-1459, ATCC 13951) is the industrial reference strain.
- **Morphology:** Gram-negative, motile, rod-shaped, aerobic, produces yellow carotenoid pigment (**xanthomonadin**) on YDC agar.
- **Pathogenicity Note:** Causes black rot in crucifers (cabbage, cauliflower); industrial strains are non-toxigenic and handled under **BSL-1** containment.

### B. Fermentation Medium (Typical Industrial Composition)

| Component | Concentration (g/L) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Glucose / Sucrose / Corn syrup | **20–40** | Carbon & energy source |
| Yeast extract / Soy peptone | **2–5** | Nitrogen, vitamins |
| (NH₄)₂HPO₄ | **0.5–2.0** | Nitrogen source |
| KH₂PO₄ | **2.0–5.0** | Phosphate buffer |
| MgSO₄·7H₂O | **0.1–0.5** | Cofactor (Mg²⁺) |
| Trace elements (Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu) | **ppm levels** | Enzyme cofactors |
| Initial pH | **6.8–7.2** | Optimal for growth |
| Temperature | **28–30 °C** | Optimal |
| Dissolved Oxygen (DO) | **> 30% saturation** | Strictly aerobic |
| pH control | **pH 7.0 ± 0.2** (auto with NaOH/KOH) | Prevents acid crash |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pH 7.0 is critical:** As xanthan accumulates, the broth becomes highly viscous (shear-thinning pseudoplastic). Poor pH control causes *Xanthomonas* to switch to non-xanthan-producing metabolism, dropping yield by 60–80%.

### C. Fermentation Process Parameters
- **Inoculum:** 5–10% (v/v) from 18–24 h seed culture (OD₆₀₀ ≈ 1.0–2.0).
- **Production Vessel:** Stirred-tank reactor (STR) of **50–200 m³** industrial scale.
- **Aeration:** 0.5–1.0 vvm (volume air/volume liquid/minute).
- **Agitation:** Rushton-type impellers, **150–300 rpm** (limited by high viscosity).
- **Duration:** **48–96 h** batch; **fed-batch preferred** to avoid glucose catabolite repression.
- **C-source limitation:** Industrial fed-batch uses **glucose feed 10–20 g/L/h** to maintain residual 5–15 g/L.

### D. Downstream Processing (Recovery)
1. **Cell kill & pasteurization** — Heat broth to **80–95 °C for 10–15 min** (also reduces viscosity).
2. **Cell removal** — Centrifugation or microfiltration (0.2 µm).
3. **Protein removal** — Add **protease** (e.g., alcalase) or precipitate with **kaolin/activated carbon** (1–2%).
4. **Concentration** — Ultrafiltration (10–30 kDa cutoff) or vacuum evaporation.
5. **Precipitation** — Add **2-propanol, ethanol, or acetone** (1:1 to 1:3 v/v); xanthan crashes out.
6. **Drying** — Spray drying (inlet 180–200 °C, outlet 80–90 °C) or drum drying.
7. **Milling & sieving** — Final product 80–200 mesh.
8. **Purity:** Food grade ≥ **91%**; pharmaceutical/industrial grade ≥ **98%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU pitfall:** Students often write that nisin production is aerobic. Nisin fermentation is **microaerophilic/anaerobic** (pO₂ < 5%), whereas xanthan gum is **strictly aerobic** (pO₂ > 30%). Confusing this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in ESE.

---

## 2.2 NISIN — Production Process (Step-by-Step Logic)

### A. The Microbial Producer
- **Strain:** *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* (formerly *Streptococcus lactis*).
- **Genetics:** The nisin gene cluster is located on a **conjugative transposon (Tn5276)** — a 70-kb mobile element. It includes structural genes **nisA, nisB, nisC, nisP, nisI, nisF, nisE, nisG, nisR, nisK**.
- **Regulation:** **Quorum-sensing (QS)** controlled — nisin itself acts as the **inducer** of its own biosynthesis (auto-induction).

### B. Fermentation Medium (Typical Composition)

| Component | Concentration (g/L) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Sucrose / Glucose / Whey permeate | **20–30** | Carbon source |
| Yeast extract | **10–20** | Nitrogen + B-vitamins (rich) |
| Tryptone / Peptone | **5–10** | Amino acids |
| KH₂PO₄ / K₂HPO₄ | **5–10** | Buffer |
| NaCl | **0.5–1.0** | Osmotic balance |
| MgSO₄·7H₂O | **0.1–0.2** | Cofactor |
| Tween-80 | **0.1–0.5 mL/L** | Antifoam + membrane fluidizer |
| pH | **6.0–6.8** | Optimal for nisin synthesis |
| Temperature | **30 °C** | Lactic bacterium optimum |
| DO | **< 5% saturation** (anaerobic / microaerobic) | Strict |

> [!NOTE]
> **Nisin vs. Lactic Acid Production Trade-off:** Nisin is a **secondary metabolite** produced after the exponential growth phase. Continuous pH adjustment (with NaOH or CaCO₃) is required to keep the pH ≥ 5.5; otherwise, lactic acid accumulation inhibits both growth and nisin synthetase activity.

### C. Fermentation Strategies
- **Batch:** 12–24 h, titer ~ 200–400 IU/mL.
- **Fed-batch:** Glucose fed at 1–2 g/L/h → titer ~ 1000–2000 IU/mL.
- **Immobilized cell (calcium alginate beads):** Higher cell density, repeated cycles.
- **Solid-state fermentation (SSF):** Using **de Man-Rogosa-Sharpe (MRS)** or wheat bran substrate.

### D. Downstream Processing (Recovery of Nisin)
1. **pH adjustment to 2.0–3.0** (using HCl) — causes nisin adsorption to producer cell surface.
2. **Cell harvesting** — Centrifugation.
3. **Nisin extraction from cells** — **NaCl (0.5–1.0 M)** elution, OR **acidic phosphate buffer (pH 2.0)**.
4. **Precipitation** — Ammonium sulfate saturation (40–60%) or **acid precipitation at pH 4.0**.
5. **Concentration & purification** — Reverse-phase HPLC, cation exchange chromatography, or expanded-bed adsorption.
6. **Drying** — Freeze drying (lyophilization) for heat-sensitive peptide.
7. **Standardization** — Activity expressed as **International Units (IU/mL)**. Commercial products: **Nisaplin™** (2.5% nisin, 10⁶ IU/g).

### E. Mode of Action of Nisin (High-Yield)
- Binds to **Lipid II** (peptidoglycan precursor) on the target bacterial membrane.
- Forms **pores** (4–8 nisin molecules + 3 Lipid II) in the cytoplasmic membrane.
- Causes **efflux of ATP, K⁺, and phosphate** → cell death.
- Most active against **Gram-positive bacteria** (e.g., *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Clostridium botulinum*, *Staphylococcus aureus*).

---

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA & QUANTITATIVE SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all engineering equations, mass balance relations, and conversion metrics essential for ESE numerical/analytical questions on this topic.

| # | Equation / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Xanthan Productivity** | $P_x = \dfrac{C_x^{final} - C_x^{initial}}{t_{fermentation}}$ | g/L·h | Batch yield |
| 2 | **Xanthan Yield on Substrate** | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{P_{xanthan}}{S_{consumed}}$ | **0.50–0.85 g/g** (industrial) | Carbon efficiency |
| 3 | **Specific Nisin Production Rate** | $q_p = \dfrac{1}{C_x}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ | IU/mg cell·h | Secondary metabolism kinetics |
| 4 | **Nisin Activity Conversion** | $1\,\text{IU} = 1\,\text{Nisin Unit (NU)} = 0.025\,\mu g$ pure nisin | 40 IU/µg | Activity assay |
| 5 | **Oxygen Transfer (for Xanthan)** | $OTR = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$ | mol O₂/L·h | Aerobic STR |
| 6 | **Power Number (high viscosity)** | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$ | W (Watt) | Impeller design |
| 7 | **Apparent Viscosity (Xanthan broth)** | $\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)}$ (Power Law) | n = 0.1–0.4 (shear-thinning) | Rheology |
| 8 | **Mass balance on substrate** | $S_0 - S_f = \dfrac{X_f - X_0}{Y_{X/S}} + \dfrac{P}{Y_{P/S}} + mX$ | g/L | Carbon distribution |
| 9 | **Luedeking-Piret (Nisin secondary metabolite)** | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \dfrac{dX}{dt} + \beta X$ | IU/mL·h | Mixed-growth kinetics |
| 10 | **Doubling Time (Lactic bacteria)** | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu_{max}}$ | 0.5–1.5 h | Growth kinetics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical clarification for KTU valuation:** The "**q_p**" equation for nisin shows β (non-growth associated) typically **dominates** over α (growth-associated), confirming nisin is a **secondary metabolite**. For xanthan, the kinetics are mixed-growth, with both α and β significant.

---

## 2.4 REAL-WORLD UTILITY & ENGINEERING SIGNIFICANCE

- **Xanthan gum** is the **gold standard of food hydrocolloids** — used in salad dressings (~75% of all xanthan produced goes to food industry), gluten-free baking, oil-drilling (as a drilling mud viscosifier), cosmetics, and pharmaceutical suspensions.
- **Nisin** is the **only bacteriocin approved as a food preservative in over 50 countries**. Used in pasteurized cheese spreads, canned soups, liquid egg products, and beer (replacing nitrate preservatives).
- Both products represent **"white biotechnology"** success stories — high-value, low-volume, low-energy bioprocesses that replace energy-intensive chemical synthesis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Nisin Activity Calculation (Typical ESE 7-Mark Style)

### Problem Statement
A fermentation broth contains **400 IU/mL** of nisin. Calculate the **mass concentration of pure nisin in µg/mL and mg/L**, and the **total mass in a 10,000 L production batch** if downstream processing achieves a **60% recovery efficiency**.

### Given Data
- Nisin activity $A = 400 \, \text{IU/mL}$
- Conversion factor $1\,\text{IU} = 0.025\,\mu g$ pure nisin
- Recovery efficiency $\eta = 60\% = 0.60$
- Batch volume $V = 10{,}000\,\text{L} = 10^7\,\text{mL}$

### Step 1 — Convert Activity to Mass Concentration

The mass concentration of pure nisin in the broth is:

$$C_{nisin} = A \times 0.025 \, \frac{\mu g}{IU}$$

Substituting the given value of activity:

$$C_{nisin} = 400 \, \text{IU/mL} \times 0.025 \, \mu g/\text{IU}$$

$$C_{nisin} = 10 \, \mu g/\text{mL}$$

**Interpretation:** Each milliliter of fermentation broth contains 10 micrograms of pure nisin.

### Step 2 — Convert to mg/L (Common Reporting Unit)

Since $1\,\text{mg} = 1000\,\mu g$ and $1\,\text{L} = 1000\,\text{mL}$:

$$C_{nisin} = 10 \, \mu g/\text{mL} \times \frac{1000 \, mL}{1 \, L} \times \frac{1 \, mg}{1000 \, \mu g}$$

$$C_{nisin} = 10 \, mg/L$$

**Interpretation:** The broth contains 10 mg of pure nisin per liter.

### Step 3 — Compute Total Mass in the Batch (Pre-Recovery)

The total mass of nisin present in the fermenter *before* downstream processing is:

$$M_{total} = C_{nisin} \times V$$

$$M_{total} = 10 \, \text{mg/L} \times 10{,}000 \, \text{L}$$

$$M_{total} = 100{,}000 \, \text{mg} = 100 \, \text{g}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Downstream Recovery Efficiency

The actual mass of purified nisin recovered is:

$$M_{recovered} = \eta \times M_{total}$$

$$M_{recovered} = 0.60 \times 100 \, g$$

$$M_{recovered} = 60 \, g$$

### Step 5 — Final Reported Answer

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Pure nisin in broth | **10 µg/mL** |
| Equivalent | **10 mg/L** |
| Total in 10,000 L batch (pre-recovery) | **100 g** |
| Final recovered (60% efficiency) | **60 g** |

---

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Xanthan Yield Coefficient & Mass Balance

### Problem Statement
A stirred-tank fermenter is charged with **50 g/L of glucose** as the initial carbon source. After **72 hours**, the residual glucose is **2.5 g/L**, the cell dry weight is **5.2 g/L**, and the xanthan gum concentration is **22.0 g/L**. Compute (i) the substrate consumed, (ii) the yield coefficient of xanthan on substrate $Y_{P/S}$, (iii) the yield coefficient of cells on substrate $Y_{X/S}$, and (iv) the maintenance coefficient $m$ (assume $\alpha = 0.1$ for cell-to-xanthan coupled synthesis).

### Given Data
- $S_0 = 50 \, g/L$, $S_f = 2.5 \, g/L$, $X_f = 5.2 \, g/L$, $P = 22.0 \, g/L$, $\alpha = 0.1 \, g_{xanthan}/g_{cell}$.

### Step 1 — Substrate Consumed

The total glucose consumed by the cells for growth, maintenance, and xanthan synthesis is:

$$-\Delta S = S_0 - S_f = 50 - 2.5 = 47.5 \, g/L$$

### Step 2 — Xanthan Yield on Substrate $Y_{P/S}$

The mass of xanthan produced per gram of substrate consumed is:

$$Y_{P/S} = \frac{P}{-\Delta S} = \frac{22.0}{47.5}$$

$$Y_{P/S} = 0.463 \, g/g$$

**Interpretation:** For every gram of glucose consumed, the cells synthesize 0.463 g of xanthan gum.

### Step 3 — Cell Yield on Substrate $Y_{X/S}$

The cell mass produced per gram of substrate consumed:

$$Y_{X/S} = \frac{X_f - X_0}{-\Delta S} = \frac{5.2 - 0}{47.5} = 0.109 \, g/g$$

### Step 4 — Verification Using Carbon Distribution Mass Balance

The Herberle-Marr-type balance for xanthan fermentation is:

$$-\Delta S = \frac{\Delta X}{Y_{X/S}} + \frac{P}{Y_{P/S}} + m \cdot X_f \cdot t$$

Substituting the values for $\Delta X$ and $P$:

$$\frac{\Delta X}{Y_{X/S}} = \frac{5.2}{0.109} = 47.7 \, g/L$$

$$\frac{P}{Y_{P/S}} = \frac{22.0}{0.463} = 47.5 \, g/L$$

Note that growth and product account for the majority of the carbon. The maintenance term (last term) is typically small but the formula balances.

---

## 3.3 ANALYTICAL COMPARISON MATRIX (Xanthan vs. Nisin)

| Feature | Xanthan Gum | Nisin |
|---|---|---|
| Product Class | Exopolysaccharide | Lantibiotic (Class I bacteriocin) |
| Producer | *X. campestris* | *L. lactis* |
| Type of Metabolism | Primary metabolite (growth-associated) | **Secondary metabolite** (non-growth associated) |
| Oxygen Requirement | **Strictly aerobic** | Anaerobic / Microaerophilic |
| pH | Neutral (6.8–7.2) | Mildly acidic (6.0–6.8) |
| Temperature | 28–30 °C | 30 °C |
| Viscosity of Broth | **Very high** (shear-thinning) | Low (typical microbial broth) |
| Key Recovery Step | Alcohol precipitation | Acid + salt precipitation + HPLC |
| Regulatory Approval | FDA GRAS 1969; E415 | FDA GRAS 1988; E234 |
| Typical Application | Food thickener, oil-drilling | Food preservative (anti-Listeria) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

---

## 4.1 MERMAID FLOWCHART — Industrial Xanthan Gum Production Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material: Glucose / Corn Syrup] --> B[Media Preparation Tank pH 7.0]
    B --> C[Sterilization 121 C 20 min]
    C --> D[Seed Inoculum X. campestris 18-24 h culture]
    D --> E[Aerated Stirred Tank Reactor 28-30 C pH 7.0 0.5 vvm]
    E --> F[48-96 h Fermentation High Viscosity Broth]
    F --> G[Heat Pasteurization 80-95 C 15 min]
    G --> H[Cell Removal Centrifugation / Microfiltration]
    H --> I[Protease Treatment Protein Hydrolysis]
    I --> J[Ultrafiltration Concentration 10 kDa cutoff]
    J --> K[Alcohol Precipitation Isopropanol 1:3 v/v]
    K --> L[Filter Cake Recovery]
    L --> M[Drying Spray Dryer 180 C inlet]
    M --> N[Milling and Sieving 80-200 mesh]
    N --> O[Quality Control QC Rheology Assay]
    O --> P[Final Xanthan Gum Powder Food / Industrial Grade]
```

---

## 4.2 MERMAID FLOWCHART — Industrial Nisin Production Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material: Whey Permeate / Sucrose] --> B[Media Prep pH 6.5]
    B --> C[Sterilization 121 C 15 min]
    C --> D[Seed Inoculum L. lactis 1 percent v/v]
    D --> E[Anaerobic / Microaerobic STR 30 C pH 6.0]
    E --> F[12-24 h Fermentation Secondary Metabolite]
    F --> G[pH Adjustment to 2.5 Acid Shock]
    G --> H[Cell Harvesting Centrifugation]
    H --> I[Nisin Elution 0.5 M NaCl]
    I --> J[Ammonium Sulfate Precipitation 60 percent]
    J --> K[Dialysis and RP-HPLC Purification]
    K --> L[Lyophilization Freeze Drying]
    L --> M[Activity Assay IU per mL]
    M --> N[Standardization to 10 to the 6 IU per g]
    N --> O[Final Nisaplin Type Product E234]
```

---

## 4.3 MERMAID BLOCK DIAGRAM — Mechanism of Nisin Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Target Gram Positive Cell] --> B[Lipid II on Membrane]
    B --> C[4-8 Nisin Molecules Bind Lipid II]
    C --> D[Conformational Change Pyrophosphate Cage]
    D --> E[Pore Formation 1-2 nm Diameter]
    E --> F[Efflux of K plus and ATP]
    F --> G[Membrane Depolarization]
    G --> H[Cell Death within 30-60 min]
```

---

## 4.4 MERMAID SCHEMATIC — Xanthan Gum Molecular Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cellulose Backbone beta 1 4 D Glucose] --> B[Trisaccharide Side Chain]
    B --> C[Mannose beta 1 4 Glucuronic Acid beta 1 2 Mannose alpha 1 3]
    C --> D[Acetyl Group on Inner Mannose]
    C --> E[Pyruvate Ketal on Terminal Mannose]
    D --> F[Anionic Negative Charge]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Double Helix in Solution]
    G --> H[High Viscosity Even at Low Concentration 0.1 percent]
```

---

## 4.5 MERMAID ARCHITECTURE — Process Comparison: Xanthan vs. Nisin

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph XG [XANTHAN GUM PRODUCTION]
        X1[Aerobic Fermentation] --> X2[High Viscosity]
        X2 --> X3[Alcohol Precipitation]
        X3 --> X4[Food Grade Powder]
    end
    subgraph NS [NISIN PRODUCTION]
        N1[Anaerobic Fermentation] --> N2[Low Viscosity]
        N2 --> N3[Acid + Salt Extraction]
        N3 --> N4[HPLC Purification]
        N4 --> N5[Lyophilized Peptide Powder]
    end
    X4 --> AP[Application: Thickener]
    N5 --> AP2[Application: Preservative]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Q1. Define xanthan gum. Mention the producing microorganism and any two industrial applications. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Xanthan gum is a high-molecular-weight extracellular heteropolysaccharide produced by the Gram-negative bacterium *Xanthomonas campestris* pv. campestris under aerobic submerged fermentation. Its repeating unit is a pentasaccharide consisting of a cellulose backbone (β-1,4-D-glucose) with a trisaccharide side chain containing D-mannose, D-glucuronic acid, and a terminal pyruvylated mannose. **Applications:** (i) Thickener and stabilizer in salad dressings, ice cream, and gluten-free baked goods. (ii) Viscosifier in oil-drilling muds and hydraulic fracturing fluids. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. What is nisin? Why is it classified as a lantibiotic? `[KTU University Exam - December 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Nisin is a 34-amino-acid antimicrobial peptide (bacteriocin) produced by *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis*. It is classified as a **lantibiotic (Class I bacteriocin)** because it contains the unusual thioether amino acids **lanthionine** and **methyl-lanthionine**, which are introduced post-translationally by the enzymes NisB (dehydratase) and NisC (cyclase). These crosslinks give nisin its characteristic polycyclic, heat-stable structure. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS WITH INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4]`

**Q. (a)** Describe in detail the **industrial production process of Xanthan gum** with reference to the producing organism, fermentation medium, process parameters, and downstream recovery. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Producer organism (1.5 marks):** *Xanthomonas campestris* pv. *campestris* is a Gram-negative, aerobic, rod-shaped bacterium, GRAS-affirmed equivalent (industrial strain NRRL B-1459). It produces a yellow pigment (xanthomonadin).

**(ii) Fermentation medium (2 marks):** Typical batch medium contains glucose/sucrose (2–4%), yeast extract (0.2%), (NH₄)₂HPO₄, KH₂PO₄ buffer, MgSO₄, and trace elements. pH is adjusted to **6.8–7.2**, sterilized at **121 °C for 20 min**.

**(iii) Process parameters (2 marks):** Production is carried out in a **stirred-tank reactor (50–200 m³)** at **28–30 °C**, **0.5–1.0 vvm aeration**, **150–300 rpm agitation**, and pH controlled at **7.0 ± 0.2** with NaOH/KOH. The fermentation lasts **48–96 h**, achieving titers of **20–30 g/L** (industrial scale 25–50 g/L with fed-batch).

**(iv) Downstream processing (1.5 marks):** Includes pasteurization (80–95 °C), cell removal by centrifugation, protease treatment for protein hydrolysis, ultrafiltration, alcohol precipitation with 2-propanol (1:3 v/v), spray drying, milling, and sieving. Final purity ≥ 91% (food grade) or ≥ 98% (industrial grade).

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **molecular structure and rheological properties of xanthan gum** that make it suitable as a food hydrocolloid. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Chemical structure (3 marks):** Xanthan is a pentasaccharide repeating unit with a β-1,4-D-glucose backbone (same as cellulose). The trisaccharide side chain is β-D-Man-(1,4)-β-D-GlcA-(1,2)-α-D-Man-(1,3)-linked to alternate backbone glucoses. The terminal mannose is partially substituted with **pyruvate ketal** (3–6%), and the inner mannose carries an **O-acetyl group** (~5%). This gives the molecule a **net anionic charge** and a stiff, rod-like conformation.

**(ii) Rheological properties (2 marks):** Xanthan solutions are highly pseudoplastic and **shear-thinning** (Power Law index n ≈ 0.1–0.4). Even at concentrations of **0.1–0.5%**, the apparent viscosity can exceed 1000 mPa·s. Solutions are stable over a wide pH (3–11), temperature (–18 to 100 °C), and salt concentration range — much more robust than alginates or carrageenans.

**(iii) Functional attributes as a food hydrocolloid (2 marks):** These properties make xanthan gum an ideal **thickener, stabilizer, emulsifier, and suspending agent** in sauces, bakery fillings, and dairy desserts. It also provides **mouthfeel, freeze-thaw stability**, and synergizes with guar gum and locust bean gum to form thermoreversible gels.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - December 2023, Module 4]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the **production of nisin by *Lactococcus lactis*** covering the producing organism, genetic regulation, fermentation medium, process control, and recovery methods. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Producer and genetics (2 marks):** *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* (a Gram-positive lactic acid bacterium) is the main commercial producer. The nisin gene cluster is located on the conjugative transposon **Tn5276** and includes structural gene *nisA*, modification genes *nisB* and *nisC*, transporter *nisT*, regulator *nisR*, histidine kinase *nisK*, and immunity genes *nisI, nisF, nisE, nisG*. Nisin acts as the **auto-inducer (quorum-sensing molecule)** via a two-component regulatory system NisK-NisR.

**(ii) Fermentation medium (1.5 marks):** Standard media use **whey permeate or sucrose (2–3%)** as carbon source, **yeast extract (1–2%)** as nitrogen/vitamin source, peptone, KH₂PO₄, NaCl, MgSO₄, and Tween-80. Initial pH **6.0–6.8**, temperature **30 °C**.

**(iii) Process control (2 marks):** Fermentation is **anaerobic or microaerobic** (DO < 5% saturation) in a stirred-tank reactor. Continuous **pH control at 6.0–6.5** with NaOH or CaCO₃ is critical to prevent lactic acid inhibition. Duration **12–24 h** in batch; fed-batch with glucose feed **1–2 g/L/h** gives titers of **1000–2000 IU/mL**.

**(iv) Recovery (1.5 marks):** Cells are harvested, subjected to **acid shock (pH 2.0–3.0)** to release adsorbed nisin, then eluted with **0.5–1.0 M NaCl**, precipitated with **60% ammonium sulfate**, purified by **RP-HPLC or cation exchange chromatography**, and **freeze-dried** to obtain a heat-sensitive peptide in stable powder form (Nisaplin™, 10⁶ IU/g).

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the **mode of action of nisin** as an antimicrobial peptide. Why is nisin not effective against Gram-negative bacteria? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Mode of action (4 marks):**
- **Step 1 — Recognition:** Nisin binds with high affinity to **Lipid II**, the membrane-bound peptidoglycan precursor in the target cell. This binding occurs via the nisin N-terminal rings A and B.
- **Step 2 — Pore formation:** Approximately **4–8 nisin molecules** plus **3 Lipid II** molecules assemble to form a defined **transmembrane pore** of 1–2 nm diameter.
- **Step 3 — Efflux:** Pore formation causes leakage of intracellular ions (K⁺), ATP, and phosphate, dissipating the proton-motive force.
- **Step 4 — Cell death:** Membrane depolarization and ATP depletion lead to rapid bacterial cell death within 30–60 min. Nisin also inhibits peptidoglycan biosynthesis by sequestering Lipid II from the transglycosylation reaction.

**(ii) Ineffectiveness against Gram-negative bacteria (3 marks):**
Gram-negative bacteria are protected by their **outer membrane (OM)**, which contains **lipopolysaccharide (LPS)** and porins. This OM acts as a permeability barrier, preventing nisin (a relatively large, hydrophobic peptide) from reaching the inner cytoplasmic membrane where its target (Lipid II) resides. Strategies to overcome this include combining nisin with **EDTA (chelates Mg²⁺, destabilizes LPS)** or with **high-pressure processing / sub-lethal heat shock**, which permeabilize the OM and sensitize Gram-negative cells.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing aeration requirements** — Writing "nisin fermentation is aerobic" → loss of **1 mark**.
> 2. **Wrong class for nisin** — Calling nisin a "Class II bacteriocin" instead of **Class I (lantibiotic)** → loss of **1 mark**.
> 3. **Incomplete downstream processing** — Stating "centrifuge and dry" without specifying **alcohol precipitation (xanthan)** or **acid + salt elution (nisin)** → loss of **2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting pyruvate / acetyl modifications** in xanthan structure → loss of **1 mark**.
> 5. **Neglecting pH control** in nisin process — Failure to mention that uncontrolled lactic acid accumulation inhibits nisin synthesis → loss of **1 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing GRAS status years** — Always verify FDA approval: Xanthan 1969, Nisin 1988.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Xanthan gum** is a **heteropolysaccharide** (pentasaccharide repeating unit) produced **aerobically** by ***Xanthomonas campestris***.
- [x] **Nisin** is a **34-aa lantibiotic** (Class I bacteriocin) produced **anaerobically/microaerophilically** by ***Lactococcus lactis***.
- [x] Xanthan is a **growth-associated primary metabolite**; Nisin is a **secondary metabolite** (auto-induced by quorum sensing).
- [x] Xanthan structure: β-1,4-glucose backbone + mannose-glucuronic acid-mannose side chain with **O-acetyl and pyruvyl modifications**.
- [x] Nisin structure contains **lanthionine** and **methyl-lanthionine** thioether crosslinks.
- [x] Xanthan fermentation: 28–30 °C, pH 7.0, 0.5–1.0 vvm, 48–96 h, titer 25–50 g/L.
- [x] Nisin fermentation: 30 °C, pH 6.0–6.5, anaerobic, 12–24 h, titer 400–2000 IU/mL.
- [x] Xanthan recovery: **alcohol (isopropanol) precipitation + spray drying**.
- [x] Nisin recovery: **acid shock + NaCl elution + (NH₄)₂SO₄ precipitation + HPLC + lyophilization**.
- [x] Nisin mode of action: **Lipid II binding → pore formation → ATP efflux → cell death**.
- [x] Nisin ineffective on Gram-negatives due to **outer membrane / LPS barrier**.
- [x] Xanthan applications: food thickener, oil-drilling mud, pharmaceutical suspension.
- [x] Nisin applications: food preservative (cheese, canned food, E234 in EU).
- [x] Both products are **GRAS-approved by FDA** (Xanthan 1969; Nisin 1988).
- [x] **Viscosity of xanthan broth is the key engineering challenge** (shear-thinning pseudoplastic, requires high-power agitators).
- [x] **Nisin uses quorum-sensing auto-induction** (NisK-NisR two-component system).
- [x] **Conversion: 1 IU nisin = 0.025 µg pure peptide = 40 IU/µg standard.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biofertilizer (Rhizobium, Azotobacter) and Biopesticide (Bacillus thuringiensis)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBTT304 - INDUSTRIAL BIOPROCESS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biofertilizer (Rhizobium, Azotobacter) and Biopesticide (Bacillus thuringiensis)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Biofertilizers — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Biofertilizers** are biologically active **microbial inoculants** (bacteria, fungi, or algae) that, upon application to seeds, plant surfaces, or soil, colonize the **rhizosphere** (the soil region immediately surrounding plant roots) or the plant interior, and promote plant growth by **fixing atmospheric nitrogen**, **solubilizing phosphorus**, or **synthesizing plant growth-promoting substances (PGPS)** such as auxins, gibberellins, and cytokinins.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, biofertilizers are positioned as sustainable, eco-friendly alternatives to chemical fertilizers under the broader umbrella of **Industrial Bioprocess Technology**, where the focus is on the **upstream selection of strain**, **fermentation production strategy**, **formulation with carrier materials**, and **shelf-life enhancement**.

### 1.2 Biopesticides — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biopesticides** are formulations containing natural enemies of pests (microorganisms, plant extracts, or biochemicals) that control pests by non-toxic, target-specific mechanisms. **Microbial biopesticides** specifically use bacteria, fungi, viruses, or protozoa. *Bacillus thuringiensis* (Bt) is the most commercially significant **bacterial biopesticide**, producing **insecticidal crystal (Cry) proteins** during sporulation.

### 1.3 The "Why" — Real-World Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Biofertilizer as a "Free Lunch Factory":**
Imagine a plant sitting in soil. The atmosphere is **78% nitrogen (N₂)**, but the plant is literally *starving* for nitrogen because it cannot break the strong triple bond of N₂. A chemical fertilizer (urea) is like buying a pre-made protein shake. A biofertilizer like *Rhizobium*, however, is like hiring a **microscopic chef that lives inside the root of the plant** and cooks nitrogen-rich food **24/7, free of cost**, in exchange for sugars from photosynthesis. The plant provides **photosynthate (sugar)**, the bacterium provides **fixed nitrogen** — a perfect **mutualistic symbiosis** (think of bees and flowers, or the remora fish and shark).

**Analogy 2 — *Azotobacter* as a "Free-Range Soil Helper":**
Unlike *Rhizobium* which needs a host plant, *Azotobacter* is a **free-living, aerobic soil bacterium** that works **independently** in the rhizosphere. Think of it as a freelance chef wandering through the soil, fixing nitrogen wherever it finds enough carbon (root exudates) and oxygen to survive. It also bakes **plant growth hormones (IAA, gibberellins)** as a bonus side-dish.

**Analogy 3 — Bt as a "Biological Smart-Bomb":**
*Bacillus thuringiensis* produces **crystal proteins (Cry toxins)** shaped like molecular keys. These keys are **inert until ingested by an insect**. Inside the insect's **highly alkaline gut (pH 10–12)**, the keys are activated, attach only to **specific receptors in the gut lining of susceptible insects** (like caterpillars), punch **holes in the gut cells**, and the insect dies of **sepsis/ starvation** within 2–3 days. Humans, birds, fish, and beneficial insects are unaffected because:
- Their stomachs are acidic (pH 1.5–3.5) — toxin stays inert
- They lack the specific receptor proteins
- This is **target specificity** — Bt is a *sniper*, not a *grenade*.

### 1.4 Key Classification (KTU-Ready Snapshot)

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Biofertilizers (Nitrogen-Fixers Focus):**
> 1. **Symbiotic N₂-fixers** — *Rhizobium*, *Bradyrhizobium*, *Azorhizobium*, *Sinorhizobium* (form nodules in legumes)
> 2. **Associative Symbiotic N₂-fixers** — *Azospirillum* (with cereals/grasses)
> 3. **Asymbiotic (Free-Living) N₂-fixers** — *Azotobacter*, *Clostridium*, *Cyanobacteria (Anabaena, Nostoc)*
> 4. **Phosphate Solubilizers** — *Bacillus megaterium*, *Pseudomonas striata*, *Aspergillus awamori*
> 5. **Phosphate Mobilizers (Mycorrhizae)** — *Glomus* spp., *Gigaspora* spp.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Metric (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - *Rhizobium* can fix **50–200 kg N/ha/year** in legume fields
> - *Azotobacter* can fix **15–20 kg N/ha/year**
> - Bt formulations typically contain **5–10% w/w active Cry protein**, applied at **0.5–2 kg/ha**
> - Standard carrier: **sterile peat / lignite powder** with **40% moisture**, **10⁸–10⁹ cells/g**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 *Rhizobium* — Symbiotic Nitrogen Fixer

#### 2.1.1 Taxonomy & Physiology

> *Rhizobium* is a **Gram-negative**, **rod-shaped** (0.5–0.9 µm × 1.2–3.0 µm), **motile** bacterium belonging to the family **Rhizobiaceae** (Class *Alphaproteobacteria*). It is **aerobic** in free-living state but functions as a **microaerophilic** bacteroid inside the nodule.

**Major Species-Host Specificity (Cross-Inoculation Groups):**

| Rhizobium Species | Host Legume | Common Name |
|---|---|---|
| *R. leguminosarum* bv. *viciae* | Pea, lentil, vetch | Pea group |
| *R. leguminosarum* bv. *phaseoli* | Common bean | Bean group |
| *R. leguminosarum* bv. *trifolii* | Clover | Clover group |
| *R. meliloti* | Alfalfa, sweet clover | Alfalfa group |
| *R. loti* | Lotus, trefoil | Lotus group |
| *Bradyrhizobium japonicum* | Soybean | Soybean group |
| *Bradyrhizobium* spp. | Cowpea, groundnut | Cowpea group |

#### 2.1.2 The Nodulation Process (Stepwise Logic)

The KTU-expected stepwise mechanism is the **"Infection Thread Pathway"**:

**Step 1 — Recognition & Chemoattraction:**
Legume roots secrete **flavonoids** (e.g., luteolin, genistein, daidzein) into the rhizosphere. These flavonoids act as **chemical signals** that activate the bacterial **nod** (*nodulation*) genes.

**Step 2 — Nod Factor Synthesis:**
The activated *nod* genes produce **NodD protein** (a transcriptional activator), which triggers expression of other *nod* genes → synthesis of **lipo-chitooligosaccharide (LCO) Nod factors**.

**Step 3 — Root Hair Curling:**
Nod factors cause **deformation and curling** of root hairs, trapping the bacteria in a "shepherd's crook" structure.

**Step 4 — Infection Thread Formation:**
The plant cell wall invaginates, forming an **infection thread** — a tubular channel that guides bacteria into the root cortex.

**Step 5 — Nodule Organogenesis:**
Bacteria are released into cortical cells, surrounded by a **peribacteroid membrane** (plant-derived). Bacteria differentiate into **bacteroids** — the N₂-fixing form.

**Step 6 — Nitrogen Fixation:**
Bacteroids contain the **nitrogenase enzyme complex**, which catalyzes:

$$N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- + 16\,ATP \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrogenase}} 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16\,ADP + 16\,P_i$$

The **leghemoglobin** (plant-synthesized, O₂-binding protein) maintains the **microaerobic condition (pO₂ ~ 10 nM)** essential for nitrogenase, which is **oxygen-sensitive** (irreversibly denatured by O₂).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does nitrogenase need 16 ATP per N₂?**
> The triple bond of N₂ (bond energy **945 kJ/mol**) requires:
> - 2 electrons for each of the 4 reduction steps (8 e⁻ total)
> - 2 ATP hydrolyzed per electron transferred (16 ATP total)
> - Therefore, **N₂ fixation is energetically expensive** — this is why the legume pays the bacterium in photosynthate (sugar).

**Step 7 — Ammonia Assimilation:**
The plant converts bacteroid-produced NH₃ into **glutamine** and **asparagine** via the **GS-GOGAT pathway** (Glutamine Synthetase – Glutamine Oxoglutarate Aminotransferase).

#### 2.1.3 Industrial Production of *Rhizobium* Biofertilizer

**Standard Production Medium — Yeast Extract Mannitol (YEM) Broth:**

| Component | Concentration (g/L) |
|---|---|
| Mannitol (C-source) | 10.0 |
| Yeast extract (N-source, vitamins) | 1.0 |
| K₂HPO₄ | 0.5 |
| MgSO₄·7H₂O | 0.2 |
| NaCl | 0.1 |
| CaCO₃ | 1.0 |
| Distilled water | to 1000 mL |

**Production Flow:**
$$\text{Pure Culture} \rightarrow \text{Mother Culture} \rightarrow \text{Seed/Pre-inoculum} \rightarrow \text{Production Fermenter (aerobic, 28–30°C, 3–7 days)} \rightarrow \text{Cell Harvest (Centrifugation)} \rightarrow \text{Mixing with Sterile Carrier (Peat/Lignite)} \rightarrow \text{Packaging}$$

**Critical Quality Parameters:**
- Final product: **≥ 10⁸ cells/g** of carrier
- Moisture: **30–40%**
- pH: **6.5–7.5**
- Contamination: **< 0.1%**
- Shelf life: **6 months at 25°C** (extends to **12 months at 4°C**)

### 2.2 *Azotobacter* — Asymbiotic Nitrogen Fixer

#### 2.2.1 Taxonomy & Physiology

> *Azotobacter* is a **Gram-negative**, **pleomorphic** (cocci to short rods, 1.5–2.0 µm), **strictly aerobic**, **free-living** soil bacterium. Family *Azotobacteriaceae* (Class *Gammaproteobacteria*).

**Key Species:**
- *A. chroococcum* — most common in arable soils
- *A. vinelandii* — used in lab studies (model organism for N₂ fixation)
- *A. beijerinckii*, *A. paspali*, *A. armeniacus*

**Unique Features:**
- Forms **cysts** (dormant, desiccation-resistant resting stage) — unlike *Rhizobium*
- Produces **water-soluble brown-black pigments** (melanin-like) in old cultures
- High **respiratory rate** (consumes O₂ rapidly to protect nitrogenase)
- Can fix N₂ across a wide pH range (4.8–8.5)

#### 2.2.2 Mechanisms of Plant Growth Promotion by *Azotobacter*

1. **Nitrogen Fixation:** 15–20 kg N/ha/year
2. **Indole-3-Acetic Acid (IAA) Production:** Tryptophan → IAA pathway
3. **Gibberellin & Cytokinin Synthesis**
4. **Phosphate Solubilization** (weak organic acids)
5. **Siderophore Production** (iron chelation → biocontrol)
6. **Antifungal Metabolites** (e.g., anisomycin, phenazines) — biocontrol of *Fusarium*, *Alternaria*

#### 2.2.3 Industrial Production — Ashby's Medium

| Component | Concentration (g/L) |
|---|---|
| Mannitol | 20.0 |
| K₂HPO₄ | 0.2 |
| MgSO₄·7H₂O | 0.2 |
| NaCl | 0.2 |
| K₂SO₄ | 0.1 |
| CaCO₃ | 5.0 |
| Distilled water | to 1000 mL |

**Why CaCO₃ at 5 g/L?** It buffers the medium to **pH 7.0–7.5** (optimal for *Azotobacter*) and prevents acidification from organic acid byproducts.

**Production Conditions:**
- Temperature: **28–30°C**
- Aeration: Vigorous (high O₂ demand, but paradoxically protects nitrogenase via respiratory protection)
- Fermentation time: **3–5 days**
- Final titre: **10⁸–10⁹ CFU/mL**

### 2.3 *Bacillus thuringiensis* (Bt) — The Flagship Biopesticide

#### 2.3.1 Taxonomy & Unique Physiology

> *Bacillus thuringiensis* is a **Gram-positive**, **spore-forming**, **facultative anaerobic** bacterium of the family *Bacillaceae* (Class *Bacilli*). It is **phylogenetically nearly identical to *B. cereus*** but distinguished by the production of a **parasporal crystal inclusion (Cry protein)** during sporulation.

**Discovery Timeline:**
- 1901 — Isolated from silkworm by **Ishiwata** (Japan) as *Bacillus sotto*
- 1911 — **Ernst Berliner** re-isolated from *Ephestia kühniella* (Mediterranean flour moth) in Thuringia, Germany — hence the name *thuringiensis*
- 1938 — First commercial product: **Sporeine** (France)

#### 2.3.2 The Cry Toxin — Structure & Classification

**Cry toxins** are **parasporal crystalline inclusions** comprising **protoxins** of **65–140 kDa**. They are classified based on **amino acid sequence homology** into:

| Toxin Family | Target Insect Order | Example Strains |
|---|---|---|
| Cry1 | Lepidoptera (caterpillars) | *Bt kurstaki* HD-1 |
| Cry2 | Lepidoptera & Diptera | *Bt kurstaki* |
| Cry3 | Coleoptera (beetles) | *Bt tenebrionis* (now *Bt morrisoni*) |
| Cry4 | Diptera (mosquito larvae) | *Bt israelensis* |
| Cry11 | Diptera | *Bt israelensis* |
| Cry5, Cry12, Cry13, Cry14 | Nematodes | Various |
| Cyt1, Cyt2 | Diptera (cytolytic) | *Bt israelensis* |

**Domains of a typical Cry1 toxin (3-domain structure):**
- **Domain I** — Helix bundle, **pore-formation** (lytic)
- **Domain II** — β-prism, **receptor binding**
- **Domain III** — β-sandwich, **receptor binding & specificity**

#### 2.3.3 Mode of Action — The Sequential "Smart-Bomb" Kill

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Ingestion:** Susceptible insect larva feeds on Bt-treated foliage/spores.
>
> **Step 2 — Solubilization:** In the **alkaline midgut (pH 10–12)**, the crystal dissolves, releasing protoxin.
>
> **Step 3 — Proteolytic Activation:** Gut proteases (trypsin/chymotrypsin) cleave the protoxin → **active toxin (~60 kDa)**.
>
> **Step 4 — Receptor Binding:** Activated toxin binds to **specific cadherin-like receptors (BtR)** on the **apical microvilli** of midgut epithelial cells.
>
> **Step 5 — Pore Formation:** Domain I inserts into the membrane → forms **cation-selective pores** → causes **osmotic lysis** of epithelial cells.
>
> **Step 6 — Septicemia & Starvation:** Gut barrier breached → gut bacteria enter hemocoel → insect dies in **24–72 hours**.

**Why is Bt safe for humans?**
- Human stomach pH is **1.5–3.5** (acidic) → crystal does NOT solubilize
- Humans lack **BtR cadherin receptors** on gut cells
- Cry toxins are degraded by **gut proteases** in mammals
- WHO classifies Bt as **Category III (slightly hazardous)**, EPA as **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)**

#### 2.3.4 Industrial Production of Bt Biopesticide

**Two Production Routes:**

| Method | Scale | Yield | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Submerged Fermentation (SmF)** | Industrial (10,000–100,000 L) | 5–10 g/L biomass | Standard, well-controlled |
| **Solid-State Fermentation (SSF)** | Pilot/Small-scale | 15–25 g/kg substrate | Cheaper, used in developing countries |

**Standard SmF Medium (Modified Glucose-Yeast Extract):**

| Component | Concentration (g/L) |
|---|---|---|
| Glucose | 10.0 |
| Yeast extract | 5.0 |
| Peptone | 5.0 |
| K₂HPO₄ | 1.0 |
| MgSO₄·7H₂O | 0.5 |
| MnSO₄·H₂O | 0.05 |
| CaCl₂·2H₂O | 0.05 |
| FeSO₄·7H₂O | 0.01 |
| Distilled water | to 1000 mL |

**Production Conditions:**
- Temperature: **30 ± 1°C**
- pH: **7.0 (start) → 8.5 (end)**
- Aeration: **0.5–1.0 vvm**
- Agitation: **200–300 rpm**
- Sporulation begins at **~18–24 h**, completes by **48–72 h**
- Final product: **spore-crystal complex** (2–4 × 10⁹ spores/mL)

**Formulation Types:**
1. **Wettable Powder (WP)** — technical powder + wetting agent + carrier
2. **Aqueous Suspension Concentrate (SC)** — 10–30% active ingredient
3. **Emulsifiable Concentrate (EC)** — oil-based
4. **Granules (GR)** — corn cob / starch carriers
5. **Transgenic plants (Bt cotton, Bt corn)** — plant expresses Cry toxin endogenously

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are exam-critical for KTU 2024. Memorize the units and constants exactly.**

| # | Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | N₂ Reduction by Nitrogenase | $N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- + 16\,ATP \rightarrow 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16\,ADP + 16\,P_i$ | 16 ATP per N₂; **8 e⁻ per N₂** |
| 2 | Cell Doubling Time | $t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu_{max}} = \frac{0.693}{\mu_{max}}$ | hours; $\mu_{max}$ in h⁻¹ |
| 3 | Specific Growth Rate | $\mu = \frac{\ln N_t - \ln N_0}{t}$ | h⁻¹ |
| 4 | Biomass Yield Coefficient | $Y_{x/s} = \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | g biomass per g substrate |
| 5 | Volumetric Productivity | $P = \frac{X_{final} - X_{initial}}{t}$ | g/L/h |
| 6 | CFU Count (Plate Dilution) | $CFU/mL = \frac{\text{Colony Count} \times \text{Dilution Factor}}{\text{Volume Plated (mL)}}$ | — |
| 7 | Henderson–Hasselbalch (Buffer pH) | $pH = pK_a + \log\frac{[\text{salt}]}{[\text{acid}]}$ | pH of carrier slurry |
| 8 | Moisture Content (Carrier) | $\%\,MC = \frac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$ | Standard 30–40% |
| 9 | Q₁₀ (Temperature Coefficient) | $Q_{10} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | 2.0–2.5 for biological reactions |
| 10 | Shelf-life (10% rule) | $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{decay}}$ | log-linear death kinetics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carriers in Biofertilizer Formulation:** Peat, lignite, charcoal, vermiculite, talc, rice husk, **sterilized at 121°C for 30 min** to eliminate native microbial contamination.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sustainable Agriculture:** Replaces 20–30% of chemical N-fertilizer use in legume cropping systems → **reduces groundwater nitrate pollution**.
- **IPM (Integrated Pest Management):** Bt is the **cornerstone** of organic farming certification worldwide; > **90% of microbial biopesticide market** is Bt-based.
- **Transgenic Bt Crops:** **Bt cotton** (expressing Cry1Ac) has reduced insecticide use in India by **~37%** since 2002 (per ICRISAT data).
- **Industrial Fermentation:** Bt production uses the **same stainless-steel fermenters** as antibiotic industry (10–100 m³), demonstrating scalability of biological manufacturing.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energetics of Biological N₂ Fixation

The conversion of atmospheric N₂ to ammonia is endergonic under standard conditions, but is driven by **ATP hydrolysis** coupled to the **nitrogenase enzyme complex**.

#### Step 1: Write the Half-Reactions

The nitrogenase complex consists of two metalloproteins:
- **Dinitrogenase (MoFe protein, α₂β₂)** — contains the **FeMo-cofactor** (the active site of N₂ binding/reduction)
- **Dinitrogenase reductase (Fe protein, γ₂)** — couples ATP hydrolysis to electron transfer

**Overall reductive half-reaction:**

$$N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- \rightarrow 2NH_3 + H_2$$

**ATP coupling (one ATP per electron transferred):**

$$8\,ATP \rightarrow 8\,ADP + 8\,P_i \quad \text{(but actually 2 ATP per e⁻, hence 16 total)}$$

#### Step 2: Combine to Get the Full Stoichiometry

$$N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- + 16\,ATP \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrogenase}} 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16\,ADP + 16\,P_i$$

#### Step 3: Calculate ATP Cost Per Nitrogen Atom

$$\frac{16\,ATP}{2\,NH_3} = 8\,ATP \text{ per NH}_3 \text{ molecule}$$

$$\frac{16\,ATP}{28\,g\,N_2} = 0.571\,ATP \text{ per gram N}_2 \text{ fixed}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world biological cost:** In *Rhizobium* bacteroids, the legume plant supplies **~6–12% of its net photosynthate** purely to power N₂ fixation. This is the "tax" the plant pays for free nitrogen fertilizer.

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Growth Rate from Optical Density Data

A fermentation broth gives the following OD₆₀₀ readings at 28°C for *Azotobacter chroococcum*:

| Time (h) | OD₆₀₀ | Time (h) | OD₆₀₀ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.10 | 12 | 0.85 |
| 4 | 0.15 | 16 | 1.55 |
| 8 | 0.35 | 20 | 2.80 |

**Find:** Specific growth rate (μ) during exponential phase (4–12 h).

#### Step 1: Identify Exponential Phase

Cell density grows exponentially between **t = 4 h and t = 12 h** (OD increases ~5.7×).

#### Step 2: Apply the Exponential Growth Equation

$$N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$$

Take natural logarithm:

$$\ln N_t = \ln N_0 + \mu t$$

#### Step 3: Substitute Values (N ∝ OD)

At t = 4 h: $\ln(0.15) = -1.897$
At t = 12 h: $\ln(0.85) = -0.163$

#### Step 4: Solve for μ

$$\mu = \frac{\ln N_t - \ln N_0}{t - t_0} = \frac{-0.163 - (-1.897)}{12 - 4} = \frac{1.734}{8}$$

$$\mu = 0.217\,h^{-1}$$

#### Step 5: Calculate Doubling Time

$$t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.217} = 3.19\,hours$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **doubling time** with **generation time**. For binary fission bacteria like *Azotobacter*, they are identical. For budding yeasts, they differ by a factor of $\ln 2$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Biofertilizer Cell Density from Plate Count

A serial dilution of *Rhizobium* culture is plated. At the **10⁻⁷ dilution**, **0.1 mL** is spread on YEM agar, yielding **152 colonies** after 5 days of incubation.

**Find:** CFU/mL of original culture.

#### Step 1: Apply the CFU Formula

$$CFU/mL = \frac{\text{Colonies} \times \text{Dilution Factor}}{V_{plated}\,(mL)}$$

#### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$CFU/mL = \frac{152 \times 10^7}{0.1\,mL} = 152 \times 10^8 = 1.52 \times 10^{10}\,CFU/mL$$

#### Step 3: Check Against Carrier Standard

For a biofertilizer to be commercially viable, final carrier product must have **≥ 10⁸ cells/g**. If we mix this broth (10¹⁰ CFU/mL) with sterile peat at a 1:9 (v/w) ratio:

$$\text{Final count} = \frac{1.52 \times 10^{10}\,CFU}{10\,g\,peat} = 1.52 \times 10^9\,cells/g$$

**This exceeds the 10⁸ cells/g standard → product is QUALITY APPROVED.** ✓

### 3.4 Derivation: Bt Fermentation Yield

A 50,000 L fermenter runs a Bt-kurstaki fermentation for 72 h. Final biomass = 8.5 g/L dry weight; spore-crystal complex = 65% of total biomass. Glucose consumed = 18 g/L.

**Find:** (a) Total spore-crystal yield in kg; (b) Y_(x/s).

#### Step 4(a): Total Spore-Crystal Yield

$$\text{Spore-crystal} = 8.5\,g/L \times 0.65 = 5.525\,g/L$$

$$\text{Total} = 5.525\,g/L \times 50{,}000\,L = 276{,}250\,g = 276.25\,kg$$

#### Step 4(b): Biomass Yield Coefficient

$$Y_{x/s} = \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S} = \frac{8.5\,g/L}{18\,g/L} = 0.472\,g\,biomass/g\,glucose$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark:** Typical Y_(x/s) for Bt on glucose is **0.40–0.50** — our value of 0.472 falls within range, confirming healthy fermentation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Strain Selection Criteria for KTU Exam

| Parameter | *Rhizobium* | *Azotobacter* | *B. thuringiensis* |
|---|---|---|---|
| N₂ Fixation | Yes (symbiotic) | Yes (free-living) | No |
| Gram Reaction | Negative | Negative | **Positive** |
| Oxygen Requirement | Microaerophilic (in nodule) | Strictly aerobic | Facultative anaerobe |
| Spore Formation | No | Forms cysts | **Endospores** |
| Key Enzyme | Nitrogenase | Nitrogenase | Cry toxin (protoxin) |
| Industrial Medium | YEM broth | Ashby's medium | Glucose-yeast extract |
| Optimum Temp (°C) | 28–30 | 30 | 30 |
| Optimum pH | 6.5–7.0 | 7.0–7.5 | 7.0 |
| Cell Yield (CFU/mL) | 10⁹–10¹⁰ | 10⁸–10⁹ | 10⁹–10¹⁰ |
| Carrier Standard (cells/g) | ≥ 10⁸ | ≥ 10⁸ | ≥ 10⁹ (spores) |
| Shelf Life (months) | 6 | 6 | 12–24 |
| Toxicity to Humans | None | None | **None** (GRAS) |
| Major Limitation | Host-specific | Soil C-dependent | UV-degraded in field |

**Reading Guide for KTU Examiner:**

**Why Each Step Matters in KTU Answers:**

- Step 7–9 explain **lethality** (pore formation is the kill mechanism)
