# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
List any **six characteristics of fermentation industry effluents** that distinguish them from domestic sewage.

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **High BOD** (often 1,000 – 30,000 mg/L) compared to ~200–300 mg/L for domestic sewage.
2. **Variable pH** (3.0–9.0) due to acid/alkali used in product recovery.
3. Contains **residual antibiotics, mycelial biomass, and solvents** — recalcitrant compounds absent in sewage.
4. **High COD/BOD ratio** (>2) indicating non-biodegradable organics.
5. Strong **color, odor, and foam** from fermentation byproducts.
6. **High salinity** from nutrient salts (ammonium sulfate, phosphates) added to fermentation medium.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the **significance of F/M ratio** in the activated sludge process? What is its typical value?

**Model Answer:**
- The **Food-to-Microorganism (F/M) ratio** is defined as:
  $$F/M = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$$
- It represents the **mass of substrate (BOD) supplied per unit mass of microorganisms per day**.
- **Significance:**
  - **High F/M (>0.5 d⁻¹):** Insufficient biomass; poor BOD removal; sludge pin-head flocs.
  - **Low F/M (<0.2 d⁻¹):** Endogenous respiration; bulking sludge risk.
  - **Optimal F/M (0.2–0.5 d⁻¹):** Stable operation with good floc formation and high BOD removal.
- Typical value: **0.2 to 0.5 d⁻¹** for conventional activated sludge.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **three stages of effluent treatment** (primary, secondary, and tertiary) for a fermentation industry. For each stage, list at least **two unit processes** with their functions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An antibiotic industry discharges an effluent with:
- Flow rate $Q = 1000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$
- $BOD_{in} = 5000 \, \text{mg/L}$
- $BOD_{out}$ (after secondary treatment) = 200 mg/L

Calculate the **(i)** BOD removal efficiency, **(ii)** mass of BOD removed per day, and **(iii)** whether the effluent meets CPCB discharge norm (BOD < 30 mg/L).

**Model Answer for (a):**

The three-stage effluent treatment framework for fermentation industries is structured to progressively remove pollutants of decreasing particle size and increasing recalcitrance:

**Stage 1 — Primary Treatment (Physical/Chemical):**
- **Function:** Remove settleable, floating, and colloidal solids; neutralize pH; equalize flow.
- **Unit Process 1: Screening and Grit Removal** — Bar screens remove floating debris; grit chambers remove sand and inorganic particles via gravity.
- **Unit Process 2: Flow Equalization** — Damping of flow, BOD, and pH fluctuations; HRT = 6–24 h.
- **Unit Process 3: Neutralization** — pH correction to 6.5–7.5 using lime, NaOH, or H₂SO₄.
- **Unit Process 4: Primary Clarifier** — Sedimentation of settleable solids; OFR = 24–32 m³/(m²·d).
- **Removal Efficiency:** 50–70% TSS; 20–35% BOD.

**Stage 2 — Secondary Treatment (Biological):**
- **Function:** Biodegradation of dissolved organics using microorganisms.
- **Unit Process 1: Activated Sludge Process (Aerobic)** — Aeration tank + clarifier with sludge recycle; F/M 0.2–0.5 d⁻¹.
- **Unit Process 2: UASB (Anaerobic)** — High-rate anaerobic reactor; OLR 10–20 kg COD/(m³·d); biogas recovery.
- **Unit Process 3: Trickling Filter** — Biofilm on rock/plastic media; organic loading 0.1–0.3 kg BOD/(m³·d).
- **Removal Efficiency:** 80–95% BOD; 70–85% COD.

**Stage 3 — Tertiary Treatment (Polishing/Disinfection):**
- **Function:** Removal of residual SS, color, pathogens, refractory organics, and nutrients to reuse quality.
- **Unit Process 1: Sand Filtration** — Removes residual suspended solids; 0.45–0.6 mm sand.
- **Unit Process 2: Activated Carbon Adsorption (GAC)** — Removes color, odor, refractory organics; modeled by Freundlich isotherm.
- **Unit Process 3: Disinfection** — Chlorination (CT 1.5–6 mg·min/L), Ozonation (5–15 mg/L), or UV (30–40 mJ/cm²).
- **Unit Process 4: Reverse Osmosis / Nutrient Removal** — Final polishing for reuse; N & P removal via nitrification-denitrification.
- **Removal Efficiency:** Effluent BOD/COD < 10 mg/L; pathogen-free.

**Model Answer for (b):**

**(i) BOD removal efficiency:**
$$\eta_{BOD} = \frac{5000 - 200}{5000} \times 100\% = \frac{4800}{5000} \times 100\% = 96\%$$

**(ii) Mass of BOD removed per day:**
$$m_{BOD} = Q \times (BOD_{in} - BOD_{out}) \times 10^{-3}$$
$$m_{BOD} = 1000 \times 4800 \times 10^{-3} = 4800 \, \text{kg/d} = 4.8 \, \text{tonnes/d}$$

**(iii) Regulatory compliance:**
- CPCB norm for inland surface water discharge: **BOD₅ ≤ 30 mg/L**.
- Treated BOD₅ = 200 mg/L.
- Since **200 > 30**, the effluent **does NOT comply** with the discharge standard.
- **Recommendation:** Install tertiary treatment — activated carbon adsorption + chlorination/UV — to further reduce BOD to < 30 mg/L and ensure pathogen-free discharge.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **aerobic (Activated Sludge Process)** and **anaerobic (UASB)** treatment of high-strength fermentation effluents. Discuss their suitability, design parameters, and biogas recovery potential.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A UASB reactor receives an antibiotic industry effluent with:
- $Q = 600 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$
- $COD_{in} = 15{,}000 \, \text{mg/L}$
- $COD_{out} = 3000 \, \text{mg/L}$

Compute the **(i)** COD removal efficiency, **(ii)** mass of COD removed per day, and **(iii)** daily methane volume produced at 0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD removed. Comment on the **energy recovery potential** of this biogas.

**Model Answer for (a):**

| Feature | Activated Sludge (Aerobic) | UASB (Anaerobic) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Aerobic oxidation by suspended biomass | Anaerobic digestion by granular sludge blanket |
| Energy demand | High (1–2 kWh/m³ effluent) | Very low (0.05–0.1 kWh/m³) |
| Sludge yield | High (0.3–0.5 kg VSS/kg COD) | Low (0.05–0.15 kg VSS/kg COD) |
| Biogas recovery | None | Yes (60–70% CH₄) |
| Suitable effluent strength | Low–medium (BOD < 1000) | High (BOD 1000–30,000+) |
| OLR | 0.3–0.7 kg BOD/(m³·d) | 10–20 kg COD/(m³·d) |
| F/M ratio | 0.2–0.5 d⁻¹ | Not typically used |
| HRT | 4–8 h | 4–12 h |
| Effluent quality | Excellent; low BOD | Good, but needs post-treatment |
| Capital cost | Moderate | High initial, low O&M |
| Footprint | Large | Compact |

**Conclusion:** For **high-strength fermentation effluents (BOD > 1000 mg/L)**, the **UASB reactor is preferred** due to low energy, low sludge, and biogas recovery. For **low-strength polishing**, ASP is preferred.

**Model Answer for (b):**

**(i) COD removal efficiency:**
$$\eta_{COD} = \frac{15000 - 3000}{15000} \times 100\% = \frac{12000}{15000} \times 100\% = 80\%$$

**(ii) Mass of COD removed per day:**
$$m_{COD} = Q \times (COD_{in} - COD_{out}) \times 10^{-3}$$
$$m_{COD} = 600 \times 12000 \times 10^{-3} = 7200 \, \text{kg COD/d}$$

**(iii) Methane volume produced:**
$$V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times m_{COD} = 0.35 \times 7200 = 2520 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$$

**Energy recovery potential:**
- Calorific value of methane: ~35.8 MJ/m³.
- Daily energy: $2520 \times 35.8 = 90{,}216$ MJ/d ≈ **25,060 kWh/d**.
- This energy can be **converted via a Combined Heat and Power (CHP) generator** to power the plant's aeration system, pumps, and lighting — recovering up to **40–60%** of the effluent treatment plant's energy cost, thereby supporting **circular bioeconomy principles** and **net-zero targets**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes** (mg/L × m³/d to kg/d) cause **2-mark deductions** — always multiply by **10⁻³** and show the step explicitly.
> 2. **Failing to mention regulatory limits** (CPCB BOD < 30 mg/L, COD < 250 mg/L) when discussing effluent disposal is a common **1–2 mark loss**.
> 3. **Confusing aerobic vs. anaerobic conditions** — students often wrongly state that activated sludge is anaerobic. State clearly: ASP is **aerobic (requires O₂)**, UASB is **anaerobic (no O₂, produces CH₄)**.
> 4. **Forgetting to draw and label the diagram** in long-answer questions — full **3–4 marks** are reserved for an accurate, well-labeled schematic.
> 5. **Misapplying the F/M formula** — the denominator is $V \times X$ (not $V$ alone). This mistake loses **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Rapid-Revision — KTU Board Exam Focus:**

- ☐ **Three Stages:** Primary (physical/chemical) → Secondary (biological) → Tertiary (polishing).
- ☐ **F/M = 0.2–0.5 d⁻¹**, **HRT = 4–8 h** for conventional ASP.
- ☐ **OLR_UASB = 10–20 kg COD/(m³·d)**; **OLR_ASP = 0.3–0.7 kg BOD/(m³·d)**.
- ☐ **SVI = 50–150 mL/g** indicates good-settling sludge; >150 = bulking.
- ☐ **Biogas yield ≈ 0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD removed** in UASB.
- ☐ **CT = C × t** governs chlorination; **1.5–6 mg·min/L** for coliform inactivation.
- ☐ **Freundlich isotherm:** $q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ for activated carbon adsorption.
- ☐ **RO rejection R = (1 − C_p/C_f) × 100%**; typically 95–99% for monovalent ions.
- ☐ **CPCB Discharge Norms:** BOD < 30 mg/L; COD < 250 mg/L; TSS < 100 mg/L (inland surface water).
- ☐ **Anaerobic = low energy + biogas**; **Aerobic = high energy + excellent effluent quality**.
- ☐ **Nitrification:** $NH_4^+ + 2O_2 \rightarrow NO_3^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$.
- ☐ **Sludge disposal options:** Landfill, land application, incineration, biogas (CHP), construction material.
- ☐ **Master Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$
  - $\theta = V/Q$ (HRT)
  - $F/M = \frac{Q S_0}{V X}$
  - $SVI = \frac{V_{30} \times 1000}{X_{TSS}}$
  - $V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times Q \times \eta \times COD_{in}$ (with proper unit conversion)
  - $CT = C \cdot t$
- ☐ **Industry Examples:** Antibiotics, ethanol, citric acid, enzymes, amino acids (lysine, glutamate), vitamin B12.
- ☐ **Circular Economy Insight:** Biogas recovery from UASB offsets plant energy demand; treated water can be **reused in cooling towers, boilers, and irrigation** — a high-yield point for **5-mark essay questions** on sustainable effluent management.
